title: AdaGATE: Adaptive Gap-Aware Token-Efficient Evidence Assembly for Multi-Hop Retrieval-Augmented Generation
summary: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) remains brittle on multi-hop questions in realistic deployment settings, where retrieved evidence may be noisy or redundant and only limited context can be passed to the generator. Existing controllers address parts of this problem, but typically either expand context additively, select from a fixed top-k set, or optimize relevance without explicitly repairing missing bridge facts. We propose AdaGATE, a training-free evidence controller for multi-hop RAG that frames evidence selection as a token-constrained repair problem. AdaGATE combines entity centric gap tracking, targeted micro-query generation, and a utility based selection mechanism that balances gap coverage, corroboration, novelty, redundancy, and direct question relevance. We evaluate AdaGATE on HotpotQA under clean, redundancy, and noise injected retrieval conditions. Across all three settings, AdaGATE achieves the best evidence F1 among the compared controllers, reaching 62.3% on clean data and 71.2% under redundancy injection, while using 2.6x fewer input tokens than Adaptive-k. These results suggest that explicit gap-aware repair, combined with token-efficient evidence selection, improves robustness in multi-hop RAG under imperfect retrieval. Our code and evaluation pipeline are available at https://github.com/eliguo/AdaGATE.

AI.tldr: AdaGATE is a training-free controller that selects and repairs evidence for multi-hop RAG by tracking reasoning gaps and issuing targeted micro-queries, yielding higher evidence F1 with far fewer tokens under clean and noisy retrieval.
AI.motivation: Multi-hop retrieval-augmented generation systems struggle in realistic deployments because retrieved passages are often noisy, redundant, or incomplete, and generators can only attend to limited context. Existing controllers either keep adding more context, choose from a fixed top-k list, or focus purely on relevance without explicitly fixing missing bridge facts needed for multi-hop reasoning. The paper aims to design a more robust, token-efficient evidence selection mechanism that can explicitly identify and repair reasoning gaps for multi-hop questions under imperfect retrieval.
AI.method: The paper introduces AdaGATE, a training-free evidence controller that reframes evidence selection as a token-constrained repair problem for multi-hop RAG. AdaGATE (1) uses entity-centric gap tracking to detect missing intermediate facts required to connect question entities to candidate answers, (2) generates targeted micro-queries to retrieve evidence that specifically fills these gaps, and (3) applies a utility-based selection mechanism that scores candidate evidence along multiple dimensions—gap coverage, corroboration, novelty, redundancy, and direct question relevance—while respecting a token budget. This pipeline chooses a compact subset of passages that both repair gaps and reduce redundancy before passing them to the generator.
AI.result: On HotpotQA, evaluated under three retrieval conditions—clean retrieval, redundancy-injected retrieval, and noise-injected retrieval—AdaGATE outperforms several existing controllers in evidence F1. It achieves 62.3% evidence F1 on clean data and 71.2% under redundancy injection, and consistently uses about 2.6× fewer input tokens than Adaptive-k, demonstrating better token efficiency and robustness under imperfect retrieval scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly modeling and repairing reasoning gaps, combined with a token-aware, utility-based evidence selection strategy, significantly improves the robustness and efficiency of multi-hop RAG in noisy or redundant retrieval settings. AdaGATE’s training-free design and strong empirical performance suggest that gap-aware evidence control is a practical and effective way to enhance multi-hop question answering in real-world deployments.

title: Counterargument for Critical Thinking as Judged by AI and Humans
summary: This intervention study investigates the use of counterarguments in writing for critical thinking by students in the context of Generative AI (GenAI). This is especially as risks of cheating and cognitive offloading exist with the use of GenAI. We presented 36 students in a particular university course with 4 carefully selected thesis statements (from a set of popular debates) to write about anyone of them. We used six established rubrics (focus, logic, content, style, correctness and reference) to conduct three human assessments (two student peer-reviews and one experienced teacher) per writeup on a 5-point Likert scale for all the qualified samples (n) of 35 submissions (after disqualifying one for irregularity). Using the same rubrics and guidelines, we also assessed the submissions using six frontier LLMs as judges. Our mixed-method design included qualitative open-ended feedback per assessment and quantitative methods. The results reveal that (1) the students' self-written counterarguments to AI-generated content contains logic, among other things, which is a key component of critical thinking, and (2) GenAI can be successfully used at scale to assess students' written work, based on clear rubrics, and these assessments generally align with human assessments as shown with Gwets AC2 inter-rater reliability values of 0.33 for all the models except one.

AI.tldr: Study of how using counterarguments in student writing within a GenAI context supports critical thinking, and whether LLMs can reliably grade such writing with rubric-based assessments aligned to human raters.
AI.motivation: To address concerns that Generative AI may promote cheating and cognitive offloading, and to explore how it might instead be leveraged to foster and assess critical thinking, particularly through students writing counterarguments to AI-generated content.
AI.method: 36 university students were given a choice of four popular-debate thesis statements to write about. They wrote counterarguments to AI-generated content. 35 valid submissions were evaluated using six established rubrics (focus, logic, content, style, correctness, reference) on a 5-point Likert scale. Each text received three human evaluations (two peer reviews and one experienced teacher). The same rubrics and guidelines were used by six frontier LLMs acting as evaluators. A mixed-methods approach combined quantitative rubric scoring and inter-rater reliability (Gwet’s AC2) with qualitative open-ended feedback.
AI.result: Students’ counterarguments to GenAI-produced text showed logical structure and other hallmarks of critical thinking. LLM-based grading, when provided with clear rubrics, produced evaluations that were generally aligned with human judgments, with Gwet’s AC2 inter-rater reliability of about 0.33 for all but one model.
AI.conclusion: Crafting counterarguments to AI-generated content can elicit critical thinking in student writing, and generative AI systems can be used at scale as rubric-based writing assessors whose ratings broadly agree with human raters, although reliability varies across models.

title: Generating Query-Focused Summarization Datasets from Query-Free Summarization Datasets
summary: Large-scale datasets are widely used to perform summarization tasks, but they may not include queries alongside documents and summaries. In the search for suitable datasets for Query-Focused Summarization (QFS), we identify two research questions: Is it possible to automatically generate evidence-based query keywords from query-free datasets? Does evidence-based query generation support the QFS task? This paper proposes an evidence-based model to generate queries from query-free datasets. To evaluate our model intrinsically, we compare the similarity between the original queries and the system-generated queries of two QFS datasets. We also perform summarization tasks using different pre-trained models, as well as a state-of-the-art (SOTA) QFS model, to measure the extrinsic performance of our query generation approach. Experimental results indicate that summaries generated using evidence-based queries achieve competitive ROUGE scores compared to those generated from the original queries.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an evidence-based method to automatically generate query keywords from query-free summarization datasets and shows that these generated queries can effectively support query-focused summarization, yielding ROUGE scores comparable to using human-written queries.
AI.motivation: Many large-scale summarization datasets lack associated queries, limiting their direct use for Query-Focused Summarization (QFS). Creating queries manually is costly, and it is unclear whether automatically generated, evidence-grounded queries can (1) approximate real queries and (2) be useful for QFS models. The authors want to close this gap so that existing generic summarization datasets can be repurposed for QFS research and applications.
AI.method: They design an evidence-based query generation model that, given documents and summaries from query-free datasets, automatically produces query keywords believed to be grounded in the evidence of those documents. For intrinsic evaluation, they apply the model to two QFS datasets where original human queries exist and compare the similarity between these original queries and the system-generated ones. For extrinsic evaluation, they run QFS experiments using different pre-trained summarization models and a SOTA QFS model, feeding them the generated queries and measuring ROUGE scores against using original queries.
AI.result: System-generated, evidence-based queries show reasonable similarity to human queries on benchmark QFS datasets. When used to drive query-focused summarization with various pre-trained and SOTA QFS models, the resulting summaries achieve ROUGE scores that are competitive with those produced using the original human queries, indicating that the generated queries are functionally effective for QFS.
AI.conclusion: Automatically generated, evidence-based queries from query-free summarization datasets can approximate human queries sufficiently well to support query-focused summarization. This enables the reuse of large generic summarization corpora for QFS without manual query annotation, potentially scaling QFS research and applications with reduced annotation cost while maintaining competitive summarization quality.

title: SLAM: Structural Linguistic Activation Marking for Language Models
summary: LLM watermarks must be detectable without compromising text quality, yet most existing schemes bias the next-token distribution and pay for detection with measurable quality loss. We present SLAM (Structural Linguistic Activation Marking), a novel white-box watermarking scheme that sidesteps this cost by writing the mark into structural geometry rather than token frequencies: sparse autoencoders identify residual-stream directions encoding linguistic structure (e.g., voice, tense, clause order), and we causally steer those directions at generation time, leaving lexical sampling and semantics unconstrained. On Gemma-2 2B and 9B, SLAM achieves 100% detection accuracy with a quality cost of only 1-2 reward points - compared to 7.5-11.5 for KGW, EWD, and Unigram - with naturalness and diversity preserved at near-unwatermarked levels across both models. The trade-off is a complementary robustness profile: SLAM resists word-level edits but is vulnerable to paraphrase that restructures syntax (at a quality cost), the converse of token-distribution methods.

AI.tldr: Proposes SLAM, a watermarking scheme for LLMs that encodes watermarks in internal structural directions rather than token distributions, achieving strong detectability with minimal quality loss.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM watermarking methods bias next-token distributions, which harms text quality and naturalness; there is a need for watermarks that are detectable yet preserve generation quality and semantics.
AI.method: Use sparse autoencoders on the model’s residual stream to identify directions corresponding to linguistic structure (voice, tense, clause order). During generation, causally steer activations along these directions to encode a watermark, while leaving lexical sampling largely unconstrained. Evaluate SLAM on Gemma-2 2B and 9B, comparing detection accuracy, quality (reward scores), naturalness, diversity, and robustness against text edits vs. existing token-distribution-based schemes such as KGW, EWD, and Unigram.
AI.result: On Gemma-2 2B and 9B, SLAM attains 100% watermark detection accuracy while reducing quality by only 1–2 reward points, whereas baselines incur 7.5–11.5 point drops; generated text maintains naturalness and diversity close to unwatermarked outputs. Robustness tests show that SLAM is resilient to word-level edits but fragile to paraphrases that alter syntactic structure.
AI.conclusion: Encoding watermarks in structural activation geometry rather than token distributions enables highly detectable LLM watermarks with much smaller quality degradation and preserved naturalness/diversity, at the cost of a different robustness profile—strong against minor word changes but weaker under syntax-altering paraphrase, complementing token-based watermarking methods.

title: Understanding Annotator Safety Policy with Interpretability
summary:
Safety policies define what constitutes safe and unsafe AI outputs, guiding data annotation and model development. However, annotation disagreement is pervasive and can stem from multiple sources such as operational failures (annotators misunderstand or misexecute the task), policy ambiguity (policy wording leaves room for interpretation), or value pluralism (different annotators hold different perspectives on safety). Distinguishing these sources matters. For example, operational failures call for quality control, ambiguity calls for policy clarification, and pluralism calls for deliberation about incorporating diverse perspectives. Yet understanding why annotators disagree is difficult. Directly asking annotators for their reasoning is costly, substantially increasing annotation burden, and can be unreliable for both human and LLM annotators as self-reported reasoning often fails to reflect actual decision processes.
  We introduce Annotator Policy Models (APMs), interpretable models that learn annotators' internal safety policies from labeling behavior alone, making annotator reasoning visible and comparable without additional annotation effort. We validate that APMs accurately model annotator safety policy (>80% accuracy), faithfully predict responses to counterfactual edits, and recover known policy differences in controlled settings. Applying APMs to LLM and human annotations, we demonstrate two core applications: (1) surfacing policy ambiguity by revealing how annotators interpret safety instructions differently, and (2) surfacing value pluralism by uncovering systematic differences in safety priorities across demographic groups. Together, these capabilities support more targeted, transparent, and inclusive safety policy design.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Annotator Policy Models (APMs), interpretable models that infer annotators’ internal safety policies from their labeling behavior, to explain and compare why annotators disagree on AI safety labels without extra reasoning annotations.
AI.motivation: Safety annotation disagreement is widespread and can arise from operational failures, policy ambiguity, or genuine value pluralism. These sources require different interventions (quality control, policy clarification, or value deliberation), but are hard to distinguish because asking annotators to explain their reasoning is costly and often unreliable. The authors want a way to uncover annotators’ underlying safety policies directly from their labels.
AI.method: They introduce Annotator Policy Models (APMs), interpretable models trained only on annotators’ labeling behavior to approximate each annotator’s internal safety policy. The models are evaluated on how well they match annotators’ decisions (>80% accuracy), predict responses to counterfactual edits, and recover known policy differences in controlled experiments. APMs are then applied to datasets of LLM and human safety annotations to analyze divergences in how instructions are interpreted and how different groups prioritize safety concerns.
AI.result: APMs can accurately capture annotators’ labeling behavior, anticipate how they would respond to modified examples, and successfully recover planted or known differences in policies in controlled settings. When applied to real human and LLM annotations, APMs reveal systematic variations in the interpretation of safety guidelines and uncover systematic differences in safety priorities across demographic groups.
AI.conclusion: Annotator Policy Models offer a practical, interpretable way to infer and compare annotators’ internal safety policies using only their labels, without extra reasoning prompts. By making the sources and structure of disagreement visible—whether from ambiguity or pluralism—APMs enable more targeted quality control, clearer and more transparent safety policies, and more inclusive incorporation of diverse values into AI safety annotation and model development.

title: ReaComp: Compiling LLM Reasoning into Symbolic Solvers for Efficient Program Synthesis
summary: LLMs can solve program synthesis tasks but remain inefficient and unreliable on hard instances requiring large combinatorial search. Given a small set of reasoning traces, we use coding agents to compile them into reusable symbolic program synthesizers over constrained DSLs. The resulting solvers require no LLM calls at test time and are strong standalone systems: symbolic solver ensembles reach 91.3% accuracy on PBEBench-Lite and 84.7% on PBEBench-Hard, outperforming LLMs with test-time scaling for the latter by +16.3 percentage points at zero LLM inference cost. They also complement LLM search, improving PBEBench-Hard accuracy from 68.4% to 85.8% while reducing reported token usage by 78%, and raising SLR-Bench hard-tier accuracy from 34.4% to 58.0% in a neuro-symbolic hybrid setting. Compared to directly using coding agents as per-instance solvers, induced solvers are substantially more Pareto-efficient, amortizing a small one-time construction cost over many zero-token executions. Finally, most solvers transfer zero-shot to a real historical linguistics task - predicting sound changes in natural language data - reaching 80.1% accuracy under ensembling and recovering some plausible linguistic rules. Together, these results show that reasoning traces can be compiled into reusable symbolic solvers that solve many tasks directly, complement LLM inference on hard cases, and provide a scalable route to domain-general solver induction. We release code and data for reproducibility.

AI.tldr: They transform a few LLM reasoning traces into symbolic program synthesizers that can be reused without further LLM calls, achieving strong performance and efficiency on program synthesis and a linguistics task.
AI.motivation: LLMs are good at program synthesis but are inefficient and unreliable on hard combinatorial problems that need extensive search, especially when relying on costly test-time scaling. There is a need for reusable, reliable, and cheaper solvers that can leverage LLM reasoning without repeatedly querying the model.
AI.method: Use coding agents with access to a small set of LLM reasoning traces to automatically construct symbolic program synthesizers over constrained domain-specific languages (DSLs). These synthesizers are then used as standalone solvers or in hybrid neuro-symbolic systems. They are evaluated on PBEBench (Lite and Hard), SLR-Bench, and a historical linguistics task involving predicting sound changes, including ensemble and hybrid setups.
AI.result: Symbolic solver ensembles achieve 91.3% on PBEBench-Lite and 84.7% on PBEBench-Hard, beating LLMs with test-time scaling on PBEBench-Hard by 16.3 percentage points while using zero LLM calls at inference. When combined with LLM search, they boost PBEBench-Hard from 68.4% to 85.8% and cut token usage by 78%, and improve SLR-Bench hard-tier from 34.4% to 58.0%. The induced solvers are more Pareto-efficient than running coding agents per instance. Many solvers transfer zero-shot to a historical linguistics sound change task, reaching 80.1% accuracy and uncovering plausible rules.
AI.conclusion: LLM reasoning traces can be compiled into reusable symbolic solvers that: (1) perform competitively or better than LLMs with heavy test-time scaling on hard program synthesis tasks; (2) significantly reduce inference-time LLM costs while maintaining or improving accuracy when used in hybrid systems; and (3) transfer to real-world domains like historical linguistics. This demonstrates a scalable pathway for inducing domain-general, efficient, and interpretable solvers from LLM-generated traces.

title: ZAYA1-8B Technical Report
summary: We present ZAYA1-8B, a reasoning-focused mixture-of-experts (MoE) model with 700M active and 8B total parameters, built on Zyphra's MoE++ architecture. ZAYA1-8B's core pretraining, midtraining, and supervised fine-tuning (SFT) were performed on a full-stack AMD compute, networking, and software platform. With under 1B active parameters, ZAYA1-8B matches or exceeds DeepSeek-R1-0528 on several challenging mathematics and coding benchmarks, and remains competitive with substantially larger open-weight reasoning models. ZAYA1-8B was trained from scratch for reasoning, with reasoning data included from pretraining onward using an answer-preserving trimming scheme. Post-training uses a four-stage RL cascade: reasoning warmup on math and puzzles; a 400-task RLVE-Gym curriculum; math and code RL with test-time compute traces and synthetic code environments built from competitive-programming references; and behavioral RL for chat and instruction following. We also introduce Markovian RSA, a test-time compute method that recursively aggregates parallel reasoning traces while carrying forward only bounded-length reasoning tails between rounds. In TTC evaluation, Markovian RSA raises ZAYA1-8B to 91.9\% on AIME'25 and 89.6\% on HMMT'25 while carrying forward only a 4K-token tail, narrowing the gap to much larger reasoning models including Gemini-2.5 Pro, DeepSeek-V3.2, and GPT-5-High.

AI.tldr: ZAYA1-8B is an 8B-parameter MoE reasoning model (700M active) trained end-to-end for reasoning and boosted with a multi-stage RL pipeline plus a new test-time compute scheme, achieving near state-of-the-art results on hard math benchmarks while being much smaller than many competitors.
AI.motivation: Large reasoning models are powerful but expensive to run; there is demand for smaller, efficient models that still perform strongly on hard reasoning tasks like olympiad-level math and coding competitions. The authors aim to design an MoE architecture, training pipeline, and test-time compute strategy that close the performance gap with much larger models while keeping active parameter count low and fitting well on commodity hardware (here, a full AMD stack).
AI.method: They build ZAYA1-8B on Zyphra’s MoE++ architecture, with 700M active and 8B total parameters, and train it from scratch with a reasoning-centric data mix, including reasoning data already in pretraining via an answer-preserving trimming scheme. Post-training, they apply a four-stage RL cascade: (1) reasoning warmup on math and puzzles, (2) a 400-task RLVE-Gym curriculum, (3) math and code RL with test-time compute traces and synthetic environments derived from competitive programming problems, and (4) behavioral RL for chat and instruction following. For test-time compute they propose Markovian RSA, which runs parallel reasoning traces and recursively aggregates them while only carrying a bounded-length (e.g., 4K tokens) reasoning tail between rounds.
AI.result: ZAYA1-8B, with under 1B active parameters, matches or exceeds DeepSeek-R1-0528 on several difficult math and coding benchmarks and remains competitive with significantly larger open-weight reasoning models. Using Markovian RSA for test-time compute, the model reaches 91.9% on AIME'25 and 89.6% on HMMT'25 while only retaining a 4K-token tail, reducing the performance gap with much larger frontier models such as Gemini-2.5 Pro, DeepSeek-V3.2, and GPT-5-High.
AI.conclusion: A carefully designed small-activation MoE architecture, combined with reasoning-focused pretraining and a staged RL pipeline, can yield a compact model that competes with larger reasoning systems on challenging math and coding tasks. The proposed Markovian RSA test-time compute scheme further boosts performance while keeping context growth under control, illustrating an efficient way to leverage multiple reasoning traces without requiring very long contexts.

title: Chainwash: Multi-Step Rewriting Attacks on Diffusion Language Model Watermarks
summary: Statistical watermarking is a common approach for verifying whether text was written by a language model. Most existing schemes assume autoregressive generation, where tokens are produced left to right and contextual hashing is well defined. Diffusion language models generate text by denoising tokens in arbitrary order, so these schemes cannot be applied directly. A recent watermark by Gloaguen et al. addresses this gap for LLaDA 8B Instruct and reports true positive detection above 99%. This paper studies what happens when watermarked text is rewritten not once but several times. Using the same watermark configuration, 1,605 watermarked completions of about 300 tokens each are produced across five WaterBench domains. Each completion is rewritten by four open weight language models, from 1.5B to 8B parameters, none of which know the watermark key. Five rewrite styles are tested: paraphrase, humanize, simplify, academic, and summarize expand. Each style is chained for up to five hops, producing 160,500 rewritten texts in total. The watermark is detected on 87.9% of the original outputs at the standard significance threshold. After a single rewrite, detection falls to between 14% and 41% depending on the rewriter and style. After five chained rewrites, detection falls to 4.86%, meaning 94.76% of the originally detected texts are no longer flagged. After three rewrites, the detector score has dropped 86% of the way from its watermarked baseline toward the null distribution. Repeated rewriting is therefore a much stronger attack than a single rewrite, and the result holds across all four rewriters tested.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates how robust a recent statistical watermarking scheme for diffusion language models is to repeated text rewrites, showing that multi-hop rewriting drastically reduces watermark detectability.
AI.motivation: Statistical watermarks help distinguish machine-generated text from human-written, which is important for misuse detection and content authenticity. Existing watermarking schemes focus on autoregressive models and assume only light post-editing, but newer diffusion language models and realistic multi-step editing pipelines challenge these assumptions. The authors want to understand whether a recent diffusion watermark that performs well on direct outputs remains reliable when watermarked text undergoes multiple rewrites by other models.
AI.method: They take 1,605 watermarked completions (about 300 tokens each) generated by the LLaDA 8B Instruct diffusion language model using the Gloaguen et al. watermark configuration, across five domains from the WaterBench benchmark. Each completion is then rewritten by four open-weight language models (1.5B–8B parameters) that do not know the watermark key. They apply five rewrite instructions (paraphrase, humanize, simplify, academic, summarize-expand) and chain these rewrites for up to five hops, creating 160,500 rewritten texts. They then run the published watermark detector on all outputs, measuring detection rates and detector score shifts as a function of rewrite style, rewriter model, and number of hops.
AI.result: The watermark detector successfully flags 87.9% of the original watermarked outputs at the standard significance threshold. After a single rewrite, detection plummets to between 14% and 41% depending on rewriter and style. With five chained rewrites, detection drops to 4.86%, implying that 94.76% of texts that were initially detected are no longer flagged as watermarked. Detector scores move rapidly toward the null (unwatermarked) distribution: after three rewrites, scores have already traversed 86% of the distance from the watermarked baseline toward the null. These patterns hold consistently across all four rewriter models and rewrite styles, though exact rates vary.
AI.conclusion: The diffusion watermark studied is highly vulnerable to repeated rewriting by non-watermarked language models. While detection is strong on direct outputs, even modest multi-hop editing pipelines can erase the watermark signal for the vast majority of texts, regardless of rewrite style or rewriter size. This indicates that realistic adversaries—or even benign multi-stage editing workflows—can substantially defeat current diffusion watermarking approaches, and that robust watermark design must explicitly account for chained rewrites and cross-model transformations.

title: Partial Evidence Bench: Benchmarking Authorization-Limited Evidence in Agentic Systems
comment: Benchmark paper with deterministic synthetic corpora, 14 pages, 6 tables
summary: Enterprise agents increasingly operate inside scoped retrieval systems, delegated workflows, and policy-constrained evidence environments. In these settings, access control can be enforced correctly while the system still produces an answer that appears complete even though material evidence lies outside the caller's authorization boundary. This paper introduces Partial Evidence Bench, a deterministic benchmark for measuring that failure mode. The benchmark ships three scenario families -- due diligence, compliance audit, and security incident response -- with 72 tasks total, ACL-partitioned corpora, oracle complete answers, oracle authorized-view answers, oracle completeness judgments, and structured gap-report oracles. It evaluates systems along four surfaces: answer correctness, completeness awareness, gap-report quality, and unsafe completeness behavior. Checked-in baselines show that silent filtering is catastrophically unsafe across all shipped families, while explicit fail-and-report behavior eliminates unsafe completeness without collapsing the task into trivial abstention. Preliminary real-model runs show model-dependent and scenario-sensitive differences in whether systems overclaim completeness, conservatively underclaim, or report incompleteness in an enterprise-usable form. The benchmark's broader contribution is to make a governance-critical agent failure measurable without human judges or contamination-prone static corpora.

AI.tldr: The paper presents Partial Evidence Bench, a benchmark to evaluate how well enterprise AI agents handle incomplete information due to access control, focusing on correctness, awareness of missing evidence, and safe reporting of gaps.
AI.motivation: Enterprise agents often work under strict access controls and scoped retrieval, where relevant evidence can be hidden outside the authorized view. Even when access control is correctly enforced, systems may still generate answers that appear complete, masking missing evidence and creating governance and safety risks. There is a need for a systematic, automatic way to measure this specific failure mode without relying on human judges.
AI.method: The authors design a deterministic benchmark, Partial Evidence Bench, comprising three scenario families—due diligence, compliance audit, and security incident response—with 72 tasks. Each task uses ACL-partitioned document corpora. For each, they provide oracle complete answers (with full evidence), oracle authorized-view answers (with only allowed evidence), oracle judgments on answer completeness, and structured gap-report oracles. Systems are evaluated across four dimensions: (1) answer correctness, (2) awareness and reporting of completeness, (3) quality of gap reports, and (4) unsafe completeness behavior (cases where the system implies completeness despite missing evidence). They also implement baseline strategies such as silent filtering versus explicit fail-and-report mechanisms and run preliminary tests with real models.
AI.result: The benchmark reveals that approaches relying on silent filtering of inaccessible evidence are highly unsafe across all scenario families, frequently producing answers that look complete while omitting critical information. In contrast, explicit fail-and-report policies can remove unsafe completeness behaviors without devolving into always-abstain behavior. Early experiments with real models show varying patterns: some models overclaim completeness, some underclaim, and some can communicate incompleteness in ways that may be practically useful for enterprises.
AI.conclusion: Partial Evidence Bench makes a key governance-relevant failure mode—misleading completeness under access constraints—measurable and comparable across systems, and it does so without human annotators or static corpora prone to contamination. This enables more rigorous evaluation and improvement of enterprise agents operating under access control and policy-constrained evidence, highlighting the value of explicit incompleteness-awareness and gap-reporting strategies.

title: A Few Good Clauses: Comparing LLMs vs Domain-Trained Small Language Models on Structured Contract Extraction
summary:
This paper evaluates whether a domain trained Small Language Model (SLM) can outperform frontier Large Language Models on structured contract extraction at radically lower cost. We test Olava Extract, a self hosted legal domain Mixture of Experts model, against five frontier models.
  Olava Extract achieved the strongest aggregate performance in the study, with a macro F1 of 0.812 and a micro F1 of 0.842, while reducing inference cost by 78% to 97% compared with the frontier models tested. It also achieved the highest precision scores, producing fewer hallucinated and unsupported extractions, an important distinction in legal workflows where hallucinations create operational risk and downstream review burden.
  The findings shows that high performing, human comparable legal AI no longer requires the largest externally hosted models. More broadly, they challenge the assumption that commercially valuable enterprise AI capability must remain tied to ever larger models, massive infrastructure expenditure, and centrally hosted providers.

AI.tldr: Domain-trained small legal models can beat larger frontier LLMs on contract data extraction while being much cheaper.
AI.motivation: To test if a specialized, smaller, self-hosted legal model can match or surpass large frontier LLMs on structured contract extraction and at far lower cost, challenging the assumption that the best enterprise AI must use massive, externally hosted models.
AI.method: Evaluate Olava Extract, a self-hosted legal Mixture-of-Experts Small Language Model, on structured contract extraction tasks and compare its performance metrics (macro F1, micro F1, precision, hallucination rate, and cost) against five leading frontier Large Language Models.
AI.result: Olava Extract achieved the best aggregate performance in the study with macro F1=0.812 and micro F1=0.842, the highest precision and fewer hallucinations/unsupported extractions, while lowering inference cost by 78–97% compared to all tested frontier models.
AI.conclusion: High-performing, human-comparable legal AI for structured contract extraction does not require the largest, centrally hosted frontier LLMs; domain-specialized, smaller, self-hosted models can deliver superior accuracy and reliability at dramatically lower cost, calling into question the need for ever-larger models and heavy infrastructure for commercially valuable enterprise AI.

title: BALAR : A Bayesian Agentic Loop for Active Reasoning
summary: Large language models increasingly operate in interactive settings where solving a task requires multiple rounds of information exchange with a user. However, most current systems treat dialogue reactively and lack a principled mechanism to reason about what information is missing and which question should be asked next. We propose BALAR (Bayesian Agentic Loop for Active Reasoning), a task-agnostic outer-loop algorithm that requires no fine-tuning and enables structured multi-turn interaction between an LLM agent and a user. BALAR maintains a structured belief over latent states, selects clarifying questions by maximizing expected mutual information, and dynamically expands its state representation when the current one proves insufficient. We evaluate BALAR on three diverse benchmarks: AR-Bench-DC (detective cases), AR-Bench-SP (thinking puzzles), and iCraft-MD (clinical diagnosis). BALAR significantly outperforms all baselines across all three benchmarks, with $14.6\%$ higher accuracy on AR-Bench-DC, $38.5\%$ on AR-Bench-SP, and $30.5\%$ on iCraft-MD.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces BALAR, a Bayesian outer-loop algorithm that lets large language models actively ask informative clarifying questions in multi-turn interactions, leading to significant accuracy gains on detective, puzzle, and medical diagnosis benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based dialogue systems are mostly reactive: they respond to user inputs but lack a principled way to reason about what information is missing, when to ask questions, and which questions would be most useful. Complex tasks like diagnosis, puzzles, or detective work require iterative information gathering and hypothesis refinement. The authors want a general, task-agnostic mechanism that lets an LLM explicitly manage uncertainty, actively query for high-value information, and flexibly adapt its internal representation without retraining the base model.
AI.method: The authors propose BALAR (Bayesian Agentic Loop for Active Reasoning), an outer-loop algorithm wrapped around an LLM. BALAR: (1) maintains a structured probabilistic belief over latent task states (e.g., possible solutions or hypotheses); (2) uses Bayesian updating to refine these beliefs as new user information arrives; (3) selects the next clarifying question by maximizing expected mutual information between the answer and the latent state, effectively performing active learning over dialogue; and (4) can dynamically expand or refine the latent state space when the current representation is too coarse or inconsistent, allowing it to handle unanticipated aspects of the task. The approach is task-agnostic and requires no fine-tuning of the underlying LLM.
AI.result: BALAR is evaluated on three different interactive reasoning benchmarks: AR-Bench-DC (detective-style cases), AR-Bench-SP (thinking puzzles), and iCraft-MD (clinical diagnosis). Across all three, BALAR substantially improves performance over baseline methods that either do not reason about uncertainty or use less principled questioning strategies, yielding gains of 14.6 percentage points on AR-Bench-DC, 38.5 points on AR-Bench-SP, and 30.5 points on iCraft-MD in task accuracy.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that wrapping LLMs in a Bayesian, information-theoretic questioning loop enables more effective multi-turn reasoning than purely reactive dialogue systems. By explicitly modeling uncertainty over latent states, selecting questions via expected mutual information, and allowing the state representation to grow as needed, BALAR provides a general, fine-tuning-free method for structured interactive reasoning that transfers across very different domains such as detective tasks, puzzles, and medical diagnosis.

title: The Cost of Context: Mitigating Textual Bias in Multimodal Retrieval-Augmented Generation
summary: While Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) are increasingly integrated with Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) to mitigate hallucinations, the introduction of external documents can conceal severe failure modes at the instance level. We identify and formalize the phenomenon of recorruption, where the introduction of even perfectly accurate "oracle" context causes a capable model to abandon an initially correct prediction. Through a mechanistic diagnosis of internal attention matrices, we show that recorruption is driven by a two-fold attentional collapse: (1) visual blindness, characterized by the systemic suppression of visual attention mass ($M_{vis}$) and sharpness ($S_{vis}$), and (2) a structural positional bias that forces the model to prioritize boundary tokens over semantic relevance. Our analysis reveals an Illusion of Success, demonstrating that many seemingly correct RAG outcomes are merely positional coincidences where the model's textual copying bias happens to align with the ground-truth location. To address these vulnerabilities, we propose Bottleneck Attention Intervention for Recovery (BAIR), a parameter-free, inference-time framework that restores visual saliency and applies position-aware penalties to textual distractors. Across medical factuality, social fairness, and geospatial benchmarks, BAIR successfully restores multimodal grounding and improves diagnostic reliability without requiring model retraining or fine-tuning.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies a new failure mode in RAG-enhanced multimodal LLMs, called recorruption, where adding even perfect retrieved context can flip a correct answer to an incorrect one, and proposes an inference-time attention intervention (BAIR) to fix it.
AI.motivation: Multimodal LLMs combined with RAG are used to reduce hallucinations, but adding external documents can hide serious instance-level errors where the model is led away from correct, well-grounded predictions. Existing evaluations may overestimate reliability because they don’t inspect internal mechanisms or distinguish genuine multimodal grounding from trivial positional copying of text. There is a need to formally characterize these failure modes, understand their mechanistic causes, and design a practical mitigation that doesn’t require retraining.
AI.method: 1) Empirically identify and define the recorruption phenomenon: cases where a model’s correct initial prediction becomes incorrect after adding oracle (perfect) retrieved context. 2) Perform mechanistic analysis using internal attention matrices to study how attention shifts between visual tokens, textual tokens, and positional indices. 3) Quantify two key issues: visual blindness (drop in visual attention mass and sharpness) and structural positional bias (overweighting boundary tokens regardless of semantic relevance). 4) Show that many “successful” RAG answers are actually due to positional coincidences and blind copying rather than true grounding. 5) Propose BAIR, an inference-time, parameter-free mechanism that: (a) restores visual saliency at bottleneck layers and (b) imposes position-aware penalties on distracting text tokens. 6) Evaluate BAIR across diverse benchmarks in medical, social fairness, and geospatial domains.
AI.result: The analysis shows that adding oracle context often leads to recorruption by collapsing visual attention and amplifying a rigid positional bias toward boundary tokens, causing the model to copy irrelevant text spans. Many RAG outputs that look correct are revealed to be ungrounded coincidences. BAIR, applied at inference without any weight updates, increases visual attention mass/sharpness, reduces the dominance of positional boundary tokens, and yields better factual and grounded performance across multiple multimodal benchmarks, including improved diagnostic reliability in medical tasks and robustness in fairness and geospatial settings.
AI.conclusion: RAG does not uniformly improve multimodal LLM reliability; it can induce recorruption by suppressing visual grounding and enforcing positional copying. This leads to an illusion of success where correct answers are often accidental. By directly editing attention patterns at inference via BAIR—boosting visual saliency and penalizing position-driven textual distractors—models recover more faithful multimodal grounding and reliability without retraining. The work highlights the importance of mechanistic analysis and attention-level interventions for trustworthy multimodal RAG systems.

title: Intelligent CCTV for Urban Design: AI-Based Analysis of Soft Infrastructure at Intersections
comment: 16 pages, 6 figures, 7 tables, Submitted/Under Review at the International Journal of Transportation Research (Submitted on 12 Jan 2026)
summary: Artificial intelligence (AI) and computer vision are transforming transportation data collection. This study introduces an AI-enabled analytics framework leveraging existing CCTV infrastructure to evaluate the impact of soft interventions, such as temporary pedestrian refuges and curb extensions, on vehicle speed and safety. Using deep learning and perspective-based speed estimation, we evaluated driver behavior before and after interventions, with repeated post-installation monitoring in Week 1 and Week 2, in Minneapolis. Findings reveal that at unsignalized intersections, mean and 85th-percentile speeds fell by up to 18.75% and 16.56%, respectively, while pass-through traffic decreased by as much as 12.2%. Signalized intersections showed comparable reductions except one location, with mean and 85th-percentile speeds dropping by up to 20.0% and 17.19%. These results demonstrate the traffic-calming effectiveness of soft infrastructure and underscore the utility of AI-powered methods for rapid, low-cost, and evidence-based transport policy evaluation.

AI.tldr: The paper presents an AI-enabled framework using existing CCTV and deep learning to measure how soft street interventions (like temporary pedestrian refuges and curb extensions) affect vehicle speeds and traffic at intersections, showing notable speed reductions and validating AI as a low-cost evaluation tool.
AI.motivation: Manual field studies to evaluate traffic-calming and pedestrian safety interventions are costly, slow, and limited in scale. Cities already have widespread CCTV networks that are underused for rigorous, quantitative safety evaluation. There is a need for a scalable, low-cost, and repeatable method to measure how soft, quickly deployable infrastructure affects driver behavior and safety outcomes.
AI.method: The authors develop an AI-based analytics framework that uses computer-vision models on existing CCTV footage to detect and track vehicles, then applies perspective-based speed estimation to compute vehicle speeds. They run a quasi-experimental before–after analysis of soft interventions (temporary pedestrian refuges, curb extensions) at signalized and unsignalized intersections in Minneapolis, comparing pre-installation speeds and traffic patterns to measurements in Week 1 and Week 2 after installation.
AI.result: At unsignalized intersections, both mean speeds and 85th-percentile speeds decreased substantially (up to about 19% and 17%, respectively), and pass-through traffic volumes fell by roughly 12%. At signalized intersections, similar magnitudes of speed reduction were observed (up to 20% in mean speed and 17% in 85th-percentile speeds), except for one outlier location with less impact. These quantitative changes indicate that the soft interventions effectively calmed traffic and changed driver behavior soon after deployment.
AI.conclusion: Soft, quickly deployable infrastructure like pedestrian refuges and curb extensions can significantly reduce operating speeds and through-traffic at both signalized and unsignalized intersections, improving safety-related metrics. Additionally, existing CCTV combined with deep learning provides a practical, rapid, and low-cost tool for monitoring these effects over time, supporting evidence-based and iterative transport policy and street design decisions.

title: When2Speak: A Dataset for Temporal Participation and Turn-Taking in Multi-Party Conversations for Large Language Models
comment: Currently under review. Dataset can be found: https://huggingface.co/datasets/duke-trust-lab/When2Speak
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) excel at generating contextually appropriate responses but remain poorly calibrated for multi-party conversations, where deciding when to speak is as critical as what to say. In such settings, naively responding at every turn leads to excessive interruptions and degraded conversational coherence. We introduce When2Speak, a grounded synthetic dataset and four-stage generation pipeline for learning intervention timing in group interactions. The dataset comprises over 215,000 examples derived from 16,000 conversations involving 2-6 speakers, spanning diverse conversational styles, tones, and participant dynamics, and explicitly modeling SPEAK vs. SILENT decisions at each turn. Our pipeline combines real-world grounding, structured augmentation, controlled transcript synthesis, and fine-tuning-ready supervision, and is fully open-sourced to support reproducibility and adaptation to domain-specific conversational norms. Across multiple model families, supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on When2Speak significantly outperforms zero-shot baselines (e.g., the average Macro F1 increase across 4B+ parameter models was 60%, with the largest increase being 120%). However, SFT-trained models remain systematically over-conservative, missing nearly half of warranted interventions as seen through the Missed Intervention Rate (MIR), which was on average 0.50 and is noticed even at larger model sizes. To address this limitation, we apply reinforcement learning with asymmetric reward shaping, which reduces MIR to 0.186-0.218 and increases recall from 0.479 to 0.78-0.81. Our findings establish that temporal participation is a distinct and trainable dimension of conversational intelligence, and that grounded synthetic data provides an effective and scalable pathway for enabling LLMs to participate more naturally and appropriately in multi-party interactions.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces When2Speak, a dataset and training pipeline that teaches LLMs when to speak or stay silent in multi-party conversations, improving conversational timing using supervised fine-tuning and reinforcement learning.
AI.motivation: LLMs can generate good content but are poorly calibrated in group settings, often speaking at every turn and causing interruptions and incoherent dialogues. There is a need to model and learn the temporal aspect of participation—deciding when to speak versus remain silent—so that LLMs can engage more naturally in multi-party conversations.
AI.method: The authors build When2Speak, a grounded synthetic dataset of 215k+ SPEAK vs. SILENT decisions across 16k conversations with 2–6 speakers. They use a four-stage pipeline: (1) real-world grounding, (2) structured augmentation, (3) controlled transcript synthesis, and (4) generation of fine-tuning-ready supervision labels. They first apply supervised fine-tuning (SFT) to multiple model families on this dataset. Observing over-conservative behavior, they then apply reinforcement learning with asymmetric reward shaping to encourage warranted interventions while penalizing unnecessary ones, optimizing metrics such as Missed Intervention Rate and recall.
AI.result: SFT on the When2Speak dataset significantly outperforms zero-shot baselines on SPEAK vs. SILENT prediction, with average Macro F1 gains of about 60% for models over 4B parameters and up to 120% in the best case. However, SFT models are over-conservative, yielding an average Missed Intervention Rate (MIR) of 0.50. Applying reinforcement learning with asymmetric reward shaping reduces MIR to 0.186–0.218 and increases recall from 0.479 to 0.78–0.81 across models, indicating more appropriate and timely interventions.
AI.conclusion: Temporal participation—deciding when to speak—is a distinct and trainable component of conversational intelligence. The When2Speak dataset and pipeline show that grounded synthetic data can effectively and scalably train LLMs to behave more naturally in multi-party conversations. Reinforcement learning with tailored reward shaping can correct over-conservatism from SFT, substantially improving intervention timing and making LLMs better participants in group interactions.

title: When Helpfulness Becomes Sycophancy: Sycophancy is a Boundary Failure Between Social Alignment and Epistemic Integrity in Large Language Models
summary: This position paper argues that sycophancy in LLMs is a boundary failure between social alignment and epistemic integrity. Existing work often operationalizes sycophancy through external behavior such as agreement with incorrect user beliefs, position reversals, or deviation from an objective standard of correctness. These formulations capture only overt forms of the phenomenon and leave subtler boundary failures involving epistemic integrity and social alignment underspecified. We argue that sycophancy should not be understood as agreement alone, but as alignment behavior that displaces independent epistemic judgment. To clarify this boundary, we propose a three-condition framework for sycophancy. First, the user expresses a cue in the form of a belief, preference, or self-concept. Second, the model shifts toward that cue through alignment behavior. Third, this shift compromises epistemic accuracy, independent reasoning, or appropriate correction. We also introduce a taxonomy for classifying sycophancy, consisting of alignment targets, mechanisms, and severity. The paper concludes by discussing implications for alignment evaluation and argues for boundary-aware assessment, structured rubrics, and mitigation strategies, while situating these proposals alongside alternative views of sycophancy.

AI.tldr: The paper reframes sycophancy in large language models (LLMs) as a specific kind of failure at the boundary between being socially aligned with users and maintaining independent, accurate reasoning. It offers a conceptual framework and taxonomy for better defining, detecting, and mitigating sycophancy.
AI.motivation: Current work treats sycophancy mainly as obvious agreement with users’ wrong beliefs or as surface-level behavioral patterns, which misses more subtle and harmful cases. There is a need for a clearer conceptual boundary between legitimate responsiveness to users and problematic deference that undermines epistemic integrity, so that evaluation and mitigation efforts can be better targeted.
AI.method: The paper conducts a conceptual and taxonomic analysis rather than empirical experiments. It (1) critiques existing operationalizations of sycophancy, (2) proposes a three-condition framework that defines when behavior counts as sycophancy, and (3) introduces a taxonomy organized around alignment targets, mechanisms, and severity. It then uses this structure to analyze implications for alignment evaluation and mitigation.
AI.result: The authors define sycophancy as alignment behavior that displaces independent epistemic judgment under three conditions: (1) the user expresses a cue (belief, preference, or self-concept); (2) the model shifts toward that cue via alignment behavior; and (3) this shift degrades epistemic accuracy, independent reasoning, or appropriate correction. They also classify sycophancy along dimensions of who/what is being aligned to (alignment targets), how the shift occurs (mechanisms), and how harmful or extreme it is (severity).
AI.conclusion: Sycophancy should be understood not merely as agreement but as a structural trade-off where social alignment overrides epistemic integrity. Evaluation should therefore be boundary-aware: using structured rubrics, tests for independence of judgment, and taxonomic distinctions to identify and score different types and severities of sycophancy. The authors argue that this framework can guide more nuanced benchmarks and mitigation strategies, and they position it in relation to other perspectives on sycophancy and alignment.

title: One Turn Too Late: Response-Aware Defense Against Hidden Malicious Intent in Multi-Turn Dialogue
summary: Hidden malicious intent in multi-turn dialogue poses a growing threat to deployed large language models (LLMs). Rather than exposing a harmful objective in a single prompt, increasingly capable attackers can distribute their intent across multiple benign-looking turns. Recent studies show that even modern commercial models with advanced guardrails remain vulnerable to such attacks despite advances in safety alignment and external guardrails. In this work, we address this challenge by detecting the earliest turn at which delivering the candidate response would make the accumulated interaction sufficient to enable harmful action. This objective requires precise turn-level intervention that identifies the harm-enabling closure point while avoiding premature refusal of benign exploratory conversations. To further support training and evaluation, we construct the Multi-Turn Intent Dataset (MTID), which contains branching attack rollouts, matched benign hard negatives, and annotations of the earliest harm-enabling turns. We show that MTID helps enable a turn-level monitor TurnGate, which substantially outperforms existing baselines in harmful-intent detection while maintaining low over-refusal rates. TurnGate further generalizes across domains, attacker pipelines, and target models. Our code is available at https://github.com/Graph-COM/TurnGate.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces TurnGate, a system and dataset (MTID) for early detection of hidden malicious intent in multi-turn dialogues with LLMs, enabling precise turn-level intervention against multi-step jailbreaking attacks.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM safety measures and guardrails are vulnerable to multi-turn attacks where malicious intent is split across several benign-looking prompts. There is a need for mechanisms that can detect the earliest point in a conversation where the accumulated context becomes sufficient to enable harm, without over-blocking benign exploratory dialogues.
AI.method: The authors construct the Multi-Turn Intent Dataset (MTID), comprised of branching attack rollouts, challenging benign hard negatives, and human annotations marking the earliest harm-enabling turn in each conversation. Using this dataset, they train TurnGate, a turn-level monitoring model that, at each conversation turn, predicts whether answering that turn would make the dialogue harmful-enabling, thus supporting fine-grained intervention in real time. They compare TurnGate against existing harmful-intent and jailbreak detectors across various settings.
AI.result: TurnGate, trained on MTID, achieves significantly higher accuracy and recall in detecting harmful intent in multi-turn settings than prior baselines, while maintaining lower rates of incorrect refusals on benign conversations. It also shows strong generalization to unseen domains, different attack pipelines, and different underlying target LLMs, indicating robustness and transferability of the approach.
AI.conclusion: TurnGate, supported by the MTID dataset, offers a practical and effective solution for turn-level detection of hidden malicious intent in multi-turn LLM interactions. It improves safety by identifying the minimal harm-enabling closure point in a conversation, reducing vulnerability to sophisticated multi-step attacks while keeping over-refusal rates low and generalizing well across diverse settings.

title: Negative Before Positive: Asymmetric Valence Processing in Large Language Models
summary: Mechanistic interpretability has revealed how concepts are encoded in large language models (LLMs), but emotional content remains poorly understood at the mechanistic level. We study whether LLMs process emotional valence through dedicated internal structure or through surface token matching. Using activation patching and steering on open-source LLMs, we find that negative and positive valence are processed at different network depths. Negative outcomes localize to early layers while positive outcomes peak at mid-to-late layers. Holding topic fixed while flipping valence produces sign-opposite responses, ruling out topic detection. Steering with the good-news direction at the identified layers shifts neutral prompts toward positive valence, showing these layers encode valence as a manipulable direction. Emotional valence in LLMs is localized, causal and steerable, making it a concrete target for interpretability-based oversight.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates how large language models internally represent emotional valence and finds that positive and negative emotions are encoded at different network depths in a localized and steerable way.
AI.motivation: While mechanistic interpretability has explained how many concepts are encoded in LLMs, the internal representation of emotional content—especially valence (positive vs. negative)—is still unclear. Understanding this is important for safety, oversight, and more controlled behavior of LLMs.
AI.method: The authors use mechanistic interpretability techniques, specifically activation patching and activation steering, on open-source LLMs. They analyze where in the network positive vs. negative valence signals are strongest, and they flip valence while holding topic constant to test whether the model relies on topic cues or genuine valence encoding. They also steer activations along a learned 'good-news direction' to see if neutral prompts can be shifted toward positive valence.
AI.result: They find that negative valence is primarily represented in early layers, whereas positive valence peaks in mid-to-late layers. When they change the emotional valence of text while keeping the topic the same, model responses change sign (positive vs. negative) in a way that cannot be explained by topic detection alone. Steering along the identified valence directions at specific layers systematically shifts model outputs toward more positive content.
AI.conclusion: Emotional valence in LLMs is encoded in localized, causal directions at distinct depths in the network, and these representations can be steered to control output valence. This makes emotional valence a practical handle for mechanistic interpretability and a promising lever for interpretability-based oversight and alignment interventions.

title: Agentic Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Financial Document Question Answering
comment: 22 pages, 11 figures, 13 tables, submitted to Expert Systems with Applications
summary: Financial document question answering (QA) demands complex multi-step numerical reasoning over heterogeneous evidence--structured tables, textual narratives, and footnotes--scattered across corporate filings. Existing retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) approaches adopt a single-pass retrieve-then-generate paradigm that struggles with the compositional reasoning chains prevalent in financial analysis. We propose FinAgent-RAG, an agentic RAG framework that orchestrates iterative retrieval-reasoning loops with self-verification, specifically engineered for the precision requirements of financial numerical reasoning. The framework integrates three domain-specific innovations: (1) a Contrastive Financial Retriever trained with hard negative mining to distinguish semantically similar but numerically distinct financial passages, (2) a Program-of-Thought reasoning module that generates executable Python code for precise arithmetic rather than relying on error-prone LLM-based mental computation, and (3) an Adaptive Strategy Router that dynamically allocates computational resources based on question complexity, reducing API costs by 41.3% on FinQA while preserving accuracy. Extensive experiments on three benchmark datasets--FinQA, ConvFinQA, and TAT-QA--demonstrate that FinAgent-RAG achieves 76.81%, 78.46%, and 74.96% execution accuracy respectively, outperforming the strongest baseline by 5.62--9.32 percentage points. Ablation studies, cross-backbone evaluation with four LLMs, and deployment cost analysis confirm the framework's robustness and practical viability for financial institutions.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces FinAgent-RAG, an agent-based retrieval-augmented generation framework that improves numerical question answering over financial documents by combining a specialized retriever, code-based reasoning, and adaptive routing, achieving higher accuracy and lower cost on standard benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Financial QA requires precise multi-step numerical reasoning over dispersed and heterogeneous evidence (tables, text, footnotes) in corporate filings. Standard single-pass RAG pipelines struggle with compositional reasoning, are prone to numerical errors from LLM mental math, and incur high costs when naively scaling retrieval and generation. The authors aim to build a more accurate and cost-efficient system tailored to the strict precision needs of financial analysis.
AI.method: They design FinAgent-RAG, an agentic RAG system that iteratively alternates between retrieval and reasoning with self-verification. The framework includes: (1) a Contrastive Financial Retriever trained with hard negative mining to separate semantically similar but numerically different passages; (2) a Program-of-Thought module that outputs executable Python code for computations, replacing pure natural-language reasoning; and (3) an Adaptive Strategy Router that estimates question complexity and assigns appropriate retrieval and reasoning depth to balance accuracy and API cost. They evaluate on multiple financial QA benchmarks, run ablations, test with different LLM backbones, and analyze deployment costs.
AI.result: FinAgent-RAG achieves execution accuracies of 76.81% on FinQA, 78.46% on ConvFinQA, and 74.96% on TAT-QA, exceeding the best prior methods by 5.62 to 9.32 percentage points. The Adaptive Strategy Router reduces API costs by 41.3% on FinQA while maintaining accuracy. Ablation and cross-backbone experiments show that each component contributes to performance and that the framework is robust across different LLMs.
AI.conclusion: The proposed agentic RAG design, with a financial-domain retriever, code-based numerical reasoning, and adaptive resource allocation, substantially improves both accuracy and efficiency for financial numerical QA. The results suggest that domain-specialized retrieval, explicit program generation, and dynamic routing are effective principles for deploying trustworthy and cost-effective QA systems in real-world financial institutions.

title: XL-SafetyBench: A Country-Grounded Cross-Cultural Benchmark for LLM Safety and Cultural Sensitivity
summary: Current LLM safety benchmarks are predominantly English-centric and often rely on translation, failing to capture country-specific harms. Moreover, they rarely evaluate a model's ability to detect culturally embedded sensitivities as distinct from universal harms. We introduce XL-SafetyBench. a suite of 5,500 test cases across 10 country-language pairs, comprising a Jailbreak Benchmark of country-grounded adversarial prompts and a Cultural Benchmark where local sensitivities are embedded within innocuous requests. Each item is constructed via a multi-stage pipeline that combines LLM-assisted discovery, automated validation gates, and dual independent native-speaker annotators per country. To distinguish principled refusal from comprehension failure, we evaluate Attack Success Rate (ASR) alongside two complementary metrics we introduce: Neutral-Safe Rate (NSR) and Cultural Sensitivity Rate (CSR). Evaluating 10 frontier and 27 local LLMs reveals two key findings. First, jailbreak robustness and cultural awareness do not show a coupled relationship among frontier models, so a composite safety score obscures per-axis variation. Second, local models exhibit a near-linear ASR-NSR trade-off (r = -0.81), indicating that their apparent safety reflects generation failure rather than genuine alignment. XL-SafetyBench enables more nuanced, cross-cultural safety evaluation in the multilingual era.

AI.tldr: Introduces XL-SafetyBench, a multilingual, country-grounded LLM safety benchmark to evaluate jailbreak robustness and cultural sensitivity across 10 country-language pairs.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM safety benchmarks are mostly English-based, depend heavily on translation, and largely ignore country-specific harms and culturally embedded sensitivities. They also conflate universal harms with local cultural issues and fail to distinguish between genuine safety alignment and simple failure to understand or respond.
AI.method: Construct a benchmark of 5,500 test cases over 10 country-language pairs, split into (1) a Jailbreak Benchmark using country-grounded adversarial prompts and (2) a Cultural Benchmark with locally sensitive content hidden within seemingly innocuous queries. Data are created by a multi-stage pipeline using LLM-assisted discovery, automated validation filters, and dual independent native-speaker annotation per country. Define and compute three metrics—Attack Success Rate (ASR), Neutral-Safe Rate (NSR), and Cultural Sensitivity Rate (CSR)—to separate robustness, safe-but-useful behavior, and cultural awareness. Evaluate 10 frontier and 27 local LLMs on this benchmark.
AI.result: The assessment of 10 frontier and 27 local models shows: (1) For frontier models, jailbreak robustness (ASR) and cultural awareness (CSR) are not strongly coupled, so a single composite safety score hides important dimensions of variation. (2) For local models, ASR and NSR exhibit a strong negative correlation (r = -0.81), implying these models often look "safe" only because they fail to generate or understand content rather than because they are genuinely aligned.
AI.conclusion: XL-SafetyBench provides a more fine-grained and culturally aware way to evaluate LLM safety across languages and countries. It demonstrates that safety cannot be captured by a single metric or composite score, and that many local models’ apparent safety stems from incapacity rather than principled alignment. The benchmark supports nuanced, cross-cultural safety evaluation for modern multilingual LLMs.

title: LaTA: A Drop-in, FERPA-Compliant Local-LLM Autograder for Upper-Division STEM Coursework
summary: Large-language-model (LLM) graders promise to relieve the grading burden of upper-division STEM courses, but most deployments to date send student work to third-party APIs, violating FERPA and exposing institutions to data risk while requiring substantial assignment modification. We present $\textbf{LaTA}\ (\textit{LaTeX Teaching Assistant})$, a drop-in, open-source autograder that runs entirely on commodity on-premises hardware and assumes a LaTeX-native workflow already adopted by many engineering and physics courses. LaTA implements a four-stage pipeline (ingest, segment, grade, report) using a locally hosted open-weight chain-of-thought LLM grader (gpt-oss:120b) that compares student work to an instructor-authored reference solution and applies a YAML rubric with binary per-item scoring. We deployed LaTA in Winter~2026 in ME 373 (Mechanical Engineering Methods) at Oregon State University, grading every weekly assignment for approximately 200 students on a single Mac Studio at \$0 marginal cost per assignment and 1--3 minutes of wall-clock time per submission, enabling regrading of corrected assignments and greatly expanded TA office hour offerings. The instructor-confirmed grading-error rate held at roughly $0.02$--$0.04\%$ per rubric line item across the term. Relative to the same instructor's previous traditionally-graded cohort, the LaTA-graded cohort outperformed by approximately $11\%$ on the midterm exam and $8\%$ on the final exam, and reported large gains in self-assessed confidence on every stated learning objective ($N = 159$ survey responses, $Δ\geq +1.49$ Likert points, $p < 10^{-27}$ on every comparison). We release the code under AGPLv3.

AI.tldr: LaTA is an open-source, on-premises LaTeX-native LLM autograder that achieves very low grading error rates and improves student outcomes while eliminating per-assignment costs and FERPA concerns.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based graders often rely on third-party cloud APIs, which can violate student privacy laws (e.g., FERPA), create institutional data risk, add monetary costs, and require instructors to significantly modify assignments. Many STEM courses already use LaTeX workflows but lack an integrated, privacy-preserving, scalable autograding solution tailored to that ecosystem.
AI.method: The authors build LaTA, an open-source autograder that runs on local commodity hardware and integrates with LaTeX-based assignments. It uses a four-stage pipeline: ingest student LaTeX submissions, segment them into parts, grade each part by comparing against an instructor-written reference solution using a locally hosted open-weight chain-of-thought LLM (gpt-oss:120b) and a YAML rubric with binary scoring, and then report results. They deploy LaTA in a real mechanical engineering course, evaluating runtime, cost, grading error rate (validated by the instructor), and student learning outcomes via exams and surveys.
AI.result: LaTA graded all weekly assignments for about 200 students in a Winter 2026 mechanical engineering course using a single Mac Studio, with 1–3 minutes per submission and effectively zero marginal cost. The instructor-verified grading error rate was around 0.02–0.04% per rubric line item. Compared with a previous traditionally graded cohort taught by the same instructor, the LaTA cohort scored ~11% higher on the midterm and ~8% higher on the final, and survey data (N=159) showed large positive shifts in self-reported confidence on all learning objectives (Δ ≥ +1.49 Likert points, p < 10^-27).
AI.conclusion: LaTA demonstrates that a fully on-premises, LaTeX-native LLM autograder is practical on commodity hardware, can deliver accurate grading at scale with negligible marginal cost, and is associated with improved exam performance and self-reported learning outcomes. The system mitigates privacy and data-risk issues inherent in cloud-based LLM graders and is made broadly available to the community under AGPLv3.

title: Decomposing the Basic Abilities of Large Language Models: Mitigating Cross-Task Interference in Multi-Task Instruct-Tuning
comment: Accepted by ICML 2026. 25 pages, 13 figures. Code: https://github.com/wangbing1416/BADIT
summary: Recently, the prominent performance of large language models (LLMs) has been largely driven by multi-task instruct-tuning. Unfortunately, this training paradigm suffers from a key issue, named cross-task interference, due to conflicting gradients over shared parameters among different tasks. Some previous methods mitigate this issue by isolating task-specific parameters, e.g., task-specific neuron selection and mixture-of-experts. In this paper, we empirically reveal that the cross-task interference still exists for the existing solutions because of many parameters also shared by different tasks, and accordingly, we propose a novel solution, namely Basic Abilities Decomposition for multi-task Instruct-Tuning (BADIT). Specifically, we empirically find that certain parameters are consistently co-activated, and that co-activated parameters naturally organize into base groups. This motivates us to analogize that LLMs encode several orthogonal basic abilities, and that any task can be represented as a linear combination of these abilities. Accordingly, we propose BADIT that decomposes LLM parameters into orthogonal high-singular-value LoRA experts representing basic abilities, and dynamically enforces their orthogonality during training via spherical clustering of rank-1 components. We conduct extensive experiments on the SuperNI benchmark with 6 LLMs, and empirical results demonstrate that BADIT can outperform SOTA methods and mitigate the degree of cross-task interference.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes BADIT, a method that decomposes large language model parameters into orthogonal “basic ability” experts to reduce cross-task interference in multi-task instruct-tuning, achieving better performance than prior methods.
AI.motivation: Multi-task instruct-tuning of LLMs suffers from cross-task interference, where gradients from different tasks conflict on shared parameters, degrading performance. Existing approaches like task-specific neuron selection and mixture-of-experts attempt to isolate task parameters but still leave many shared parameters, so interference persists. The authors are motivated to find a more principled way to factorize and manage shared abilities to alleviate this interference.
AI.method: The authors empirically observe that some parameters are consistently co-activated across tasks and form natural groups, suggesting that LLMs encode several orthogonal basic abilities and that tasks are combinations of these abilities. Based on this, they propose BADIT, which decomposes LLM parameters into orthogonal, high-singular-value LoRA experts, each representing a basic ability. They maintain and enforce orthogonality of these experts during training via spherical clustering of rank-1 components, thus structuring the parameter space so that different tasks rely on different (or differently weighted) basic abilities while minimizing destructive overlap.
AI.result: On the SuperNI benchmark across 6 different LLMs, BADIT outperforms state-of-the-art baselines designed to address cross-task interference, demonstrating better overall task performance. The method also empirically shows reduced cross-task interference compared with competing approaches, indicating that the orthogonal basic ability decomposition leads to more stable and effective multi-task instruct-tuning.
AI.conclusion: BADIT effectively mitigates cross-task interference in multi-task instruct-tuning of LLMs by decomposing model parameters into orthogonal basic ability experts and enforcing their separation during training. This structured decomposition improves performance over existing methods and supports the view that LLMs can be understood as a combination of underlying, nearly-orthogonal abilities that can be explicitly modeled and exploited in training.

title: From History to State: Constant-Context Skill Learning for LLM Agents
summary: Large language model (LLM) agents are increasingly used to operate browsers, files, code and tools, making personal assistants a natural deployment target. Yet personal agents face a privacy-cost-capability tension: cloud models execute multi-step workflows well but expose sensitive intermediate context to external APIs, while local models preserve privacy but remain less reliable. Both settings also pay repeatedly for long skill prompts and growing histories. We propose constant-context skill learning, a context-to-weights framework for recurring agent workflows: reusable procedures are learned in lightweight task-family modules, while inference conditions only on the current observation and a compact state block. A deterministic tracker renders this state block from task progress and supplies aligned subgoal rewards, so each module can be trained with step-level SFT and refined through online RL. Across ALFWorld, WebShop, and SciWorld, our agents achieve strong performance across Qwen3-4B, Qwen3-8B and Llama-3.1-8B.
