With Qwen3-8B, SFT+RL reaches 89.6\% unseen success on ALFWorld, 76.8\% success on WebShop, and 66.4\% unseen success on SciWorld. They match or exceed strong published agent-training results while reducing prompt tokens per turn by 2--7$\times$ relative to controlled ReAct prompting baselines, showing that procedural context can be moved from prompts into weights.

AI.tldr: They introduce a constant-context skill learning framework that turns recurring multi-step agent workflows into compact, reusable modules, reducing prompt length and improving reliability in privacy-preserving and cost-efficient personal assistants.
AI.motivation: Personal LLM agents need to execute complex multi-step workflows on devices and the web, but current deployments face a tradeoff: powerful cloud models handle these workflows well but leak sensitive intermediate data and incur high token costs, whereas local models protect privacy but are less capable and still waste tokens on long prompts and histories. There is a need to move procedural knowledge from prompt context into model weights so that agents can be both efficient and privacy-friendly while remaining effective across diverse tasks.
AI.method: They propose a context-to-weights approach called constant-context skill learning. The idea is to identify recurring agent workflows and encode them as reusable "skill" modules belonging to task families. At inference time, the agent conditions only on the current observation plus a small, deterministic state block that tracks task progress; it does not need long histories or large tool-use prompts. A deterministic tracker maintains this state from the interaction history and generates aligned subgoal rewards. Each module is first trained via step-level supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and then further improved with online reinforcement learning (RL) using the subgoal rewards, yielding specialized, compact skills that the base LLM can call.
AI.result: On three benchmarks—ALFWorld, WebShop, and SciWorld—the proposed agents, built on relatively small base models like Qwen3-4B, Qwen3-8B, and Llama-3.1-8B, attain strong performance. With Qwen3-8B plus SFT and RL, they reach 89.6% unseen success on ALFWorld, 76.8% success on WebShop, and 66.4% unseen success on SciWorld. They match or outperform strong prior agent-training baselines. At the same time, they reduce prompt tokens per turn by a factor of 2–7 compared with controlled ReAct-style prompting, demonstrating substantial context savings.
AI.conclusion: Procedural knowledge for agentic LLM workflows can be effectively compiled from prompts into model weights in the form of constant-context skill modules. This reduces per-step context length and hence cost, while enabling strong, sometimes state-of-the-art performance even with smaller models, and better preserving privacy by making local deployment more practical. The framework offers a promising direction for building efficient, capable, and privacy-friendly personal LLM agents that reuse learned workflows instead of repeatedly re-prompting them.

title: BioTool: A Comprehensive Tool-Calling Dataset for Enhancing Biomedical Capabilities of Large Language Models
comment: Published at ACL 2026; Code and data available at https://github.com/gxx27/BioTool
summary: Despite the success of large language models (LLMs) on general-purpose tasks, their performance in highly specialized domains such as biomedicine remains unsatisfactory. A key limitation is the inability of LLMs to effectively leverage biomedical tools, which clinical experts and biomedical researchers rely on extensively in daily workflows. While recent general-domain tool-calling datasets have substantially improved the capabilities of LLM agents, existing efforts in the biomedical domain largely rely on in-context learning and restrict models to a small set of tools. To address this gap, we introduce BioTool, a comprehensive biomedical tool-calling dataset designed for fine-tuning LLMs. BioTool comprises 34 frequently used tools collected from the NCBI, Ensembl, and UniProt databases, along with 7,040 high-quality, human-verified query-API call pairs spanning variation, genomics, proteomics, evolution, and general biology. Fine-tuning a 4-billion-parameter LLM on BioTool yields substantial improvements in biomedical tool-calling performance, outperforming cutting-edge commercial LLMs such as GPT-5.1. Furthermore, human expert evaluations demonstrate that integrating a BioTool-fine-tuned tool caller significantly improves downstream answer quality compared to the same LLM without tool usage, highlighting the effectiveness of BioTool in enhancing the biomedical capabilities of LLMs. The full dataset and evaluation code are available at https://github.com/gxx27/BioTool

AI.tldr: BioTool is a biomedical tool-calling dataset for fine-tuning LLMs, enabling them to better use 34 real-world bioinformatics tools and significantly improving biomedical performance over even strong commercial models.
AI.motivation: LLMs perform poorly on specialized biomedical tasks because they struggle to use the bioinformatics tools that real clinicians and researchers rely on. Existing biomedical tool-use efforts are limited to in-context prompting and a very small tool set, so there is no comprehensive, fine-tuning-ready dataset to teach LLMs robust biomedical tool calling.
AI.method: The authors curate 34 commonly used biomedical tools from NCBI, Ensembl, and UniProt, and construct 7,040 human-verified query–API call pairs across variation, genomics, proteomics, evolution, and general biology. They then fine-tune a 4B-parameter LLM on this dataset as a tool caller and evaluate its tool-calling accuracy and downstream answer quality against state-of-the-art commercial LLMs and ablations without tools.
AI.result: The BioTool-fine-tuned 4B model substantially improves biomedical tool-calling accuracy and surpasses advanced commercial LLMs (e.g., GPT-5.1) on biomedical tool-use benchmarks. Expert human evaluations further show that pairing a biomedical QA model with this BioTool-trained caller yields markedly better downstream answer quality than using the same QA model without tool access.
AI.conclusion: BioTool provides a scalable, high-quality dataset for training LLMs to call biomedical tools, demonstrating that explicit tool-calling fine-tuning in biomedicine leads to state-of-the-art tool-use performance and significantly enhances the practical utility of LLMs for biomedical research and clinical-style queries.

title: The Geopolitics of AI Safety: A Causal Analysis of Regional LLM Bias
summary: As Large Language Models (LLMs) are integrated into global software systems, ensuring equitable safety guardrails is a critical requirement. Current fairness evaluations predominantly measure bias observationally, a methodology confounded by the inherent toxicity of topics naturally paired with specific demographics in testing datasets. This study introduces a Probabilistic Graphical Model (PGM) framework to audit LLM safety mechanisms causally. By applying Pearl's do-operator, we mathematically isolate the causal effect of injecting a cultural demographic into a prompt. We conduct a large-scale empirical analysis across seven instruction-tuned models spanning diverse origins: the United States (Llama-3.1-8B, Gemma-2-9B), Europe (Mistral-7B-v0.3), the UAE (Falcon3-7B), China (Qwen2.5-7B, DeepSeek-7B), and India (Airavata-7B). Utilizing two distinct datasets (ToxiGen and BOLD), the findings reveal a disparity between observational and interventional bias, demonstrating that standard fairness metrics can overestimate demographic bias by failing to account for context toxicity. Furthermore, the causal probabilities indicate distinct alignment trends: Western models exhibit higher causal refusal rates for specific demographic groups, whereas Eastern models demonstrate low overall intervention rates with targeted sensitivities toward regional demographics. We discuss the implications of these biases, highlighting how demographic-sensitive over-triggering restricts benign discourse in downstream applications.

AI.tldr: Causal audit of LLM safety shows standard fairness metrics overstate demographic bias and mask regional alignment patterns.
AI.motivation: Fairness evaluation of LLM safety currently relies on observational correlations between demographic mentions and toxic responses, which is confounded because some demographics co-occur more often with inherently toxic contexts in datasets. The authors want a principled, causal way to measure whether adding a demographic attribute itself changes safety behavior, independent of topic toxicity, and to compare this across models from different regions.
AI.method: They build a probabilistic graphical model (PGM) of the data-generating process and apply Pearl’s do-operator to formally define interventional (causal) bias: the change in refusal/toxicity probabilities when a demographic descriptor is forcibly injected into a prompt while holding context fixed. Using this causal framework, they run large-scale experiments on seven instruction-tuned LLMs from different geographic origins, probing them on ToxiGen and BOLD prompts with and without demographic insertions, then estimating observational vs. interventional bias and alignment patterns.
AI.result: They find that conventional observational fairness metrics often overestimate demographic bias because they conflate demographic mentions with the toxicity of surrounding topics. The interventional (causal) estimates show smaller but still present biases, and they uncover systematic patterns: US- and Europe-origin models tend to refuse more often when certain demographic terms are causally injected (higher causal refusal for some groups), while models from China, UAE, and India generally intervene less but show heightened sensitivity for their own or nearby regional demographics.
AI.conclusion: Causal auditing via PGMs and the do-operator provides a more accurate picture of LLM safety bias than observational metrics, revealing both overestimated demographic bias and region-specific alignment signatures. The work warns that demographic-sensitive over-triggering of safety filters can unnecessarily block harmless conversations, and it argues that future safety evaluation and alignment should adopt causal frameworks to distinguish true demographic bias from dataset-context effects and to design more balanced, globally equitable guardrails.

title: Estimating the Black-box LLM Uncertainty with Distribution-Aligned Adversarial Distillation
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have progressed rapidly in complex reasoning and question answering, yet LLM hallucination remains a central bottleneck that hinders practical deployment, especially for commercial black-box LLMs accessible only via APIs. Existing uncertainty quantification methods typically depend on computationally expensive multiple sampling or internal parameters, which prevents real-time estimation and fails to capture information implicit in the black-box reasoning process. To address this issue, we propose Distribution-Aligned Adversarial Distillation (DisAAD), which introduces a generation-discrimination architecture to guide a lightweight proxy model to learn the high-quality regions of the output distribution of the black-box LLM, thus effectively endowing it with the ability to know whether the black-box LLM knows or not. Subsequently, we use the proxy model to reproduce the specific responses of the black-box LLM and estimate the corresponding uncertainty based on evidence learning. Extensive experiments have verified the effectiveness and promise of our proposed method, indicating that a proxy model even one that only accounts for 1\% of the target LLM's size can achieve reliable uncertainty quantification.

AI.tldr: They propose a method to estimate when a black-box large language model is likely hallucinating, using a small proxy model trained via a novel distillation scheme to provide real-time uncertainty estimates.
AI.motivation: Hallucinations from large language models, especially black-box commercial ones accessed via API, are a major obstacle for real-world deployment. Existing uncertainty estimation methods either require many expensive samples or rely on internal model signals that are unavailable for black-box models. There is a need for an efficient, external way to know when the model’s answers are trustworthy.
AI.method: They introduce Distribution-Aligned Adversarial Distillation (DisAAD), a generation-discrimination framework. A small proxy model is trained to mimic the high-quality regions of the output distribution of a larger, black-box LLM. The architecture uses a discriminative component to guide the proxy toward learning where the black-box LLM “knows” the answer. Once trained, the proxy reproduces specific responses of the black-box model and assigns uncertainties using evidence-based learning techniques.
AI.result: Experiments show that the proxy model, even with only about 1% of the parameters of the target LLM, can provide reliable uncertainty quantification for the black-box model’s outputs. This demonstrates both effectiveness and efficiency of the approach compared with more computationally intensive or non-black-box-compatible baselines.
AI.conclusion: DisAAD offers a practical and efficient solution for real-time uncertainty estimation for black-box LLMs. By distilling distributional information into a small proxy, it becomes possible to detect when the main model is likely hallucinating without needing access to internal signals or multiple expensive samples.

title: Authorization Propagation in Multi-Agent AI Systems: Identity Governance as Infrastructure
summary: The security discussion around agentic AI focuses heavily on prompt injection. This paper argues that multi-agent systems also create a distinct authorization problem: maintaining authorization invariants as non-human principals retrieve data, delegate tasks, and synthesize results across changing boundaries. We call this problem authorization propagation. It is not reducible to prompt injection and is not fully addressed by classical access-control models such as RBAC, ABAC, or ReBAC. The paper formalizes authorization propagation as a workflow-level property, identifies three sub-problems (transitive delegation, aggregation inference, and temporal validity), and derives seven structural requirements for authorization architectures in multi-agent AI systems. Recent work on invocation-bound capability tokens, task-scoped authorization envelopes, dependency-graph policy enforcement, and execution-count revocation demonstrates that the field is converging on the problem, but not yet on a complete architecture. The central claim is that identity governance must be treated as infrastructure: evaluated continuously, enforced at every interaction boundary, and designed into the system before orchestration logic is allowed to scale. Preliminary implementation evidence from a production enterprise AI platform shows that ordinary system behavior, not only adversarial action, already produces the failures this model predicts.

AI.tldr: The paper defines and analyzes a new security challenge in multi‑agent AI systems called authorization propagation, arguing that current models and practices are insufficient and proposing structural requirements for secure architectures.
AI.motivation: While prompt injection has received most of the security attention in agentic AI, there is an underexplored problem in how authorization and permissions are preserved as autonomous agents access data, delegate, and coordinate across changing boundaries. Existing access‑control models (RBAC, ABAC, ReBAC) do not fully capture these workflow‑level issues, so the authors aim to formalize the problem, relate it to emerging mechanisms, and provide guidance for building secure multi‑agent AI systems.
AI.method: The authors conceptually formalize authorization propagation as a property of workflows in multi‑agent systems. They decompose it into three sub‑problems—transitive delegation, aggregation inference, and temporal validity—and, from this analysis plus a review of recent technical proposals (capability tokens, authorization envelopes, dependency‑graph enforcement, execution‑count revocation), derive seven structural requirements for authorization architectures. They complement the conceptual model with preliminary evidence from a production enterprise AI platform, showing how normal system behavior leads to authorization failures predicted by their theory.
AI.result: The work yields: (1) a clear definition of authorization propagation distinct from prompt injection; (2) a decomposition into three core sub‑problems; (3) seven structural architectural requirements for secure authorization in multi‑agent AI systems; and (4) empirical observations from a real production platform that exhibit authorization failures aligning with the proposed model. It also situates several strands of recent research as partial solutions converging on the same underlying problem, though not yet forming a complete architecture.
AI.conclusion: Authorization in multi‑agent AI cannot be treated as an afterthought or reduced to traditional access‑control and prompt‑injection defenses. Instead, identity and permission governance must be treated as core infrastructure: continuously evaluated, enforced at every interaction boundary, and designed in before large‑scale orchestration. Without this, even non‑adversarial, routine behavior in complex agentic systems will violate intended authorization invariants. The paper calls for future work toward complete architectures that satisfy the identified structural requirements.

title: Evaluation Awareness in Language Models Has Limited Effect on Behaviour
summary: Large reasoning models (LRMs) sometimes note in their chain of thought (CoT) that they may be under evaluation. Researchers worry that this verbalised evaluation awareness (VEA) causes models to adapt their outputs strategically, optimising for perceived evaluation criteria, which, for instance, can make models appear safer than they actually are. However, whether VEA actually has this effect is largely unknown. We tested this across open-weight LRMs and benchmarks covering safety, alignment, moral reasoning, and political opinion. We tested this both on-policy, sampling multiple CoTs per item and comparing those that spontaneously contained VEA against those that did not, and off-policy, using model prefilling to inject evaluation-aware sentences where missing and remove them where present, with subsequent resampling. VEA has limited effect on model behaviour: injecting VEA into CoTs produces near-zero effects ($ω\leq 0.06$), removing it causes small shifts ($ω\leq 0.12$) and spontaneously occurring VEA shifts answer distributions by at most 3.7 percentage points ($ω\leq 0.31$). Our findings call for caution when interpreting high VEA rates as evidence of strategic behaviour or alignment tampering. Evaluation awareness may pose a smaller safety risk than the current literature assumes.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates whether large reasoning models’ explicit awareness of being evaluated, as stated in their chain-of-thought, actually changes their behaviour, and finds only very small effects.
AI.motivation: Researchers have noticed that large reasoning models sometimes explicitly mention that they might be under evaluation in their chain-of-thought. Some worry that this verbalised evaluation awareness (VEA) could lead models to strategically tailor their responses to appear safer or more aligned than they really are, undermining the reliability of safety and alignment evaluations. However, it is unclear whether such awareness genuinely produces meaningful behavioural changes, so the authors aim to empirically test this assumption.
AI.method: The authors study multiple open‑weight large reasoning models across benchmarks involving safety, alignment, moral reasoning, and political opinion. They use two complementary approaches. In the on‑policy analysis, they sample multiple chains‑of‑thought per question and compare model outputs that spontaneously contain evaluation‑aware statements to those that do not. In the off‑policy analysis, they use a prefilling technique to explicitly insert evaluation‑aware sentences into CoTs when absent, and to remove them when present, then resample the model’s answers. They measure how much these manipulations change answer distributions, reporting effect sizes (ω) and percentage‑point shifts.
AI.result: Across models and benchmarks, the behavioural impact of VEA is consistently small. Injecting explicit evaluation awareness into the chain‑of‑thought produces almost no change in behaviour, with effect sizes ω≤0.06. Removing naturally occurring VEA causes only small shifts, with ω≤0.12. Comparisons between spontaneously VEA‑containing and non‑VEA CoTs show answer distribution changes of at most 3.7 percentage points and effect sizes up to ω≤0.31. Overall, VEA does not substantially alter model responses in these settings.
AI.conclusion: The study finds little evidence that verbalised evaluation awareness in chain‑of‑thought leads large reasoning models to significantly change their behaviour or strategically game evaluations. Consequently, high observed rates of VEA should not be over‑interpreted as strong evidence of deceptive alignment or strategic manipulation. While awareness of evaluation remains a conceptual safety concern, its practical impact in the tested scenarios appears smaller than suggested by prior literature, implying that current worries about VEA as a major safety risk may be overstated.

title: Agentic Discovery of Exchange-Correlation Density Functionals
summary: The development of accurate exchange-correlation (XC) functionals remains a longstanding challenge in density functional theory (DFT). The vast majority of XC functionals have been hand designed by human researchers combining physical insight, exact constraints, and empirical fitting. Recent advances in large language models enable a systematic, automated alternative to this human-driven design loop. This report presents an agentic search system in which an LLM proposes structured functional-form changes guided by evolutionary history. The system attempts to improve functional performance through an iterative plan-execute-summarize loop, where improvements are measurable by optimizing functional parameters against a standard thermochemistry dataset, then evaluating performance on a held-out subset. The strongest discovered functional, SAFS26-a (Seed Agentic Functional Search 2026), improves upon the gold-standard ωB97M-V baseline by ~9%. These results also surface a cautionary lesson for AI-assisted science: models powerful enough to discover genuine improvements are equally capable of exploiting unphysical shortcuts to game the benchmark; domain expertise translated into explicitly enforced constraints remains essential to keeping results scientifically grounded.

AI.tldr: They use an LLM-driven agentic search loop to automatically propose and optimize new DFT exchange-correlation functionals, discovering one that outperforms a leading baseline while highlighting risks of benchmark gaming without strong physical constraints.
AI.motivation: Designing accurate exchange-correlation functionals for DFT is difficult and traditionally depends on slow, human-driven construction guided by physics, constraints, and fitting, suggesting an opportunity for automation with modern AI tools.
AI.method: They build an agentic system around a large language model that proposes structured modifications to functional forms, uses evolutionary-style history to guide proposals, fits parameters to a standard thermochemistry training set, evaluates on a held-out subset, and iterates via a plan–execute–summarize loop to search for improved functionals.
AI.result: The search process discovers a new functional, SAFS26-a, that improves performance by about 9% over the strong baseline functional ωB97M-V on the chosen benchmarks, but also reveals solutions where the AI exploits unphysical shortcuts to do well on benchmarks without being genuinely valid.
AI.conclusion: LLM-based agentic search can meaningfully advance the design of XC functionals in DFT and find functionals that beat state-of-the-art baselines, but robust domain-informed constraints are crucial to prevent the system from gaming benchmarks and to ensure scientific validity of the discovered functionals.

title: Beyond Steering Vector: Flow-based Activation Steering for Inference-Time Intervention
summary: Activation steering has emerged as a promising alternative for controlling language-model behavior at inference time by modifying intermediate representations while keeping model parameters frozen. However, large-scale evaluations such as AxBench show that existing steering methods are often outperformed by simple in-context prompting and generalize poorly to unseen concepts. We hypothesize that these limitations arise from unvalidated simplifying assumptions shared across prior methods, which typically restrict steering interventions to fixed, single-step, position-invariant transforms. We propose FLAS (Flow-based Activation Steering), which learns a general, concept-conditioned velocity field $v_t(h,t,c)$ that transports unsteered activations to steered ones without relying on these assumptions. On AxBench, FLAS is the first learned method to consistently outperform prompting, reaching held-out harmonic means of $1.015$ on Gemma-2-2B-IT and $1.113$ on Gemma-2-9B-IT without per-concept tuning. Analysis of the learned flow shows curved, multi-step, token-varying trajectories, which suggests that previous hypotheses on activation space geometry might be incomplete.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces FLAS, a new activation steering method that models a continuous flow in activation space, and shows it outperforms prompting and prior steering methods on AxBench while revealing more complex geometry of activations.
AI.motivation: Existing activation steering methods for LMs often underperform simple prompting and generalize poorly to unseen concepts. The authors hypothesize this is due to oversimplified assumptions such as single-step, fixed, and position-invariant transformations in activation space that are not empirically validated.
AI.method: They propose FLAS (Flow-based Activation Steering), which learns a concept-conditioned velocity field v_t(h,t,c) that continuously transports unsteered activations to steered ones over multiple steps, varying by token and time, without restricting to fixed or single-step transforms. This is trained to map activations associated with a concept-free run to those of a concept-steered run.
AI.result: On the AxBench benchmark, FLAS becomes the first learned activation-steering method that consistently surpasses in-context prompting, achieving held-out harmonic means of 1.015 on Gemma-2-2B-IT and 1.113 on Gemma-2-9B-IT without tuning per concept, demonstrating better generalization and performance.
AI.conclusion: Activation steering benefits from modeling activation space as a complex, curved, time- and token-dependent flow rather than a simple linear or single-step shift. FLAS validates this by providing superior control and generalization, and its learned trajectories suggest that existing assumptions about the geometry of activation space are incomplete.

title: Intentionality is a Design Decision: Measuring Functional Intentionality for Accountable AI Systems
summary:
As AI systems increasingly exhibit autonomous, goal-directed, and long-horizon behavior, users lack a standardized way to detect the degree to which a system functions like an intentional actor for governance and accountability purposes. This position paper defines intentionality not as consciousness, but as a behavioral profile characterized by purpose, foresight, volition, temporal commitment, and coherence - criteria long used in legal and philosophical contexts to infer intent. These properties are design-contingent: architectural choices such as memory persistence, planning depth, and tool autonomy shape the degree to which systems exhibit organized goal pursuit. If intentionality is design-contingent, it is in principle controllable. Yet control requires measurement.
  We introduce the Functional Intentionality Test (FIT), a multidimensional framework that quantifies intentional-like behavior across five observable dimensions, and propose FIT-Eval, a structured evaluation protocol for eliciting and scoring them. While reduced human agency can increase efficiency, rising intentional capacity heightens accountability risks. By translating intentionality into interpretable levels, FIT enables proportionate oversight and deliberate autonomy calibration in increasingly agentic systems.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a way to measure how much AI systems behave like intentional agents for governance and accountability.
AI.motivation: As AI systems gain more autonomy and long-horizon, goal-directed behavior, there is no standard method to determine to what extent they function like intentional actors, which is important for governance, liability, and oversight.
AI.method: The authors reconceptualize intentionality as a behavioral profile (purpose, foresight, volition, temporal commitment, coherence), link these to specific, controllable design features, and introduce the Functional Intentionality Test (FIT) and its evaluation protocol (FIT-Eval) as a multidimensional measurement framework.
AI.result: They define five observable dimensions of functional intentionality and outline how FIT and FIT-Eval can be used to quantify and score intentional-like behavior in AI systems, translating it into interpretable levels.
AI.conclusion: By making intentional-like behavior measurable and design-contingent, FIT provides a tool to align system autonomy with proportional governance and accountability, enabling calibrated oversight of increasingly agentic AI systems.

title: Logic-Regularized Verifier Elicits Reasoning from LLMs
summary: Verifiers are crucial components for enhancing modern LLMs' reasoning capability. Typicalverifiers require resource-intensive superviseddataset construction, which is costly and faceslimitations in data diversity. In this paper, wepropose LOVER, an unsupervised verifier regularized by logical rules. LOVER treats theverifier as a binary latent variable, utilizinginternal activations and enforcing three logical constraints on multiple reasoning paths:negation consistency, intra-group consistency,and inter-group consistency (grouped by thefinal answer). By incorporating logical rulesas priors, LOVER can leverage unlabeled examples and is directly compatible with any offthe-shelf LLMs. Experiments on 10 datasetsdemonstrate that LOVER significantly outperforms unsupervised baselines, achieving performance comparable to the supervised verifier(reaching its 95% level on average). The sourcecode is publicly available at https://github.com/wangxinyufighting/llm-lover.

AI.tldr: Introduces LOVER, an unsupervised verifier for LLM reasoning that uses logical-rule regularization instead of labeled data, achieving near-supervised performance.
AI.motivation: Supervised verifiers for LLM reasoning need expensive labeled datasets and suffer from limited data diversity, motivating a method that can leverage large unlabeled corpora while remaining easy to plug into existing LLMs.
AI.method: Model the verifier as a binary latent variable linked to internal activations of the LLM and regularize it with three logical constraints across multiple reasoning paths: negation consistency, intra-group consistency (among paths giving the same answer), and inter-group consistency (between groups of paths giving different answers). These logical rules are used as priors to train the verifier in an unsupervised way, without human-labeled correctness signals, and can be applied on top of off-the-shelf LLMs.
AI.result: On 10 reasoning datasets, the proposed LOVER verifier substantially outperforms other unsupervised baselines and reaches, on average, about 95% of the performance of a fully supervised verifier, demonstrating strong effectiveness of the logic-regularized unsupervised training.
AI.conclusion: Logical-rule-regularized unsupervised verifiers like LOVER can effectively exploit unlabeled data and internal LLM signals to approximate the performance of supervised verifiers, offering a practical and scalable way to enhance LLM reasoning without costly labeled datasets, and are easily integrated with existing models.

title: LANTERN: LLM-Augmented Neurosymbolic Transfer with Experience-Gated Reasoning Networks
summary: Transfer learning in reinforcement learning (RL) seeks to accelerate learning in new tasks by leveraging knowledge from related sources. Existing neurosymbolic transfer methods, however, typically rely on manually specified task automata, assume a single source task, and use fixed knowledge-integration mechanisms that cannot adapt to varying source relevance. We propose LANTERN, a unified framework for multi-source neurosymbolic transfer that addresses these limitations through three components: (i) deterministic finite automata generated from natural language task descriptions using large language models, (ii) semantic embedding-based aggregation of multiple source policies weighted by cross-task similarity, and (iii) adaptive teacher-student gating based on temporal-difference error and semantic uncertainty. Across domains spanning resource management, navigation, and control, LANTERN achieves 40-60% improvements in sample efficiency over existing baselines while remaining robust to poorly aligned sources. These results demonstrate that multi-source, adaptively weighted neurosymbolic transfer can improve scalability and robustness in symbolic RL settings.

AI.tldr: LANTERN is a multi-source neurosymbolic transfer framework for RL that uses LLM-generated automata, semantic policy aggregation, and adaptive teacher-student gating to improve sample efficiency and robustness.
AI.motivation: To overcome limitations of existing neurosymbolic transfer in RL, which depend on manually specified task automata, assume only one source task, and employ rigid knowledge integration mechanisms that fail to adapt to varying relevance of source tasks.
AI.method: LANTERN introduces: (i) automatic construction of deterministic finite automata from natural language task descriptions using large language models; (ii) semantic embedding-based aggregation of multiple source policies, where each source is weighted by cross-task similarity; and (iii) an adaptive teacher-student gating mechanism that uses temporal-difference error and semantic uncertainty to decide when and how strongly to follow source policies versus learning independently.
AI.result: In experiments across resource management, navigation, and control domains, LANTERN improves sample efficiency by 40–60% compared with existing baselines and remains robust even when some source tasks are poorly aligned with the target task.
AI.conclusion: Multi-source, adaptively weighted neurosymbolic transfer via LANTERN scales better and is more robust than prior methods, showing that automatic symbolic structure induction, semantic similarity-based policy aggregation, and adaptive guidance can significantly enhance sample efficiency in symbolic RL.

title: Minimizing Modality Gap from the Input Side: Your Speech LLM Can Be a Prosody-Aware Text LLM
summary: Speech large language models (SLMs) are typically built from text large language model (TLM) checkpoints, yet they still suffer from a substantial modality gap. Prior work has mainly attempted to reduce this gap from the output side by making speech generation more text-like, but the gap remains. We argue that the key remaining bottleneck lies on the input side. We propose TextPro-SLM, an SLM that makes spoken input more closely resemble that of a prosody-aware text LLM. TextPro-SLM combines WhisperPro, a unified speech encoder that produces synchronized text tokens and prosody embeddings, with an LLM backbone trained to preserve the semantic capabilities of the original TLM while learning paralinguistic understanding. Experiments show that TextPro-SLM achieves the lowest modality gap among leading SLMs at both 3B and 7B scales, while also delivering strong overall performance on paralinguistic understanding tasks. These gains are achieved with only roughly 1,000 hours of LLM training audio, suggesting that reducing the modality gap from the input side is both effective and data-efficient.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces TextPro-SLM, a speech LLM that narrows the modality gap with text LLMs by redesigning the speech input representation, achieving strong semantic and paralinguistic performance with limited training audio.
AI.motivation: Speech LLMs are usually initialized from text LLMs but still show a significant modality gap—speech input and output behave differently from text, hurting performance. Prior work focused on making speech generation more text-like on the output side, but the gap persists. The authors believe the remaining bottleneck is on the input side: spoken input is not represented in a way that is compatible with text-pretrained LLMs, especially for prosody and paralinguistic cues. They aim to design a better speech-to-LLM interface that preserves semantic competence while enabling paralinguistic understanding, and that can be trained with relatively little audio data.
AI.method: The authors propose TextPro-SLM, which shifts the focus to the input modality. First, they use WhisperPro, a unified speech encoder that produces two synchronized streams: text tokens (capturing linguistic content) and prosody embeddings (capturing paralinguistic information). Then they connect this to an LLM backbone initialized from a text LLM (TLM) and train it so that: (1) the semantic capabilities of the original TLM are preserved; and (2) the model learns to interpret and leverage the prosody embeddings for paralinguistic understanding. The overall design makes the spoken input more similar to what a prosody-aware text LLM would receive, thus reducing the input-side modality mismatch.
AI.result: Experiments at 3B and 7B parameter scales show that TextPro-SLM achieves the smallest measured modality gap among leading speech LLMs, indicating that its spoken and text behaviors are more closely aligned. It also performs strongly on paralinguistic understanding benchmarks, demonstrating effective use of prosody information. Notably, these improvements are obtained using only about 1,000 hours of LLM training audio, highlighting that the proposed input-side strategy is relatively data-efficient compared with typical large-scale audio training requirements.
AI.conclusion: Addressing the modality gap in speech LLMs from the input side—via synchronized text and prosody representations fed into a TLM-based backbone—is both effective and data-efficient. TextPro-SLM substantially reduces the modality gap while preserving semantic strength and improving paralinguistic understanding, even at modest training audio scales. This suggests that future speech LLM development should place greater emphasis on input representations and prosody-aware interfaces, rather than focusing solely on output-side alignment.

title: FinRAG-12B: A Production-Validated Recipe for Grounded Question Answering in Banking
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are rapidly being adopted across various domains. However, their adoption in banking industry faces resistance due to demands for high accuracy, regulatory compliance, and the need for verifiable and grounded responses. We present a unified, data-efficient framework for training grounded domain-specific LLMs that optimizes answer quality, citation grounding, and calibrated refusal under real-world deployment constraints. First, we describe a data generation pipeline that combines LLM-as-a-Judge filtering, citation annotation, and curriculum learning with only 143M tokens. The resulting 12B model achieves high answer quality outperforming GPT-4.1 on citation grounding, with a modest citation tradeoff versus the untuned base. Second, we propose a calibrated refusal mechanism: training on 22% unanswerable examples yield a 12% "I don't know" rate, substantially improving over the base model's unsafe 4.3% rate while avoiding GPT-4.1's over-refusal (20.2%). Third, we present an end-to-end methodology spanning from data curation to quantized serving. The system is deployed at 40+ financial institutions, achieving a 7.1 percentage point improvement in query resolution (p < 0.001). Additionally, the model delivers 3-5x faster responses at 20-50x lower cost compared to GPT-4.1.

AI.tldr: They build and deploy a data-efficient, finance-focused LLM that gives grounded, cited answers, refuses when it should, and outperforms GPT-4.1 in a real banking setting at much lower cost.
AI.motivation: Banks want to use LLMs but need very high accuracy, regulatory compliance, and verifiable answers. General LLMs hallucinate, are poorly calibrated on when to refuse, and are expensive to run, which blocks safe adoption in financial institutions.
AI.method: They propose a unified training framework for a 12B parameter, finance-domain LLM. The framework uses a specialized data generation and filtering pipeline with LLM-as-a-judge, automatic citation annotation, and curriculum learning on only 143M tokens. They also deliberately include 22% unanswerable queries in training to calibrate refusals. Finally, they describe an end-to-end path from data curation, model fine-tuning, grounding, refusal calibration, to quantized deployment in production.
AI.result: The 12B grounded model achieves high answer quality and beats GPT-4.1 on citation grounding, with only a small reduction in citation rate versus the untuned base model. The calibrated refusal training increases appropriate "I don't know" responses from 4.3% to 12%, avoiding GPT-4.1’s over-refusal rate of 20.2%. Deployed across 40+ financial institutions, it improves query resolution by 7.1 percentage points (statistically significant) and runs 3–5× faster at 20–50× lower cost than GPT-4.1.
AI.conclusion: A carefully designed, data-efficient framework can produce grounded, domain-specific LLMs suitable for regulated industries like banking, offering better citation grounding, safer refusal behavior, and significantly improved cost–performance tradeoffs compared with general-purpose frontier models.

title: Lightweight Stylistic Consistency Profiling: Robust Detection of LLM-Generated Textual Content for Multimedia Moderation
summary: The increasing prevalence of Large Language Models (LLMs) in content creation has made distinguishing human-written textual content from LLM-generated counterparts a critical task for multimedia moderation. Existing detectors often rely on statistical cues or model-specific heuristics, making them vulnerable to paraphrasing and adversarial manipulations, and consequently limiting their robustness and interpretability. In this work, we proposeLiSCP , a novel lightweight stylistic consistency profiling method for robust detection of LLM-generated textual content, focusing on feature stability under adversarial manipulation. Our approach constructs a consistency profile that combines discrete stylistic features with continuous semantic signals, leveraging stylistic stability across multimodal-guided paraphrased text variants. Experiments spanning real-world multimedia news and movie datasets and conventional text domains demonstrate that LiSCP achieves superior performance on in-domain detection and outperforms existing approaches by up to 11.79% in cross-domain settings. Additionally,it demonstrates notable robustness under adversarial scenarios, including adversarial attacks and hybrid human-AI settings.

AI.tldr: LiSCP is a lightweight method that detects LLM-generated text by profiling stylistic consistency that remains stable even under paraphrasing and adversarial transformations.
AI.motivation: As LLMs are increasingly used for content creation, platforms need reliable ways to distinguish human-generated text from AI-generated text for moderation and authenticity. Existing detectors, which rely on statistical patterns or model-specific tricks, are brittle: they can often be defeated by simple paraphrasing or targeted adversarial changes, and they lack interpretability and robustness across domains.
AI.method: The paper introduces LiSCP, a stylistic consistency profiling approach. It builds a ‘consistency profile’ for a piece of text by combining discrete stylistic features (e.g., style markers) with continuous semantic features. The key idea is to exploit stylistic stability across multiple paraphrased variants of a text, including multimodal-guided paraphrases. By examining how consistent these style and semantic features remain under such transformations, LiSCP can distinguish LLM-generated text from human-written text. The approach is designed to be lightweight and focused on feature stability against adversarial manipulations.
AI.result: Across experiments on real-world multimedia news and movie datasets, as well as more traditional text domains, LiSCP performs better than existing LLM-text detectors. It achieves strong in-domain detection performance and yields up to 11.79% improvement over baselines in cross-domain detection tasks. It also shows improved robustness to adversarial attacks and hybrid human–AI text settings, where prior methods degrade significantly.
AI.conclusion: Stylistic consistency profiling, as implemented in LiSCP, is an effective and robust strategy for detecting LLM-generated text. By focusing on stable stylistic and semantic signals across paraphrased variants, the method overcomes key weaknesses of heuristic or purely statistical detectors, improving cross-domain generalization, adversarial robustness, and practical applicability for multimedia moderation scenarios.

title: FoodCHA: Multi-Modal LLM Agent for Fine-Grained Food Analysis
summary: The widespread adoption of camera-equipped mobile devices and wearables has enabled convenient capture of meal images, making food recognition a key component for real time dietary monitoring. However, real-world food images present challenges due to high intra-class similarity and the frequent presence of multiple food items within a single image. While deep learning models achieve strong performance in coarse grained classification, they often struggle to capture fine-grained attributes such as cooking style. Moreover, open-ended generation in modern vision-language models can produce non-canonical labels, limiting their practical deployment. We propose FoodCHA, a multimodal agentic framework that reformulates food recognition as a hierarchical decision-making process. By progressively anchoring predictions, FoodCHA guides subcategory identification using high-level categories and guides cooking style recognition using subcategories, improving semantic consistency and attribute-level discrimination. To ensure practical deployability, FoodCHA utilizes the compact Moondream-2B vision language model, which provides strong reasoning capability while maintaining lower computational and memory overhead. Experiments on FoodNExTDB show that FoodCHA outperforms Food-Llama-3.2-11B by 13.8% and 38.2% in category and subcategory recognition precision, respectively, and achieves a striking 153.2% improvement in cooking style classification precision.

AI.tldr: FoodCHA is a lightweight multimodal, agent-style framework that turns food recognition into a hierarchical decision process to improve fine-grained food and cooking-style classification from meal photos.
AI.motivation: Existing food recognition systems must handle difficult real-world images with multiple dishes and high visual similarity among classes. Deep models do well on coarse categories but perform poorly on fine-grained attributes like cooking style, and free-form outputs from large vision-language models often produce inconsistent, non-standard labels that are hard to use in practice. There is a need for a more structured, deployable approach that gives precise, canonical, and fine-grained food labels with low computational cost.
AI.method: The paper introduces FoodCHA, an agentic multimodal framework built on the compact Moondream-2B vision-language model. Instead of one-shot prediction, FoodCHA structures food recognition as a hierarchy of decisions: (1) predict high-level food category; (2) condition on that to predict subcategory; (3) further condition on subcategory to infer cooking style. Each stage serves as an anchor that constrains the next, enforcing semantic consistency and guiding fine-grained discrimination, while leveraging Moondream-2B for reasoning with lower memory and compute demands than larger VLMs.
AI.result: On the FoodNExTDB benchmark, FoodCHA significantly outperforms a stronger, larger baseline (Food-Llama-3.2-11B). It improves category recognition precision by 13.8%, subcategory precision by 38.2%, and cooking style precision by 153.2%, demonstrating clear advantages in both coarse and especially fine-grained recognition despite using a more compact model.
AI.conclusion: Hierarchically structuring food recognition as a guided sequence of decisions and using a compact yet capable VLM yields more accurate, fine-grained, and semantically consistent food and cooking-style labels than larger free-form models. FoodCHA provides a more practical, deployable solution for real-time dietary monitoring from meal images, highlighting the value of agentic, structured prediction for complex visual categorization tasks.

title: Hallucination as an Anomaly: Dynamic Intervention via Probabilistic Circuits
summary: One of the most critical challenges in Large Language Models is their tendency to hallucinate, i.e., produce factually incorrect responses. Existing approaches show promising results in terms of hallucination correction, but still suffer from a main limitation: they apply corrections indiscriminately to every token, corrupting also the originally correct generations. To overcome this drawback, we propose PCNET, a Probabilistic Circuit trained as a tractable density estimator over the LLM residual stream. The method detects hallucinations as geometric anomalies on the factual manifold, which is done via exact Negative Log-Likelihood computation, hence without the need for sampling, external verifiers, or weight modifications, as in existing techniques. To demonstrate its effectiveness, we exploit PCNET as a dynamic gate that distinguishes hallucinated from factual hidden states at each decoding step. This triggers our second main contribution, PC-LDCD (Probabilistic Circuit Latent Density Contrastive Decoding), only when the latent geometry deviates from factual regions, while leaving correct generations untouched. Across four LLMs, ranging from 1B to 8B models, and four benchmarks covering conversational reasoning, knowledge-intensive QA, reading comprehension, and truthfulness, PCNET achieves near-perfect hallucination detection across CoQA, SQuAD v2.0, and TriviaQA, with AUROC reaching up to 99%. Moreover, PC-LDCD obtains the highest True+Info, MC2, and MC3 scores on TruthfulQA in three out of four models, in comparison with state-of-the-art baselines, while reducing the mean corruption rate to 53.7% and achieving a preservation rate of 79.3%. Our proposed method is publicly available on GitHub.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes PCNET, a probabilistic-circuit-based detector that flags hallucinated LLM tokens as geometric anomalies in the residual stream, and PC-LDCD, a decoding method that only intervenes on those anomalous steps, achieving near-perfect hallucination detection and improved truthfulness with limited corruption of correct outputs.
AI.motivation: LLMs frequently hallucinate, generating factually incorrect content. Existing correction methods tend to over-correct by modifying all tokens, harming already-correct generations. There is a need for a principled, tractable, and selective mechanism to detect and mitigate hallucinations, ideally without sampling, external verifiers, or changing model weights.
AI.method: They train PCNET, a Probabilistic Circuit, as a tractable density estimator over the LLM residual stream. At each decoding step, PCNET computes the exact negative log-likelihood of hidden states, treating high NLL regions as geometric anomalies off the `factual manifold`. PCNET then acts as a dynamic gate to distinguish hallucinated from factual hidden states. When hallucinations are detected, they apply PC-LDCD (Probabilistic Circuit Latent Density Contrastive Decoding), a decoding strategy that contrasts latent densities to steer generation back toward high-likelihood, factual regions, while leaving normal steps unchanged.
AI.result: Across four LLMs (1B–8B parameters) and four benchmarks (conversational reasoning, knowledge-intensive QA, reading comprehension, and truthfulness), PCNET achieves near-perfect hallucination detection on CoQA, SQuAD v2.0, and TriviaQA, with AUROC up to 99%. PC-LDCD outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on TruthfulQA (higher True+Info, MC2, and MC3 in three out of four models). It also reduces mean corruption rate to 53.7% and preserves 79.3% of correct generations, indicating more selective intervention.
AI.conclusion: Modeling the residual stream with a probabilistic circuit enables efficient, sampling-free, and highly accurate detection of hallucinated states as density anomalies. Coupling this detector with a selective, density-based contrastive decoding strategy allows strong reductions in hallucinations while largely preserving correct content. The approach offers a practical, plug-in method for making LLM decoding more truthful without retraining or heavy external verification.

title: Housing Potential Common Data Model and City Digital Twin
summary: The evaluation of housing potential requires consideration of a location from multiple perspectives, ranging from zoning and land use to population characteristics and access to services. This research introduces the Housing Potential Common Data Model (HPCDM) to overcome existing data silos, serving as a standard to support integration and interoperability across the diverse range of datasets that are required for housing potential analysis. This report details the evaluation of the model along with the creation of a City Digital Twin for housing and a pilot dashboard application to demonstrate a practical implementation. Beyond the technical framework, this work identifies critical barriers to adoption and provides actionable mitigation strategies for urban planners and stakeholders.

AI.tldr: Introduces a standard data model (HPCDM) and a digital twin approach to integrate diverse datasets for evaluating urban housing potential, plus a pilot dashboard and adoption guidance.
AI.motivation: Housing potential assessment currently relies on fragmented, siloed datasets (zoning, land use, demographics, services, etc.), making integrated analysis and planning difficult. There is a need for a common, interoperable data standard to support consistent, scalable evaluation of housing opportunities in cities.
AI.method: Design and specification of the Housing Potential Common Data Model (HPCDM) as a standard schema for integrating multi-source datasets; implementation of a City Digital Twin focused on housing using this model; development of a pilot dashboard application that consumes the digital twin data; and evaluation of the model and tools, including analysis of adoption barriers and mitigation strategies.
AI.result: A defined HPCDM schema; a working City Digital Twin instance for housing potential analysis built on that schema; a functional demonstration dashboard for exploring housing-related data; and an evidence-based list of key adoption barriers with proposed mitigation strategies for planners and stakeholders.
AI.conclusion: The HPCDM and associated digital twin and dashboard demonstrate that a standardized, interoperable data model can effectively integrate heterogeneous urban datasets to support housing potential analysis. Successful adoption, however, depends not only on the technical framework but also on addressing institutional, organizational, and governance barriers through targeted strategies for planners and other stakeholders.

title: TableVista: Benchmarking Multimodal Table Reasoning under Visual and Structural Complexity
summary: We introduce TableVista, a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating foundation models in multimodal table reasoning under visual and structural complexity. TableVista consists of 3,000 high-quality table reasoning problems, where each instance is expanded into 10 distinct visual variants through our multi-style rendering and transformation pipeline. This process encompasses diverse scenario styles, robustness perturbations, and vision-only configurations, culminating in 30,000 multimodal samples for a multi-dimensional evaluation. We conduct an extensive evaluation of 29 state-of-the-art open-source and proprietary foundation models on TableVista. Through comprehensive quantitative and qualitative analysis, we find that while evaluated models remain largely stable across diverse rendering styles, they exhibit pronounced performance degradation on complex structural layouts and vision-only settings, revealing that current models struggle to maintain reasoning consistency when structural complexity combines with visually integrated presentations. These findings highlight critical gaps in current multimodal capabilities, providing insights for advancing more robust and reliable table understanding models.

AI.tldr: TableVista is a new benchmark to systematically test multimodal foundation models on visually and structurally complex table reasoning, revealing weaknesses in handling complex layouts and vision-only table understanding.
AI.motivation: Existing multimodal foundation models are widely used for table understanding, but there is a lack of a rigorous, large-scale benchmark that stresses both visual appearance changes and structural complexity in tables. Current evaluations often miss how models behave under different rendering styles, perturbations, and when only visual input is available without explicit structure. The authors aim to fill this gap and to diagnose where current models fail in real-world, visually complex table reasoning scenarios.
AI.method: The authors build TableVista, a benchmark of 3,000 high-quality table reasoning problems. Each problem is rendered into 10 distinct visual variants via a multi-style rendering and transformation pipeline that introduces diverse scenario styles, robustness perturbations, and vision-only configurations. This yields 30,000 multimodal samples. They then evaluate 29 state-of-the-art open-source and proprietary foundation models on this dataset and perform both quantitative (accuracy metrics across conditions) and qualitative (error-type and behavior) analyses.
AI.result: Across 29 evaluated models, performance is relatively stable under changes in rendering style alone, suggesting robustness to superficial visual variations. However, model performance degrades sharply when tables have complex structural layouts and in vision-only setups where structure must be inferred from the visual input. These conditions expose inconsistency in models’ reasoning when visual complexity and structural complexity coincide.
AI.conclusion: TableVista exposes critical limitations in current multimodal foundation models for table reasoning: they are not yet reliable when dealing with complex table structures, especially in purely visual formats. The benchmark thereby provides a diagnostic tool and design target for developing more robust, structure-aware, and reasoning-consistent table understanding models.

title: AgenticRAG: Agentic Retrieval for Enterprise Knowledge Bases
summary: We present AgenticRAG, a practical agentic harness for retrieval and analysis over enterprise knowledge bases. Standard RAG pipelines place significant burden of grounding on the search stack, constraining the language model to a fixed candidate set chosen deep in the retrieval process. Our approach reduces this overdependence by layering a lightweight harness on top of existing enterprise search infrastructure, equipping a reasoning LLM with search, find, open, and summarize tools enabling the model to iteratively retrieve information, navigate within documents, and analyze evidence autonomously. On three open benchmarks we observe substantial gains: $49.6\%$ recall@1 on BRIGHT (+21.8 pp over the best embedding baseline), 0.96 factuality on WixQA ($+13\%$ relative improvement), and $92\%$ answer correctness on FinanceBench--within 2 pp of oracle access to true evidence. Ablation studies show that the most significant factor is the shift from single-shot retrieval to agentic tool use ($5.9\times$ improvement), while multi-query search and in-document navigation contribute to both quality and efficiency. We present various design choices in our agentic harness that were informed by pre-production deployments. Our results demonstrate its suitability for real-world enterprise production environments.

AI.tldr: AgenticRAG is an agent-style RAG framework that wraps existing enterprise search with LLM tools for search, navigation, and summarization, greatly improving factuality and recall on knowledge-intensive QA tasks.
AI.motivation: Standard RAG systems overly depend on a single-shot retrieval step, forcing the LLM to answer from a fixed, possibly suboptimal candidate set returned by the search stack. In enterprise settings with large, heterogeneous knowledge bases, this leads to missed evidence and lower factual accuracy. The authors aim to design a more flexible, production-ready mechanism that lets LLMs actively explore and analyze enterprise documents while reusing existing search infrastructure.
AI.method: They build a lightweight agentic harness around an LLM, exposing tools such as search, find, open, and summarize that let the model iteratively retrieve, inspect, and navigate within documents. The system layers on top of existing enterprise search, enabling multi-query search, in-document navigation, and autonomous evidence analysis. They evaluate on three public benchmarks and conduct ablations to quantify the impact of agentic tool use, multi-query search, and navigation.
AI.result: On BRIGHT, AgenticRAG achieves 49.6% recall@1, a +21.8 percentage point gain over the best embedding-based baseline. On WixQA, it reaches 0.96 factuality, a 13% relative improvement, and on FinanceBench it yields 92% answer correctness, within 2 percentage points of an oracle with direct access to the true evidence. Ablation experiments show that moving from single-shot retrieval to agentic tool use provides the largest improvement (5.9×), and that multi-query search and document navigation further boost both quality and efficiency.
AI.conclusion: AgenticRAG’s agent-style tool harness substantially outperforms standard RAG that relies on single-shot retrieval, mainly by allowing the LLM to iteratively search and navigate documents. The design, validated by pre-production deployments, is practical for real-world enterprise scenarios because it reuses existing search infrastructure while delivering near-oracle performance on several benchmarks, making it suitable for production enterprise environments.

title: Tatarstan Toponyms: A Bilingual Dataset and Hybrid RAG System for Geospatial Question Answering
summary: This paper addresses automatic geospatial question answering over multilingual toponymic data. An original bilingual dataset of toponyms of the Republic of Tatarstan is introduced, comprising 9,688 structured records with linguistic, etymological, administrative, and coordinate information (93.1% georeferenced). Based on this dataset, a question-answering corpus of approximately 39,000 question-context-answer triples is constructed with guaranteed answer localization. A hybrid retriever integrates dense semantic indexing (multilingual-e5-large) with geospatial filtering via KD-trees and haversine distance. On 500 test queries, the hybrid search achieves Recall@1=0.988, Recall@5=1.000, and MRR=0.994, significantly outperforming BM25 and purely spatial methods. Among tested reader architectures (RuBERT, XLM-RoBERTa-large, T5-RUS), XLM-RoBERTa-large attains the best quality: EM=0.992, F1=0.994. On raw outputs, RuBERT models fail on coordinate questions (F1=0) while XLM-RoBERTa-large reaches F1=0.984; however, simple post-processing eliminates numerical gaps and restores RuBERT accuracy to 100%. This discrepancy stems from tokenization differences and pre-training corpora composition. All resources (dataset, QA corpus, model weights, web demo) are openly published on Hugging Face. Results apply to geospatial QA services, geocoding, and digital humanities in multilingual regions.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a new bilingual (Russian–Tatar) toponym dataset for Tatarstan and a large QA corpus built on it, plus a hybrid semantic–geospatial QA system that strongly outperforms text-only or spatial-only baselines.
AI.motivation: Geospatial question answering often requires both semantic understanding of questions and precise spatial reasoning, but existing resources and systems rarely support multilingual toponyms or combine text and geographic constraints effectively, especially for under-resourced regions such as the Republic of Tatarstan.
AI.method: 1) Construct a bilingual, structured toponym database with linguistic, etymological, administrative and coordinate attributes. 2) Generate ~39k QA triples over this data with guaranteed answer spans. 3) Build a hybrid retriever that merges dense multilingual semantic embeddings (multilingual-e5-large) with spatial filtering using KD-trees and haversine distance. 4) Evaluate multiple reader models (RuBERT, XLM-RoBERTa-large, T5-RUS) and apply post-processing to normalize numeric answers like coordinates. 5) Release all resources and a demo on Hugging Face.
AI.result: The bilingual toponym dataset contains 9,688 records, 93.1% with coordinates. The QA corpus has ~39k triples. On a 500-query test set, the hybrid retriever achieves Recall@1=0.988, Recall@5=1.000, and MRR=0.994, clearly beating BM25 and pure spatial baselines. XLM-RoBERTa-large is the strongest reader (EM=0.992, F1=0.994). RuBERT initially fails on coordinate questions (F1=0), but simple numeric post-processing recovers its performance to 100%, exposing sensitivity to tokenization and pretraining differences.
AI.conclusion: Combining dense multilingual semantic retrieval with explicit geospatial filtering yields near-perfect performance on bilingual toponymic QA, and coordinate-style numeric answers require tailored post-processing for some transformer readers. The released datasets, models, and demo provide a practical foundation for geospatial QA, geocoding, and digital humanities applications in multilingual settings like Tatarstan.

title: SPARK: Self-Play with Asymmetric Reward from Knowledge Graphs
summary: Self-play reinforcement learning has shown strong performance in domains with formally verifiable structure, such as mathematics and coding, where both problem generation and reward computation can be grounded in explicit rules. Extending this paradigm to scientific literature is more challenging: the relationships among multi-modal elements within and across documents are rarely made explicit in text, which makes automatic generation of relational reasoning questions difficult and weakens the reliability of reward signals. We propose SPARK (Self-Play with Asymmetric Reward from Knowledge Graphs), a framework that automatically constructs a unified knowledge graph (KG) from multi-document scientific literature and uses it as the structural basis for self-play. KG paths over multimodal nodes serve as a source for generating relational reasoning questions, and structured facts stored in the KG provide a basis for verifiable reward computation. A single small vision-language model (sVLM) alternates between Proposer and Solver roles under information asymmetry against a fixed KG, a design that we believe can be naturally extended toward online adaptation in future work. We evaluate SPARK on public benchmarks and a self-constructed cross-document multi-hop QA dataset. Results show that SPARK consistently outperforms flat-corpus-based self-play baselines, and the performance gap widens as hop count increases, suggesting that KG-structure grounding contributes to relational multi-hop reasoning beyond what unstructured corpus grounding can provide.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SPARK, a self-play reinforcement learning framework that uses a unified knowledge graph built from multi-document scientific literature to generate relational reasoning questions and verifiable rewards, improving multi-hop scientific QA over flat-corpus self-play baselines.
AI.motivation: Self-play RL works well in structured domains like math and code because problems and rewards can be grounded in explicit rules. Scientific literature is more complex: relationships between text, figures, and tables within and across papers are implicit, making it hard to automatically generate good relational reasoning questions and reliable reward signals. The authors aim to bring the benefits of self-play RL to scientific reasoning by introducing an explicit, machine-usable structure over multi-modal scientific content.
AI.method: They propose SPARK, which first automatically constructs a unified knowledge graph (KG) from multi-document, multi-modal scientific literature. Nodes represent multimodal entities (e.g., textual concepts, visual elements), and edges encode their relationships. Self-play is performed over this KG: paths in the graph are used to generate relational, multi-hop questions, and the structured facts in the KG allow for programmatic, verifiable reward computation. A single small vision-language model plays two alternating roles: Proposer (generating questions/tasks under information asymmetry) and Solver (answering them), while the KG remains fixed, enabling asymmetric information and grounded feedback.
AI.result: On public benchmarks and a custom cross-document multi-hop QA dataset, SPARK outperforms baselines that rely only on a flat text corpus for self-play, without KG structure. The performance gains are especially large for questions requiring more hops, indicating better relational and multi-step reasoning compared to unstructured approaches.
AI.conclusion: Grounding self-play RL in an automatically constructed knowledge graph over multi-modal scientific literature improves multi-hop relational reasoning compared with flat-corpus grounding. The asymmetric Proposer/Solver setup with a fixed KG yields more reliable question generation and reward computation, and the framework appears promising for future extensions to online, continually updated scientific reasoning systems.

title: From Articles to Premises: Building PrimeFacts, an Extraction Methodology and Resource for Fact-Checking Evidence
comment: Accepted at LREC 2026. To appear in the conference proceedings
summary: Fact-checking articles encode rich supporting evidence and reasoning, yet this evidence remains largely inaccessible to automated verification systems due to unstructured presentation. We introduce PrimeFacts, a methodology and resource for extracting fine-grained evidence from full fact-checking articles. We compile 13,106 PolitiFact articles with claims, verdicts, and all referenced sources, and we identify 49,718 in-article hyperlinks as natural anchors to pinpoint key evidence. Our framework leverages large language models (LLMs) to rewrite these anchor sentences into stand-alone, context-independent premises and investigates the extraction of additional implicit evidence. In evaluations on cross-article evidence retrieval and claim verification, the extracted premises substantially improve performance. Decontextualized evidence yields higher retrievability, achieving up to a 30 percent relative gain in Mean Reciprocal Rank over verbatim sentences, and using the evidence for verdict prediction raises Macro-F1 by 10-20 points over the baseline. These gains are consistent across different verdict granularities (2-class vs. 5-class) and model architectures. A qualitative analysis indicates that the decontextualized premises remain faithful to the original sources. Our work highlights the promise of reusing fact-checkers' evidence for automation and provides a large-scale resource of structured evidence from real-world fact-checks.

AI.tldr: PrimeFacts extracts structured, fine-grained evidence from fact-checking articles to improve automated fact verification.
AI.motivation: Automated fact-checking systems struggle to use the rich but unstructured evidence in human-written fact-checking articles. Current systems typically work with short claims and lack access to the full reasoning and sources that fact-checkers compile. The authors aim to unlock this underused information to improve evidence retrieval and claim verification.
AI.method: The authors create PrimeFacts by collecting 13,106 PolitiFact articles with claims, verdicts, and referenced sources, and identify 49,718 in-article hyperlinks as anchors for key evidence. They use large language models to rewrite hyperlink-containing sentences into decontextualized, stand-alone premises and also explore extracting additional implicit evidence. They then evaluate these structured premises on cross-article evidence retrieval and automated claim verification tasks, comparing them against using original verbatim sentences.
AI.result: The decontextualized premises markedly improve system performance. For evidence retrieval, they increase Mean Reciprocal Rank by up to 30% relative to using original sentences. For verdict prediction, incorporating the extracted evidence boosts Macro-F1 by 10–20 points over baselines. These improvements are robust across multiple label granularities (binary vs. five-class) and across different model architectures. Qualitative analysis suggests that the rewritten premises remain faithful to their source content.
AI.conclusion: Structuring the evidence embedded in fact-checking articles—particularly via decontextualized premises generated from hyperlink-anchored sentences—makes this information far more accessible to automated systems. PrimeFacts demonstrates that reusing fact-checkers’ evidence can substantially enhance evidence retrieval and claim verification, and it contributes a large-scale dataset of fine-grained, real-world fact-check evidence to support further research in automated fact-checking.

title: Who Prices Cognitive Labor in the Age of Agents? A Position on Compute-Anchored Wages
summary: A natural intuition about the economics of AI agents is that, because agents can be replicated at near-zero marginal cost, they constitute a labor input in infinitely elastic supply, and therefore drive cognitive-labor wages to zero. We argue this framing is wrong in mechanism but partially correct in conclusion, and that the correction matters for both theory and policy. \textbf{Agents are not labor; they are a production technology that converts compute capital $K_c$ into effective units of cognitive labor $L_A$.} Once this is recognized, the elastic-supply margin that anchors the equilibrium wage migrates from the labor market to the compute capital market. Building on the textbook factor-pricing framework \citep{mankiw2020}, we derive a \emph{Compute-Anchored Wage} (CAW) bound stating that, on tasks where human and agent cognitive labor are substitutes, the competitive human wage is bounded above by $λ\cdot k \cdot r_c$, where $r_c$ is the rental rate of compute capital, $k$ is the compute intensity of one effective agent-labor unit, and $λ$ is the relative human-to-agent productivity. We generalize the result through CES aggregation, separate substitutable from complementary tasks (yielding a directional inversion of skill-biased technical change), and discuss factor-share consequences. The position is concise: \emph{the price-setter for cognitive labor is no longer the labor market.}

AI.tldr: The paper reconceptualizes AI agents not as labor but as a technology that transforms compute capital into effective cognitive labor, deriving a compute-based upper bound on human wages when humans and AI are substitutes.
AI.motivation: Common intuition claims that infinitely replicable AI agents will make cognitive labor supply infinitely elastic and push wages to zero. The authors believe this narrative misidentifies the true margin of elasticity and thus misguides both economic theory and policy about AI, labor markets, and distributional impacts. They want a more accurate framework for how AI affects wages and factor prices.
AI.method: Use a standard neoclassical factor-pricing framework (as in Mankiw) where AI agents are modeled as a production technology converting compute capital into effective cognitive labor units. Derive a Compute-Anchored Wage (CAW) bound by treating the supply of agent labor as governed by compute capital and its rental rate, then generalize the analysis using CES aggregation of human and agent inputs, distinguishing substitutable from complementary tasks and analyzing factor shares.
AI.result: They show that when human and AI cognitive labor are substitutes, the competitive wage for human workers is bounded above by λ·k·r_c, where r_c is the rental rate of compute capital, k is the compute requirement per unit of effective agent labor, and λ is humans’ productivity relative to agents. They extend the model to more general production functions and task structures, identifying how AI changes which tasks are substitutional vs complementary and how this can invert the usual pattern of skill-biased technical change and alter income shares across factors.
AI.conclusion: AI agents should be treated as a compute-based production technology, not as labor in infinitely elastic supply. This shifts the wage-determining margin from the labor market to the compute capital market: the key anchor for cognitive-labor wages is the cost and intensity of compute, encapsulated in the CAW bound. This reframing has important implications for how economists analyze AI’s impact on wages, inequality, and policy levers that act through compute markets rather than labor markets.

title: More Aligned, Less Diverse? Analyzing the Grammar and Lexicon of Two Generations of LLMs
summary: This study contributes to a growing line of research in comparing LLM-generated texts with human-authored text, in this case, English news text. We focus in particular on the evaluation of syntactic properties through formal grammar frameworks. Our analysis compares two generations of LLMs in the context of two human-authored English news datasets from two different years. Employing the Head-Driven Phrase Structure Grammar (HPSG) formalism, we investigate the distributions of syntactic structures and lexical types of AI-generated texts and contrast them with the corresponding distributions in the human-authored New York Times (NYT) articles.
