This paper proposes an automated diagnostic approach for severity grading of KOA by blending a deep learning convolutional neural network (CNN) with a device-based inference platform powered by TensorFlow Lite. It proposes a model based on the ResNet-18 convolutional neural network. The designed model is trained on publicly available database. Through a transfer learning approach obtained knee images are first classified into five Kellgren-Lawrence (KL) grades. Further the developed model is optimised. During the training of the model test accuracy of 94.48% with stable convergence has been achieved. Subsequently the optimised model transformed into a lightweight TensorFlow Lite format, facilitating seamless deployment on resource-constrained devices. The designed model is capable enough to operate in the environment having no continuous internet connectivity. Also, an auxiliary Large Language Model (Gemini-2.0-flash) is applied to generate structured interpretive findings like potential symptoms, risk factors, and preventive majors etc. The LLM component functions as interface without influencing the classification process. The proposed model articulates the feasibility of an on-device, interpretable decision-support tools for early diagnosis and improve accessibility to Artificial Intelligence (AI)-assisted knee screening tool.

AI.tldr: They build an automated system using a ResNet-18 CNN plus TensorFlow Lite deployment and an auxiliary LLM to grade knee osteoarthritis severity on-device with high accuracy.
AI.motivation: Knee osteoarthritis is common, causes pain and disability, and current diagnostic grading is subjective and variable between clinicians. There is a need for precise, timely, and consistent severity assessment that can work even in settings with limited resources and connectivity.
AI.method: They use transfer learning with a ResNet-18 CNN trained on a public knee X-ray database to classify images into five Kellgren-Lawrence grades. After achieving good performance and stable convergence, they optimize and convert the model into a lightweight TensorFlow Lite version for deployment on resource-constrained devices. Additionally, they integrate an external LLM (Gemini-2.0-flash) to generate structured explanatory outputs (symptoms, risk factors, prevention) as a non-intrusive interpretive interface.
AI.result: The CNN model achieves 94.48% test accuracy and stable training convergence on KL grade classification. The optimized model is successfully converted to TensorFlow Lite and can run locally on devices without continuous internet access. The LLM component can provide structured interpretive text without affecting the classification results.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates that an on-device AI system using a compact CNN and TensorFlow Lite, supplemented by an LLM for interpretation, is feasible for accurate, accessible, and interpretable decision support in early KOA diagnosis and severity grading, particularly in low-connectivity or resource-limited environments.

title: GATHER: Convergence-Centric Hyper-Entity Retrieval for Zero-Shot Cell-Type Annotation
summary: Zero-shot single-cell cell-type annotation aims to determine a cell's type from a given set of expressed genes without any training. Existing knowledge-graph-based RAG approaches retrieve evidence by expanding from source entities and relying on iterative LLM reasoning. However, in this setting each query contains tens to hundreds of genes, where no single gene is decisive and the label emerges only from their collective co-occurrence. Such hyper-entity queries fundamentally challenge local, entity-wise exploration strategies, which reason from individual genes, leading to poor scalability and substantial LLM cost. We propose GATHER (Graph-Aware Traversal with Hyper-Entity Retrieval), a convergence-centric retriever tailored to hyper-entity queries. It performs global multi-source graph traversal and identifies topological convergence points -- nodes jointly reachable from many input genes. These convergence nodes act as high-information hyper-entities that capture entity synergy. By incorporating node- and path-importance scoring, GATHER selects informative evidence entirely without LLM involvement during retrieval. Instantiated on a self-constructed cell-centric biological knowledge graph (VCKG), GATHER outperforms strong KG-RAG baselines (ToG, ToG-2, RoG, PoG) on two datasets (Immune and Lung), achieving the highest exact-match accuracy (27.45% and 59.64%) with only a single LLM call per sample, compared to 2--61 calls for KG-RAG baselines. Our results demonstrate that convergence nodes compress multi-entity signals into compact, high-information evidence that conveys more per item than multi-hop paths, providing an efficient global alternative to local entity-wise reasoning.

AI.tldr: A new retrieval method, GATHER, improves zero-shot cell-type annotation from gene sets by globally traversing a biological knowledge graph to find convergence nodes that summarize joint gene signals, reducing LLM calls while improving accuracy over existing KG-RAG baselines.
AI.motivation: Zero-shot single-cell cell-type annotation must identify cell types from large gene sets where no single gene is decisive, making traditional knowledge-graph RAG methods inefficient because they expand from individual entities and rely on many iterative LLM calls. There is a need for a retrieval strategy that can handle hyper-entity queries (many genes at once) efficiently and capture their collective signal without expensive local reasoning.
AI.method: The paper introduces GATHER (Graph-Aware Traversal with Hyper-Entity Retrieval), a convergence-centric retrieval algorithm designed for hyper-entity queries. GATHER performs global multi-source graph traversal starting from all query genes simultaneously, then detects topological convergence points: nodes that are jointly reachable from many input genes. These convergence nodes act as high-information hyper-entities summarizing gene co-occurrence. GATHER further scores nodes and paths for importance and selects evidence purely via graph algorithms, without involving LLMs during retrieval. The method is implemented on a custom cell-centric biological knowledge graph (VCKG) and tested on cell-type annotation tasks.
AI.result: On two single-cell datasets (Immune and Lung), GATHER achieves higher exact-match cell-type annotation accuracy than strong KG-RAG baselines (ToG, ToG-2, RoG, PoG), with reported exact-match accuracies of 27.45% and 59.64%, respectively. It also substantially reduces LLM usage, requiring only a single LLM call per sample versus 2 to 61 calls for baseline methods, while still improving performance.
AI.conclusion: Convergence-based graph traversal can effectively compress information from many input entities into a small set of high-information convergence nodes, which provide richer evidence than traditional multi-hop paths. This global, LLM-free retrieval step makes zero-shot cell-type annotation more accurate and efficient, offering a scalable alternative to local, entity-wise KG-RAG approaches for hyper-entity queries in biological knowledge graphs.

title: SDFlow: Similarity-Driven Flow Matching for Time Series Generation
summary: Vector quantization (VQ) with autoregressive (AR) token modeling is a widely adopted and highly competitive paradigm for time-series generation. However, such models are fundamentally limited by exposure bias: during inference, errors can accumulate across sequential predictions, leading to pronounced quality degradation in long-horizon generation. To address this, we propose SDFlow ($\textbf{S}$imilarity-$\textbf{D}$riven $\textbf{Flow}$ Matching), a non-autoregressive framework that operates entirely in the frozen VQ latent space and enables parallel sequence generation via flow matching. We tackle three key challenges in making this transition: (1) eliminating exposure bias by replacing step-wise token prediction with a global transport map; (2) mitigating the high-dimensionality of VQ token spaces via a low-rank manifold decomposition with a learned anchor prior over the latent manifold; and (3) incorporating discrete supervision into continuous transport dynamics by introducing a categorical posterior over codebook indices within a variational flow-matching formulation. Extensive experiments show that SDFlow achieves state-of-the-art performance, improving Discriminative Score and substantially reducing Context-FID, particularly for challenging long-sequence generation. Moreover, SDFlow provides significant inference speedups over autoregressive baselines, offering both high fidelity and computational efficiency. Code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/SDFlow-D6F3/

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SDFlow, a non-autoregressive, flow-matching-based framework in VQ latent space that removes exposure bias and enables fast, high-fidelity long-horizon time-series generation.
AI.motivation: Autoregressive token modeling over vector-quantized latents is strong for time-series generation but suffers from exposure bias, especially in long-horizon generation, where sequential prediction errors accumulate and degrade quality. There is a need for a generation framework that both avoids exposure bias and accelerates inference while maintaining or improving fidelity.
AI.method: The authors propose SDFlow, which generates sequences non-autoregressively in a frozen VQ latent space using flow matching. They (1) replace step-wise token prediction with a global transport map to avoid exposure bias; (2) address the high-dimensional VQ token space via a low-rank manifold decomposition and a learned anchor prior over the latent manifold; and (3) integrate discrete supervision into continuous transport dynamics through a categorical posterior over codebook indices within a variational flow-matching formulation.
AI.result: Across extensive experiments, SDFlow attains state-of-the-art performance, notably improving Discriminative Score and substantially lowering Context-FID, with especially strong gains on long-sequence generation tasks. The method also yields significant inference speedups compared to autoregressive baselines.
AI.conclusion: SDFlow demonstrates that non-autoregressive flow matching in VQ latent space can overcome exposure bias and high-dimensional token challenges, enabling parallel, computationally efficient, and high-fidelity time-series generation, particularly effective for long-horizon scenarios.

title: MiA-Signature: Approximating Global Activation for Long-Context Understanding
comment: This is a work in progress; we will continue to revise and improve the manuscript
summary: A growing body of work in cognitive science suggests that reportable conscious access is associated with \emph{global ignition} over distributed memory systems, while such activation is only partially accessible as individuals cannot directly access or enumerate all activated contents. This tension suggests a plausible mechanism that cognition may rely on a compact representation that approximates the global influence of activation on downstream processing. Inspired by this idea, we introduce the concept of \textbf{Mindscape Activation Signature (MiA-Signature)}, a compressed representation of the global activation pattern induced by a query. In LLM systems, this is instantiated via submodular-based selection of high-level concepts that cover the activated context space, optionally refined through lightweight iterative updates using working memory. The resulting MiA-Signature serves as a conditioning signal that approximates the effect of the full activation state while remaining computationally tractable. Integrating MiA-Signatures into both RAG and agentic systems yields consistent performance gains across multiple long-context understanding tasks.

AI.tldr: They propose MiA-Signature, a compressed representation of global activation patterns in LLMs, to approximate conscious-like global ignition and improve long-context tasks in RAG and agentic systems.
AI.motivation: There is a tension between the idea that conscious access corresponds to global ignition of distributed representations and the fact that agents (biological or artificial) cannot enumerate or directly access all activated content. This suggests a need for a compact surrogate that captures the downstream influence of a large activation pattern without exposing all its details, particularly in long-context reasoning for LLMs.
AI.method: They define Mindscape Activation Signature (MiA-Signature) as a compressed representation of the global activation pattern induced by a query. In LLMs, they construct this via submodular optimization to select a subset of high-level concepts that cover the activated context space. They optionally refine this representation through lightweight iterative updates using a working-memory-like mechanism. This MiA-Signature is then used as a conditioning signal in RAG and agentic pipelines, approximating the effect of the full activation state in a cheaper form.
AI.result: When MiA-Signatures are integrated into RAG and agentic LLM systems, they achieve consistent performance improvements on multiple long-context understanding benchmarks compared to baselines without MiA-Signatures.
AI.conclusion: MiA-Signatures provide an effective, computationally tractable approximation to a globally activated state in LLMs, inspired by theories of conscious access in cognitive science, and yield measurable gains in long-context reasoning across different system settings such as RAG and agent frameworks.

title: ReFlect: An Effective Harness System for Complex Long-Horizon LLM Reasoning
summary: Current reasoning paradigms for LLMs include chain-of-thought, ReAct, and post-hoc self-critique. These paradigms rely on two assumptions that fail on long-horizon, multi-stage tasks. As a result, errors accumulate silently across reasoning steps, leaving an open question: can a reasoning system effectively detect and recover from its own failures? We present ReFlect, a \emph{harness} system for LLM reasoning that creates standalone error detection and recovery logic as a deterministic wrapper around the model. Controlled experiments across 6 reasoning domains show that prompt-level self-critique produces formulaic templates that flag no issues in 90 of 100 audited reflection blocks, and the investigated LLMs wrongly accept a wrong answer in at least 76\% of cases. Our ReFlect harness achieves task success rates ranging from 41\% on gpt-4o-mini to 56\% on Claude Sonnet 4.5 across six models spanning small and frontier scale, with per-model gains over Direct CoT ranging from +7 pp on Qwen2.5-72B to +29 pp on Claude Sonnet 4.5, and additionally raises SWE-bench patch-structural quality from 0\% (Direct CoT) to between 82\% (Qwen2.5-72B) and 87\% (GPT-4o). Notably, the harness gain is inversely proportional to the model's Direct CoT task success rate (the fitted slope is -1.69 with r=-0.76): each pp lost in baseline success rate is mechanically recovered by 1.69 pp of harness gain. We spot that adding structured reasoning state and operators yields only 15.0--18.7\% pair-mean on Llama-3.3-70B and Qwen2.5-72B because models at this scale cannot reliably populate the state its operators require. ReFlect is model-agnostic, training-free, and operates entirely at inference time.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ReFlect, a deterministic harness around LLMs that detects and recovers from reasoning failures, significantly improving success rates on long-horizon, multi-stage tasks compared to standard chain-of-thought and self-critique methods.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM reasoning paradigms like chain-of-thought, ReAct, and post-hoc self-critique assume that intermediate steps are mostly correct and that the model can reliably critique itself. These assumptions break down on long-horizon, multi-stage tasks where small errors accumulate, and current self-reflection methods often fail to detect mistakes. The authors aim to answer whether a reasoning system can systematically detect and recover from its own failures, without retraining models.
AI.method: They design ReFlect, a model-agnostic, training-free harness that wraps an LLM with deterministic logic for explicit error detection and recovery. Instead of relying on the model’s own unstructured self-critique, ReFlect maintains structured reasoning state and uses operators to check for and respond to failures. The harness orchestrates multiple calls to the base model at inference time, guiding the reasoning process and triggering recovery when inconsistencies or low-quality outputs are detected. They evaluate ReFlect across six reasoning domains and multiple models of varying scale, comparing it mainly to Direct Chain-of-Thought and prompt-level self-critique baselines.
AI.result: Prompt-level self-critique is shown to be largely ineffective: 90% of 100 audited reflection blocks flag no issues, and examined LLMs accept wrong answers in at least 76% of cases. ReFlect improves task success rates to 41–56% across six models, with per-model gains over Direct CoT ranging from +7 percentage points (Qwen2.5-72B) to +29 percentage points (Claude Sonnet 4.5). On SWE-bench, ReFlect raises patch structural quality from 0% under Direct CoT to 82–87% depending on the model. The harness benefit is inversely correlated with baseline performance: each percentage point of baseline accuracy lost is compensated by approximately 1.69 percentage points of gain from the harness (slope -1.69, r=-0.76). They also find that simply adding structured state and operators without a robust harness yields only 15.0–18.7% pair-mean performance on mid-scale models like Llama-3.3-70B and Qwen2.5-72B, due to their inability to reliably fill the required state.
AI.conclusion: ReFlect demonstrates that a deterministic, model-agnostic harness can substantially improve LLM performance on long-horizon, multi-stage reasoning tasks by systematically detecting and recovering from reasoning failures at inference time, without any model training. Naive self-critique and lightly structured reasoning are insufficient, especially for mid-scale models that struggle to maintain accurate reasoning state. The gains from ReFlect are larger for weaker baselines, suggesting that robust external control logic can effectively compensate for limitations in the underlying LLM’s reasoning abilities.

title: From 124 Million Tokens to 1,021 Neologisms: A Large-Scale Pipeline for Automatic Neologism Detection
comment: 14 pages, 5 tables. Accepted at NeoLLM 2026 Workshop, co-located with LREC-COLING 2026
summary: We present a scalable, modular pipeline for automatic neologism detection that combines rule-based filtering with LLM classification. The pipeline is grounded in two complementary word-formation frameworks, grammatical and extra-grammatical morphology, which jointly define the scope of what counts as a neologism and inform a four-class classification scheme (neologism, entity, foreign, none). While designed to be modular and transferable at the architectural level, the pipeline is instantiated on 527 million English-language Reddit posts spanning 2005-2024. From this corpus, we extract 124.6 million unique tokens and reduce them by over 99.99% to yield 1,021 neologism candidates, a set small enough for manual expert verification. Multiple LLMs independently classify each candidate via majority vote, with a final verification step, revealing substantial cross-model disagreement and highlighting the challenge of operationalizing neologism detection at scale. Manual annotation of all 1,021 candidates confirms that 599 (58.7%) are genuine lexical innovations. The pipeline code, vocabulary compilation scripts, and the annotated candidate list are available at https://github.com/DiegoRossini/neologism-pipeline.

AI.tldr: A scalable, modular pipeline detects neologisms in massive Reddit data by combining rule-based filtering with LLM-based multi-model classification, yielding a small set of high-precision neologism candidates for expert validation.
AI.motivation: Detecting neologisms at web scale is difficult due to the vast number of noisy, rare, or non-standard tokens. Existing approaches often lack scalability, clear theoretical grounding in word-formation, or practical mechanisms to narrow millions of candidate forms down to a manageable, high-quality subset for expert review. The authors aim to create a robust, theoretically informed, and scalable pipeline that can be applied to very large corpora to systematically identify genuine lexical innovations.
AI.method: The authors design a modular pipeline rooted in grammatical and extra-grammatical morphology that first applies rule-based filtering to 124.6M unique tokens extracted from 527M Reddit posts (2005–2024). This filtering reduces noise and focuses on forms that align with word-formation theories. They then apply multiple large language models as independent classifiers over the remaining candidates, assigning each token to one of four classes (neologism, entity, foreign, none). A majority-vote scheme aggregates LLM outputs, followed by a final expert verification step. The architecture is corpus-agnostic even though instantiated on Reddit here, and all code and resources are released.
AI.result: From 124.6 million unique tokens, the pipeline reduces the candidate space by over 99.99% to 1,021 neologism candidates suitable for manual inspection. Cross-model comparisons show substantial disagreement among LLMs on class labels, underscoring the difficulty of consistently operationalizing neologism detection. Manual expert annotation verifies that 599 (58.7%) of the 1,021 candidates are genuine neologisms, indicating that the pipeline achieves a relatively high precision in surfacing true lexical innovations from extremely large, noisy data.
AI.conclusion: A theoretically grounded, modular, and scalable pipeline can effectively identify genuine neologisms from massive online text collections, drastically shrinking the search space from hundreds of millions of tokens to around a thousand candidates. While large language models are useful components in this process, their disagreements reveal limitations and the continued necessity of expert validation. The released code, scripts, and annotated data support reuse, extension, and adaptation of the approach to other corpora and languages.

title: HyperLens: Quantifying Cognitive Effort in LLMs with Fine-grained Confidence Trajectory
summary: While Large Language Models (LLMs) achieve strong performance across diverse tasks, their inference dynamics remain poorly understood because of the limited resolution of existing analysis tools. In this work, we identify an intrinsic magnification mechanism in transformer architectures: deeper layers inherently magnify the small changes of layer-wise confidence, providing a fine-grained confidence trajectory. Building on this insight, we introduce HyperLens, a high-resolution probe designed to trace confidence trajectories and quantify the cognitive effort during inference. Across LLMs and datasets, HyperLens reveals a consistent divergence in confidence trajectories that separates complex from simple tasks. We abstract this pattern into a quantitative cognitive effort metric. Our analysis reveals a fundamental principle: complex tasks consistently require higher cognitive effort. Finally, we provide a mechanistic diagnosis of a common side effect of standard Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT): it can reduce cognitive effort and consequently degrade performance on in-domain tasks.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces HyperLens, a tool to trace layer-wise confidence trajectories in LLMs and define a cognitive effort metric, revealing that complex tasks induce higher effort and that standard SFT can undesirably reduce this effort and harm in-domain performance.
AI.motivation: Although LLMs are strong across many tasks, we lack a fine-grained understanding of how their confidence evolves during inference because existing tools provide low-resolution views of internal dynamics. The authors want to uncover intrinsic mechanisms in transformers that can be exploited to better analyze and quantify the reasoning or effort LLMs expend on different tasks.
AI.method: They first identify a magnification mechanism in transformers where deeper layers amplify small changes in layer-wise confidence, which yields detailed confidence trajectories. Using this, they design HyperLens, a high-resolution probing framework that measures layer-wise confidence over time during inference. They apply HyperLens across various LLMs and datasets, compare the confidence trajectories for simple versus complex tasks, and formalize a quantitative cognitive effort metric derived from these trajectories. They then use this metric to study the impact of standard supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on cognitive effort and performance.
AI.result: HyperLens consistently finds distinct confidence trajectories between complex and simple tasks: complex tasks show more pronounced and extended changes in confidence, corresponding to higher cognitive effort. The derived cognitive effort metric reliably separates complex from simple tasks across models and datasets. When analyzing models before and after standard SFT, they observe that SFT often reduces measured cognitive effort, which is associated with degraded performance on in-domain tasks that require substantial reasoning effort.
AI.conclusion: Transformer architectures naturally magnify small changes in layer-wise confidence as depth increases, enabling fine-grained tracking of inference dynamics. Leveraging this effect, HyperLens provides a high-resolution view of confidence trajectories and a quantitative cognitive effort metric. The central principle they derive is that complex tasks demand higher cognitive effort, visible in internal dynamics. Moreover, standard SFT can inadvertently suppress this effort and harm performance, implying that future training and fine-tuning regimes should preserve or encourage appropriate cognitive effort rather than dampening it.

title: COVID-19 Infodemic. Understanding content features in detecting fake news using a machine learning approach
summary: The use of content features, particularly textual and linguistic for fake news detection is under-researched, despite empirical evidence showing the features could contribute to differentiating real and fake news. To this end, this study investigates a selection of content features such as word bigrams, part of speech distribution etc. to improve fake news detection. We performed a series of experiments on a new dataset gathered during the COVID-19 pandemic and using Decision Tree, K-Nearest Neighbor, Logistic Regression, Support Vector Machine and Random Forest. Random Forest yielded the best results, followed closely by Support Vector Machine, across all setups. In general, both the textual and linguistic features were found to improve fake news detection when used separately, however, combining them into a single model did not improve the detection significantly. Differences were also noted between the use of bigrams and part of speech tags. The study shows that textual and linguistic features can be used successfully in detecting fake news using the traditional machine learning approach as opposed to deep learning.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates textual and linguistic content features (like word bigrams and POS tags) with classical ML models for COVID-19 fake news detection and finds that such features, especially with Random Forest and SVM, can effectively distinguish fake from real news without deep learning.
AI.motivation: Although there is evidence that textual and linguistic characteristics can distinguish fake from real news, most fake news detection work focuses on other signals (e.g., social context, deep models) and under-explores these content features. The authors aim to systematically study how far simple, interpretable content-based features can go for fake news detection, particularly in the context of COVID-19 misinformation.
AI.method: The authors construct a new fake/real news dataset collected during the COVID-19 pandemic. They extract several textual and linguistic features from the news items, including word bigrams and part-of-speech (POS) distribution statistics. They then train and evaluate multiple traditional machine learning classifiers—Decision Tree, K-Nearest Neighbor, Logistic Regression, Support Vector Machine, and Random Forest—under different feature setups: textual features alone, linguistic features alone, and combined features.
AI.result: Across all experiments and feature setups, Random Forest achieves the best performance, with Support Vector Machine performing second best. Both textual features (e.g., bigrams) and linguistic features (e.g., POS distributions) independently improve fake news detection compared to not using them. However, merging textual and linguistic features into a single combined feature space offers little or no additional performance gain. The study also observes distinct performance behaviors when using bigrams versus POS-based features.
AI.conclusion: Textual and linguistic content features, even when used with standard, non-deep-learning classifiers, can effectively support fake news detection, at least in the COVID-19 news domain. Traditional machine learning models such as Random Forest and SVM, fed with carefully designed content features, remain competitive and can be a practical alternative to more complex deep learning approaches. However, simply concatenating different types of content features does not necessarily yield further improvements, suggesting the need for more nuanced feature integration strategies.

title: Best Arm Identification in Generalized Linear Bandits via Hybrid Feedback
summary: We study fixed-confidence best arm identification in generalized linear bandits under a hybrid feedback model: at each round, the learner may query either (i) absolute reward feedback from a single arm or (ii) relative (dueling) feedback from an arm pair, both governed by generalized linear models. We introduce a likelihood-ratio--based confidence sequence that unifies heterogeneous generalized linear observations and yields an explicit ellipsoidal confidence set under a self-concordance assumption. Building on this confidence set, we propose a hybrid Track-and-Stop algorithm that adaptively allocates queries by tracking a minimax-optimal design over a joint action space of arms and pairs. We establish $δ$-correctness and provide high-probability upper bounds on the stopping time. We further extend the framework to a cost-aware setting that accounts for heterogeneous acquisition costs across feedback modalities. Empirical experiments demonstrate that the proposed algorithms significantly improve sample efficiency over baseline methods.

AI.tldr: Hybrid-feedback generalized linear bandits for best-arm identification with a unified confidence sequence and adaptive query allocation, achieving sample-efficient and δ-correct algorithms, including a cost-aware extension.
AI.motivation: Best arm identification in bandits is well-studied for either absolute or relative feedback, but practical systems often have access to both types with different costs. There lacks a principled method that (1) unifies heterogeneous feedback under generalized linear models, (2) adaptively chooses between absolute and relative feedback, and (3) is provably sample-efficient and cost-aware in the fixed-confidence setting.
AI.method: Formulate best arm identification in a generalized linear bandit setting with two feedback modalities: absolute rewards from single arms and relative (dueling) feedback from arm pairs. Develop a likelihood-ratio–based confidence sequence that can incorporate both observation types and, using self-concordance, derive an explicit ellipsoidal confidence set. Design a hybrid Track-and-Stop algorithm that tracks a minimax-optimal design over the combined space of arms and pairs to decide which query to make at each round. Extend this design to handle heterogeneous acquisition costs for different feedback types, balancing information gain versus query cost.
AI.result: Prove that the proposed hybrid Track-and-Stop algorithm is δ-correct for fixed-confidence best arm identification in this hybrid feedback setting, and derive high-probability upper bounds on its stopping time. Show that the minimax-optimal design tracking leads to sample complexity guarantees, and in the cost-aware extension, that the algorithm respects cost heterogeneity while maintaining correctness. Empirical evaluations indicate that the method uses significantly fewer queries (i.e., improved sample efficiency) than baselines that rely only on a single feedback mode or that do not optimally combine them.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides a unified statistical and algorithmic framework for best arm identification with mixed absolute and relative feedback in generalized linear bandits. By constructing a likelihood-ratio–based confidence sequence and an explicit confidence ellipsoid, and by adaptively tracking a minimax-optimal design over both arms and pairs, the proposed hybrid Track-and-Stop algorithm achieves δ-correctness with strong stopping-time guarantees. The cost-aware extension makes the framework applicable to settings with heterogeneous query costs. Experiments validate that intelligently combining multiple feedback modalities can substantially improve sample efficiency compared to existing methods.

title: Towards Emotion Consistency Analysis of Large Language Models in Emotional Conversational Contexts
summary: In this work, we conduct an analysis to examine the consistency of Large Language Models (LLMs) with respect to their own generated responses in an emotionally-driven conversational context. Specifically, the text generated by LLM is framed as a query to the same model, and its responses are subsequently assessed. This is performed with three queries across two dimensions of extreme and moderate emotions. The three queries are, in particular, false claim queries that contain inherently wrong assumptions (false presuppositions) in increasing order of intensity. Two commercial models, Claude-3.5-haiku, GPT4o-mini, and a medium-sized model, Mistral-7B, are considered in the study. Our findings indicate that LLMs exhibit below-average performance and remain vulnerable to false beliefs embedded within queries. This susceptibility is especially pronounced for moderate emotional content. Furthermore, an extended attention-score-based analysis highlights a shift in models' priority from evaluative to generative. The results raise important considerations for LLMs' deployment in high-stakes, emotionally sensitive contexts.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes how consistently large language models respond to their own emotionally framed outputs, especially when the prompts contain false assumptions, and finds notable vulnerabilities.
AI.motivation: To understand whether LLMs can reliably detect and resist false presuppositions in emotionally charged conversations, particularly when those presuppositions come from the model’s own prior outputs. This is crucial for safe use in high‑stakes, emotionally sensitive applications.
AI.method: The authors generate emotionally driven conversational text using LLMs, then feed that text back to the same model as a query. They construct three types of false-claim queries with increasing emotional intensity and consider two emotional dimensions: extreme and moderate. They test two commercial models (Claude-3.5-haiku, GPT4o-mini) and one medium-sized open model (Mistral-7B). They evaluate how the models handle the false presuppositions and perform an attention-score-based analysis to see how attention shifts between evaluative (checking truth) and generative (producing continuations) behavior.
AI.result: All tested LLMs show below-average robustness to false beliefs embedded in emotionally driven prompts, demonstrating susceptibility particularly in the case of moderate emotional content. Attention-score analysis suggests that, under these conditions, models’ attention shifts away from critical evaluation of the input toward generative continuation, exacerbating their failure to reject false presuppositions.
AI.conclusion: LLMs are inconsistent and vulnerable when confronted with emotionally framed prompts containing false assumptions, especially at moderate emotional intensity. Their internal attention patterns favor generation over evaluation in such contexts, raising concerns about deploying them in high-stakes scenarios that involve emotional users or content, and motivating the need for improved safeguards and training strategies.

title: Evaluating Explainability in Safety-Critical ATR Systems: Limitations of Post-Hoc Methods and Paths Toward Robust XAI
summary: Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) is increasingly rec ognized as essential for deploying machine learning systems in safety critical environments. In Automatic Target Recognition (ATR), where models operate on image, video, radar, and multisensor data, high pre dictive performance alone is insufficient. Model decisions must also be interpretable, reliable, and suitable for validation. This paper presents a structured evaluation of explainability methods in the context of safety-critical ATR systems: We identify major XAI paradigms, including saliency-based, attention-based, and surrogate ap proaches, as well as recent detection-aware extensions. Based on this, we formalize explainability as an assurance-oriented assessment problem, introduce a taxonomy, and assess these methods with respect to four key dimensions: interpretability, robustness, vulnerability to manipula tion, and suitability for validation and verification. The analysis identifies systematic limitations of current post-hoc explanation methods. In par ticular, we derive critical failure modes such as spurious explanations, instability under perturbations, and overtrust induced by visually con vincing outputs. These findings indicate that widely used XAI techniques may be insufficient for safety-critical deployment. Finally, we discuss implications for ATR systems and outline directions toward more robust, causally grounded, and physically informed explain ability methods. Our results emphasize the need to move beyond visually plausible explanations toward approaches that support reliable decision making and system-level assurance.

AI.tldr: The paper critically evaluates existing XAI methods for Automatic Target Recognition (ATR) in safety-critical settings and shows that many popular post-hoc techniques are not reliable enough for assurance and validation.
AI.motivation: In safety-critical ATR applications (e.g., using image, video, radar, multisensor data), high accuracy is not enough; decision-makers need explanations that are interpretable, robust, and verifiable. Current XAI methods are widely used but their adequacy for such stringent contexts is unclear, motivating a systematic assessment focused on assurance needs rather than only visual plausibility.
AI.method: The authors survey and categorize major XAI paradigms relevant to ATR: saliency-based, attention-based, surrogate models, and detection-aware extensions. They formalize explainability as an assurance-oriented evaluation problem and introduce a taxonomy. Using this framework, they analyze these XAI methods along four dimensions: interpretability, robustness, vulnerability to manipulation, and suitability for validation and verification, identifying typical behaviors and failure modes.
AI.result: The evaluation reveals systematic weaknesses in current post-hoc XAI methods, such as generating spurious or misleading explanations, high sensitivity to small input perturbations, and susceptibility to intentional or unintentional manipulation. Visually appealing explanations can create unjustified user trust, and many methods fail to meet the stringent requirements of safety-critical ATR, especially regarding robustness and verifiability.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that widely used XAI techniques are currently inadequate for safety-critical ATR deployment because they do not reliably support assurance and trustworthy decision-making. It advocates moving beyond visually plausible, heuristic explanations toward more robust, causally grounded, and physically informed methods that can be integrated into system-level assurance and validation processes, and outlines research directions toward achieving this.

title: Litespark Inference on Consumer CPUs: Custom SIMD Kernels for Ternary Neural Networks
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have transformed artificial intelligence, but their computational requirements remain prohibitive for most users. Standard inference demands expensive datacenter GPUs or cloud API access, leaving over one billion personal computers underutilized for AI workloads. Ternary models offer a path forward: their weights are constrained to {-1, 0, +1}, theoretically eliminating the need for floating-point multiplication. However, existing frameworks fail to exploit this structure, treating ternary models as dense floating-point networks. We address this gap with custom SIMD kernels that replace matrix multiplication with simple addition and subtraction operations, targeting the integer dot product instructions available on modern CPUs. Our implementation, Litespark-Inference, is pip-installable and integrates directly with Hugging-Face, achieving 9.2x faster time-to-first-token, 52x higher throughput, and 14x memory reduction compared to standard PyTorch inference on Apple Silicon, with similar speedups on Intel and AMD processors.

AI.tldr: The paper presents Litespark-Inference, a CPU-optimized inference engine for ternary large language models that dramatically speeds up and shrinks memory usage by exploiting integer SIMD instructions instead of floating-point math.
AI.motivation: Although LLMs are powerful, they are too computationally expensive for most personal computers, which leaves a vast amount of consumer hardware underutilized. Ternary models theoretically remove the need for floating-point multiplications, but existing frameworks do not exploit this advantage, treating them like dense floating-point models. There is a need to fully leverage ternary structure to make LLM inference efficient on commodity CPUs.
AI.method: The authors design custom SIMD kernels that map ternary weight operations to simple integer additions and subtractions, replacing standard matrix multiplications. They target modern CPU integer dot-product instructions and integrate these kernels into an inference framework, Litespark-Inference, that is pip-installable and compatible with Hugging Face models.
AI.result: On Apple Silicon CPUs, Litespark-Inference provides 9.2x faster time-to-first-token, 52x higher throughput, and a 14x reduction in memory usage compared with standard PyTorch inference. Similar performance gains are observed on Intel and AMD processors, demonstrating broad applicability.
AI.conclusion: By exploiting the discrete {-1, 0, +1} structure of ternary models using custom integer SIMD kernels, Litespark-Inference makes LLM inference dramatically more efficient on commodity CPUs. This approach enables practical deployment of powerful language models on over a billion personal computers without reliance on datacenter GPUs or cloud APIs.

title: Confidence is the key: how conformal prediction enhances the generative design of permeable peptides
summary: Generative models coupled with reinforcement learning (RL), such as REINVENT and PepINVENT, have emerged as a powerful framework for de novo molecular design. During the ideation process these generative frameworks utilize various predictive models as part of the optimization objectives. However, the utility of the predictive models can be limited by their domain of applicability. When RL is used to explore the chemical space with predictive models, it can suggest molecules that lie outside the predictor's domain of applicability. As a result, the predictions may become less reliable, potentially steering designs into high reward but also high uncertainty chemical spaces. This is particularly pronounced for cyclic peptides which show therapeutic promise due to their modifiability and large interaction surfaces but are understudied compared to small molecules. While passive membrane permeation in cyclic peptides has attracted interest, identifying optimal permeable designs remains challenging yet crucial for targeting intracellular sites. We present an RL-guided generative framework that designs permeable cyclic peptides using an uncertainty-aware permeability predictor as the scoring component. To address predictive uncertainty, especially impacted by novel chemistry, we integrate conformal prediction (CP) as our uncertainty quantification method. CP assesses designs based on the calibrated model under a user-defined confidence level. We demonstrate that rewarding generated peptides with CP-informed predictions improves both reliability and efficiency of peptide optimization process. This also discourages exploration outside the predictor's applicability domain. This approach bridges the gap between predictive uncertainty and RL-guided exploration, showing how generative modelling and conformal prediction can be combined for the first time.

AI.tldr: They build an RL-driven generative model for cyclic peptide design that directly incorporates calibrated uncertainty from a conformal-prediction-based permeability model, yielding more reliable, efficient, and in-domain proposals for permeable cyclic peptides.
AI.motivation: Generative RL models like REINVENT and PepINVENT optimize molecules using predictive models, but often push into chemical space where these predictors are unreliable, especially for underexplored modalities such as cyclic peptides. For properties like passive membrane permeability, this out-of-domain exploration yields high-scoring but actually uncertain and potentially incorrect designs. There is a need for a framework that explicitly accounts for predictive uncertainty and domain of applicability during RL-guided molecular design.
AI.method: They develop a reinforcement-learning-based generative framework for cyclic peptides that uses a permeability predictor augmented with conformal prediction (CP) for uncertainty quantification. CP provides calibrated prediction intervals or confidence measures under a user-selected confidence level. The RL reward is constructed to favor peptides with high predicted permeability that also lie within the model’s domain of applicability, as indicated by CP, and to penalize uncertain, out-of-domain proposals. This embeds uncertainty awareness directly into the optimization loop.
AI.result: Using this uncertainty-aware reward, the framework generates cyclic peptides that are more reliably permeable according to the calibrated predictor, while reducing exploration into regions of high prediction uncertainty. The optimization process becomes more efficient (e.g., faster convergence, higher proportion of high-quality designs) compared with RL that ignores uncertainty. The generated designs better respect the permeability model’s domain of applicability.
AI.conclusion: Integrating conformal prediction with RL-guided generative models offers a principled way to manage predictive uncertainty in de novo molecular design. For cyclic peptide permeability, this leads to more trustworthy and efficient optimization and discourages unrealistic, out-of-domain suggestions. The work demonstrates, apparently for the first time, a practical combination of generative modeling and CP, and suggests a general strategy for uncertainty-aware molecular design in other modalities and properties.

title: The Frequency Confound in Language-Model Surprisal and Metaphor Novelty
comment: to be presented and published at the 15th Joint Conference on Lexical and Computational Semantics (*SEM 2026)
summary: Language-model (LM) surprisal is widely used as a proxy for contextual predictability and has been reported to correlate with metaphor novelty judgments. However, surprisal is tightly intertwined with lexical frequency. We explore this interaction on metaphor novelty ratings using two different word frequency measures. We analyse surprisal estimates from eight Pythia model sizes and 154 training checkpoints. Across settings, word frequency is a stronger predictor of metaphor novelty than surprisal. Across training stages, the surprisal--novelty association peaks at an early stage and then falls again, mirroring a similarly timed increase in the surprisal--frequency association. These results suggest that the often-reported optimal LM surprisal settings may incorrectly associate contextual predictability with metaphor novelty and processing difficulty, whereas lexical frequency may be the major underlying factor.

AI.tldr: The paper examines how language-model surprisal and lexical frequency each predict human judgments of metaphor novelty, finding that word frequency is generally a stronger predictor and that the surprisal–novelty link is confounded by frequency and LM training stage.
AI.motivation: Researchers often use language-model surprisal as a stand-in for contextual predictability and processing difficulty, and prior work claimed that surprisal correlates with metaphor novelty. However, surprisal is mathematically and empirically linked to lexical frequency, so it is unclear whether surprisal effects on metaphor processing are genuinely about context-driven predictability or are mostly driven by how common the words are. This paper aims to disentangle these factors and to test how robust the surprisal–novelty relationship is across frequency measures, model sizes, and training stages.
AI.method: The authors take human metaphor novelty ratings and regress them on two predictors derived from large language models: (1) token-level surprisal estimates from eight sizes of the Pythia model family, each observed at 154 checkpoints spanning the training process, and (2) two distinct word frequency measures. They then perform statistical analyses comparing how well surprisal vs. frequency account for metaphor novelty judgments, and they track how the correlations between surprisal, frequency, and novelty evolve over training time.
AI.result: Across all tested model sizes and training checkpoints, lexical frequency consistently explains more variance in metaphor novelty ratings than LM surprisal does. The correlation between surprisal and metaphor novelty is not stable: it is strongest early in training but declines later, while the correlation between surprisal and lexical frequency grows over roughly the same period. This suggests that as the model learns, surprisals increasingly reflect word frequency, and any early surprisal–novelty effect is progressively absorbed by this frequency dependence.
AI.conclusion: The findings challenge the common practice of interpreting LM surprisal as a pure measure of contextual predictability, especially in studies of metaphor processing. Much of what has been attributed to surprisal in predicting metaphor novelty and processing difficulty may instead be driven by lexical frequency. Researchers should carefully control for frequency and be cautious about selecting particular training stages or model configurations that may artifactually maximize surprisal–behavior correlations.

title: CircuitFormer: A Circuit Language Model for Analog Topology Design from Natural Language Prompt
summary: Automating analog circuit design remains a longstanding challenge in Electronic Design Automation (EDA). While Transformer-based Large Language Models (LLMs) have revolutionized software code generation, their application to analog hardware design is hindered by two critical limitations: (i) the scarcity of analog design datasets containing natural language description of a design and its corresponding netlist, and (ii) the inefficiency of general-purpose tokenizers (e.g., Byte Pair Encoding (BPE)) in capturing the inherent graph structure of circuits. To bridge this gap, first, we curate the largest annotated dataset of analog circuit netlists to date, comprising 31,341 netlist-natural language description pairs across all major circuit classes. Furthermore, we propose Circuit Tokenizer (CKT), a novel circuit graph tokenizer designed to encode netlist connectivity by explicitly mining frequent subcircuits. In terms of scalability, CKT overcomes the bottleneck of prior circuit graph serialization methods where vocabulary size scales linearly with maximum number of components in the dataset, n_max, (O(n_max)); instead, CKT decouples vocabulary growth from circuit complexity, achieving a constant O(1) complexity. Empirically, CKT outperforms standard BPE on circuit topology representation, reducing sequence length by 57% and achieving a 2.3x superior compression ratio using a compact, fixed vocabulary of size 512. Leveraging this optimized tokenization, we train a circuit-specific language model, CircuitFormer, a 511M parameter encoder-decoder transformer. Our model achieves 100% syntactic correctness and an 83% functional success rate across all major analog circuit categories, outperforming state-of-the-art open-source LLMs by 10% and 14%, respectively, while requiring 240x fewer parameters. The dataset is publicly available at https://huggingface.co/datasets/touhid314/cktformer-dataset.

AI.tldr: They create a large analog circuit netlist–text dataset and a specialized circuit tokenizer (CKT) that better captures graph structure, then train a compact transformer (CircuitFormer) that strongly outperforms much larger general LLMs on analog circuit generation.
AI.motivation: Automating analog circuit design is hard and current LLMs are ineffective because there are too few paired circuit–text datasets and standard tokenizers like BPE do not respect the graph-structured nature of circuits, leading to inefficient representations and poor performance.
AI.method: 1) Curate a dataset of 31,341 analog circuit netlists paired with natural language descriptions across major circuit classes. 2) Design Circuit Tokenizer (CKT), a tokenizer that mines frequent subcircuits and encodes netlist connectivity directly, decoupling vocabulary size from maximum circuit size (O(1) instead of O(n_max)). 3) Empirically compare CKT to BPE in terms of sequence length and compression. 4) Train CircuitFormer, a 511M-parameter encoder–decoder transformer specialized for circuits using CKT tokens, and evaluate syntactic and functional correctness of generated circuits against open-source LLM baselines.
AI.result: CKT achieves better circuit topology representation than BPE, with 57% shorter sequences and a 2.3× higher compression ratio using a fixed vocabulary of 512 tokens. CircuitFormer, trained with CKT, achieves 100% syntactic correctness and 83% functional success across major analog circuit categories, beating state-of-the-art open-source LLMs by 10% in syntactic correctness and 14% in functional success, while using 240× fewer parameters.
AI.conclusion: Specialized, graph-aware tokenization tailored to analog circuits plus a domain-specific transformer enables far more efficient and accurate analog circuit generation than general-purpose LLMs. The released dataset and models provide a scalable foundation for future EDA tools that integrate language-based circuit design automation.

title: STALE: Can LLM Agents Know When Their Memories Are No Longer Valid?
summary: Large Language Model (LLM) agents are increasingly expected to maintain coherent, long-term personalized memory, yet current benchmarks primarily measure static fact retrieval, overlooking the ability to revise stored beliefs when new evidence emerges. We identify a critical and underexplored failure mode, Implicit Conflict: a later observation invalidates an earlier memory without explicit negation, requiring contextual inference and commonsense reasoning to detect. To rigorously evaluate this capability, we introduce STALE, a benchmark of 400 expert-validated conflict scenarios (1,200 evaluation queries across three probing dimensions) spanning over 100 everyday topics with contexts up to 150K tokens. We propose a three-dimensional probing framework that tests State Resolution (detecting that a prior belief is outdated), Premise Resistance (rejecting queries that falsely presuppose a stale state), and Implicit Policy Adaptation (proactively applying updated states in downstream behavior). A systematic evaluation of frontier LLMs and specialized memory frameworks reveals a pervasive gap between retrieving updated evidence and acting on it, with even the best evaluated model achieving only 55.2% overall accuracy. Models often accept outdated assumptions embedded in a user's query, and they struggle to recognize when a change in one aspect of the user's state should invalidate related memories. To establish an initial baseline for state-aware memory, we further present CUPMem, a prototype that strengthens write-time revision through structured state consolidation and propagation-aware search, suggesting that explicit state adjudication is a promising direction for robust agentic memory.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces STALE, a benchmark to test whether LLM agents can detect and update implicit conflicts in long-term memory, and proposes CUPMem as a prototype memory system that better revises and propagates user state updates.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM benchmarks mainly assess static fact retrieval and explicit contradiction handling, but real agents must maintain and revise long-term personalized memory as user states change. A critical missing capability is handling implicit conflicts, where new information invalidates old beliefs without explicit negation. The paper aims to systematically characterize and evaluate this underexplored failure mode in LLMs and memory systems.
AI.method: The authors define the notion of Implicit Conflict and design STALE, a benchmark of 400 expert-validated scenarios (1,200 queries) across 100+ everyday topics with very long contexts (up to 150K tokens). They create a three-dimensional probing framework measuring: (1) State Resolution—detecting outdated beliefs; (2) Premise Resistance—rejecting queries that assume stale states; and (3) Implicit Policy Adaptation—using updated states in downstream behavior. They then evaluate several frontier LLMs and specialized memory frameworks on these tasks. Finally, they design CUPMem, a prototype memory system that emphasizes write-time revision via structured state consolidation and propagation-aware retrieval/search, and test it as an initial baseline on STALE.
AI.result: Across models and memory frameworks, performance is poor, with the best model achieving only 55.2% accuracy on STALE. Systems can often retrieve the latest evidence but still act on outdated assumptions in user queries. They also fail to see when a change in one aspect of user state should trigger revision of related memories. CUPMem improves state-aware behavior relative to naive baselines, demonstrating benefits of explicit state consolidation and propagation-aware search, though it still leaves substantial room for improvement.
AI.conclusion: Current LLM agents and memory systems are not reliably state-aware: they struggle to detect and resolve implicit conflicts in long-term memory, resist stale premises in user queries, and propagate state changes to related beliefs. STALE exposes these weaknesses in a structured way, and the CUPMem prototype suggests that explicit state adjudication and structured memory revision are promising directions. The work positions implicit conflict handling as a key challenge for building robust, long-lived LLM agents.

title: HEDP: A Hybrid Energy-Distance Prompt-based Framework for Domain Incremental Learning
summary: Domain Incremental Learning is a critical scenario that requires models to continuously adapt to new data domains without retraining. However, domain shifts often cause severe performance degradation. To address this, we propose Hybrid Energy-Distance Prompt, a domain-incremental framework inspired by Helmholtz free energy. HEDP introduces an energy regularization loss to enhance the separability of domain representations and a hybrid energy-distance weighted mechanism that fuses energy-based and distance-based cues to improve domain selection and generalization. Experiments on multiple benchmarks, including CORe50, show that HEDP achieves superior performance on unseen domains with a 2.57\% accuracy gain, effectively mitigating catastrophic forgetting and enhancing open-world adaptability. Our code is \href{https://github.com/dannis97500/HEDP/}{available here}.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes HEDP, a Helmholtz-energy-inspired prompt framework for domain incremental learning that improves performance and reduces forgetting across shifting domains.
AI.motivation: Domain Incremental Learning requires models to adapt to sequentially arriving domains without retraining, but domain shifts cause severe performance drops and catastrophic forgetting. Existing methods struggle to maintain performance on unseen domains and to select suitable domain representations in an open-world setting. The paper aims to better handle domain shifts and improve generalization to unseen domains while mitigating forgetting.
AI.method: The authors introduce Hybrid Energy-Distance Prompt (HEDP), a domain-incremental framework inspired by Helmholtz free energy. HEDP adds (1) an energy regularization loss that encourages better separability of domain representations, and (2) a hybrid energy-distance weighted mechanism that combines energy-based cues (e.g., model confidence/energy scores) and distance-based cues (e.g., feature similarity) for domain selection and prediction. The prompts are adapted across domains, and the hybrid weighting is used to choose or weight domain-specific components during inference.
AI.result: On multiple benchmarks, including CORe50, HEDP outperforms existing methods on unseen domains, with a reported 2.57% accuracy improvement. It also better preserves past-domain performance, indicating reduced catastrophic forgetting and stronger open-world adaptability.
AI.conclusion: HEDP successfully leverages an energy-inspired regularization and a hybrid energy-distance mechanism to improve domain selection, representation separability, and generalization in Domain Incremental Learning. The method yields higher accuracy on unseen domains and mitigates catastrophic forgetting, making it suitable for open-world scenarios. Code is publicly available for reproducibility.

title: Efficient Pre-Training with Token Superposition
summary: Pre-training of Large Language Models is often prohibitively expensive and inefficient at scale, requiring complex and invasive modifications in order to achieve high data throughput. In this work, we present Token-Superposition Training (TST), a simple drop-in method that significantly improves the data throughput per FLOPs during pre-training without modifying the parallelism, optimizer, tokenizer, data, or model architecture. TST is done in two phases: (i) A highly efficient superposition phase where we combine many contiguous tokens into one bag and train using a multi-hot cross-entropy (MCE) objective, and (ii) a recovery phase where we revert back to standard training. We extensively evaluate TST on the scale of 270M and 600M parameters and validate on 3B and a 10B A1B mixture of experts model, demonstrating that it is highly robust in different settings. Ultimately, TST consistently outperforms baseline loss and downstream evaluations, and under equal-loss settings, TST yields up to a 2.5x reduction in total pre-training time at the 10B A1B scale.

AI.tldr: They propose Token-Superposition Training (TST), a simple two-phase method that speeds up LLM pre-training and improves efficiency without changing model architecture or training stack.
AI.motivation: Standard pre-training of LLMs is extremely expensive and often bottlenecked by data throughput, and existing efficiency techniques usually require complex and invasive system or algorithmic changes. The authors want a method that increases data-throughput-per-FLOP in a simple, drop-in way.
AI.method: Introduce Token-Superposition Training (TST), which has two phases. In the initial superposition phase, multiple contiguous tokens are combined into a single bag representation and trained with a multi-hot cross-entropy (MCE) objective, effectively predicting a bag of tokens rather than a single token. After this efficient phase, a recovery phase returns to standard token-level training. The approach does not alter model architecture, tokenizer, optimizer, data pipeline, or parallelism strategies.
AI.result: On 270M and 600M parameter models, and validated on 3B and 10B A1B MoE models, TST shows robustness across scales. It consistently improves pre-training loss and downstream task performance over baselines while significantly increasing training efficiency; at comparable loss levels, it achieves up to 2.5x reduction in total pre-training time for the 10B A1B model.
AI.conclusion: TST is an effective, robust, and easy-to-adopt method to accelerate LLM pre-training. By using a token-superposition phase with a multi-hot objective followed by a standard recovery phase, it can yield substantial time savings (up to 2.5x at 10B scale) without invasive changes to existing training setups, while also improving model quality.

title: Von Neumann Networks
summary: In the mid-twentieth century, mathematician and polymath John von Neumann created a computational system on an array of cells as a simple model of the human brain, where each cell had one of a finite set of roles or states that he predicted would be modelled by a diffusion process. In this work, we show that such a system, when developed in a modern deep learning setting, enables the construction of an artificial neuron having specialized roles that can be learnt. We refer to this neuron as the Von Neumann neuron, and the resulting neural network from such neurons result in a self-engineered design whose architecture is only dependent on the structure and locations of its inputs and outputs on this cellular array. The mathematical framework for these Von Neumann Networks (VNNs) is also constructed and shows that they are based on the extension of neural operators and the learning of Green's functions with convolutions on a cellular topology having a diffusion signature. We also prove that these VNNs are part of a more general computational system called Cellular Machines that are computationally universal. Initial experiments show that VNN based multi-layered perceptrons outperform their equivalent deep learning variant on basic tasks, while being more parameter efficient and are capable of learning new types of tasks. This includes the ability to solve for and construct an extension of the Von Neumann (hardware) architecture common to all modern computers to cells and suggests new opportunities that could be explored.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Von Neumann Networks (VNNs), neural architectures built from specialized "Von Neumann neurons" arranged on a cellular array with diffusion-like connectivity, showing improved performance and parameter efficiency over standard MLPs and establishing a general framework called Cellular Machines that is computationally universal.
AI.motivation: To revisit John von Neumann’s mid-20th century idea of modelling brain-like computation via an array of cells with finite roles, and to modernize it within deep learning to obtain self-organizing neural architectures that are more efficient, more flexible, and theoretically better grounded than standard neural networks.
AI.method: The authors construct a new neuron type—the Von Neumann neuron—embedded in a cellular array where roles/states diffuse over the topology. They formalize Von Neumann Networks (VNNs) via an extension of neural operators and learning of Green’s functions implemented as convolutions with a diffusion-like signature across the cellular topology. They also define a broader framework of Cellular Machines and prove their computational universality. Empirical comparisons are performed between VNN-based MLPs and conventional deep learning MLPs on basic tasks.
AI.result: VNN-based multilayer perceptrons outperform equivalent standard deep learning MLPs on basic benchmark tasks while using fewer parameters. VNNs also demonstrate the capacity to learn novel types of tasks, including solving for and constructing a generalized Von Neumann hardware architecture on the cellular array.
AI.conclusion: Von Neumann Networks, built from learned, role-specialized neurons on a cellular diffusion topology, provide a self-engineered neural architecture that is both theoretically powerful (universal computation via Cellular Machines) and practically advantageous (better accuracy and parameter efficiency than standard MLPs). They also open possibilities for new computational paradigms by extending classic Von Neumann hardware concepts to cell-based architectures.

title: Continuous Latent Diffusion Language Model
comment: 99 pages, 31 figures, 9 tables. Project page: https://hongcanguo.github.io/Cola-DLM/
summary: Large language models have achieved remarkable success under the autoregressive paradigm, yet high-quality text generation need not be tied to a fixed left-to-right order. Existing alternatives still struggle to jointly achieve generation efficiency, scalable representation learning, and effective global semantic modeling. We propose Cola DLM, a hierarchical latent diffusion language model that frames text generation through hierarchical information decomposition. Cola DLM first learns a stable text-to-latent mapping with a Text VAE, then models a global semantic prior in continuous latent space with a block-causal DiT, and finally generates text through conditional decoding. From a unified Markov-path perspective, its diffusion process performs latent prior transport rather than token-level observation recovery, thereby separating global semantic organization from local textual realization. This design yields a more flexible non-autoregressive inductive bias, supports semantic compression and prior fitting in continuous space, and naturally extends to other continuous modalities. Through experiments spanning 4 research questions, 8 benchmarks, strictly matched ~2B-parameter autoregressive and LLaDA baselines, and scaling curves up to about 2000 EFLOPs, we identify an effective overall configuration of Cola DLM and verify its strong scaling behavior for text generation. Taken together, the results establish hierarchical continuous latent prior modeling as a principled alternative to strictly token-level language modeling, where generation quality and scaling behavior may better reflect model capability than likelihood, while also suggesting a concrete path toward unified modeling across discrete text and continuous modalities.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Cola DLM, a hierarchical latent diffusion language model that generates text by modeling global semantics in continuous latent space instead of token-by-token autoregression, demonstrating competitive or better scaling and generation quality compared to similarly sized autoregressive baselines.
AI.motivation: Autoregressive language models generate text in a fixed left-to-right order, which can limit flexibility, global semantic coherence, and efficiency. Existing non-autoregressive or alternative order methods have not simultaneously achieved efficient generation, strong representation learning, and robust global semantic modeling. The authors aim to design a principled alternative that decouples global semantic planning from local surface realization and can naturally extend beyond text to other continuous modalities.
AI.method: The authors propose Cola DLM, a hierarchical latent diffusion language model. First, they train a Text VAE to learn a stable mapping from text to a continuous latent space and back (text-to-latent and latent-to-text). Second, they learn a global semantic prior in this latent space using a block-causal Diffusion Transformer (DiT), which applies a diffusion process over continuous latents rather than over tokens. From a Markov-path viewpoint, the diffusion process is used to transport the latent prior, separating the modeling of global semantics from local textual realization. Finally, text is generated by sampling from the learned latent prior and decoding it conditionally back into text. The model introduces a hierarchical decomposition of information and a non-autoregressive inductive bias, and the authors systematically explore design choices and scaling behavior using controlled experiments and parameter-matched baselines.
AI.result: Empirically, Cola DLM is evaluated on 8 benchmarks addressing 4 research questions, with strictly matched ~2B-parameter autoregressive and LLaDA baselines. Across experiments and scaling up to about 2000 EFLOPs, Cola DLM demonstrates strong scaling behavior for text generation and competitive or improved generation quality. The model shows that hierarchical continuous latent prior modeling can be trained effectively at scale and that generation quality and scaling trends can outperform what is suggested by conventional likelihood-based evaluation for token-level models.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that hierarchical continuous latent prior modeling via a latent diffusion language model is a principled and effective alternative to strictly token-level autoregressive language modeling. By decoupling global semantic organization from local text realization and operating in a continuous latent space, Cola DLM achieves flexible non-autoregressive generation, supports semantic compression, and scales well. The findings suggest that generation quality and scaling behavior may be more indicative of model capability than likelihood alone and point toward a viable path for unified modeling across discrete text and continuous modalities using shared latent-diffusion frameworks.

title: Sheet as Token: A Graph-Enhanced Representation for Multi-Sheet Spreadsheet Understanding
summary: Workbook-scale spreadsheet understanding is increasingly important for language-model-based data analysis agents, but remains challenging because relevant information is often distributed across multiple sheets with heterogeneous schemas, layouts, and implicit relationships. Existing retrieval-augmented approaches typically decompose spreadsheets into rows, columns, or blocks to improve scalability; however, such chunk-centric representations can fragment worksheets into isolated text spans and weaken global sheet-level semantics. We propose Sheet as Token, a graph-enhanced framework that treats each worksheet as a unified semantic unit for multi-sheet spreadsheet retrieval. Our method extracts schema-aware records from sheet names, column headers, representative values, and layout features, and encodes each worksheet into a compact dense token. Given a natural-language query, a Graph Retriever constructs a query-specific candidate graph over sheet tokens using semantic, query-conditioned, schema-consistency, and shape-compatibility relations, and composes these channels through a multi-stage graph transformer to retrieve supporting sheet sets. Experiments on a constructed multi-sheet spreadsheet corpus show that sheet-level tokenization learns stable representations, and that graph-enhanced cross-sheet reasoning improves listwise retrieval over a shallow graph baseline with limited additional graph-side computation. These results suggest that sheet-level tokenization is a promising abstraction for scalable multi-sheet spreadsheet understanding.

AI.tldr: They introduce a new way to understand entire workbooks by treating each worksheet as a single dense token and using a graph-based retriever to select relevant sheets for a query.
AI.motivation: Language-model-based agents need to understand complex spreadsheets where information is spread across many heterogeneous sheets, but current retrieval methods break sheets into small chunks (rows/columns/blocks), which loses global sheet semantics and makes multi-sheet reasoning harder.
AI.method: Treat each worksheet as a unified semantic token by extracting schema-aware records from sheet names, headers, representative values, and layout features, then encode these into dense vectors. For a natural-language query, construct a query-specific graph over sheet tokens using several relation types (semantic similarity, query conditioning, schema consistency, and shape compatibility). Apply a multi-stage graph transformer (Graph Retriever) over this graph to reason across sheets and retrieve the most relevant sheet sets.
AI.result: On a custom multi-sheet spreadsheet corpus, the sheet-as-token representation yields stable sheet embeddings, and the graph-enhanced reasoning notably improves listwise retrieval performance compared to a shallow graph baseline, while adding only modest extra graph-side computation.
AI.conclusion: Representing worksheets as single semantic tokens and using graph-based cross-sheet reasoning is an effective and scalable abstraction for multi-sheet spreadsheet retrieval, making it promising for workbook-scale spreadsheet understanding in LLM-based data agents.

title: Long Context Pre-Training with Lighthouse Attention
summary: Training causal transformers at extreme sequence lengths is bottlenecked by the quadratic time and memory of scaled dot-product attention (SDPA). In this work, we propose Lighthouse Attention, a training-only symmetrical selection-based hierarchical attention algorithm that wraps around ordinary SDPA and can be easily removed towards the end of the training. Our hierarchical selection is also gradient-free, which exempts us from dealing with a complicated and potentially inefficient backward pass kernel. Our contribution is three-fold: (i) A subquadratic hierarchical pre- and post-processing step that does adaptive compression and decompression of the sequence. (ii) A symmetrical compression strategy that pools queries, keys and values at the same time, while preserving left-to-right causality, which greatly improves parallelism. (iii) A two stage training approach which we pre-train for the majority of the time with Lighthouse Attention and recover a full attention model at the end with a short training. We run preliminary small scale LLM pre-training experiments that show the effectiveness of our method compared to full attention training with all other settings matched, where we achieve a faster total training time and lower final loss after the recovery phase. Full code is available at: https://github.com/ighoshsubho/lighthouse-attention

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Lighthouse Attention, a training-time hierarchical selection mechanism around standard attention that reduces quadratic costs for very long sequences, then switches back to full attention for final fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Standard scaled dot-product attention has quadratic time and memory complexity in sequence length, making training causal transformers at extreme sequence lengths very expensive or impossible with current hardware.
