The authors want a way to speed up and scale training without permanently changing the attention mechanism at inference time or requiring complex backward passes.
AI.method: They design Lighthouse Attention, which wraps around ordinary SDPA with a subquadratic hierarchical pre-processing (compression) and post-processing (decompression) of the sequence. The procedure symmetrically compresses queries, keys, and values while preserving left-to-right causality, enabling more parallelism. The selection/compression mechanism is gradient-free, avoiding the need to backpropagate through it. Training is done in two stages: first, a long pre-training phase using Lighthouse Attention for efficiency, then a short recovery phase where the model is trained again with full attention after removing Lighthouse, yielding a standard transformer for deployment.
AI.result: Preliminary small-scale language-model pre-training experiments show that, under matched settings, models trained mostly with Lighthouse Attention and then briefly with full attention reach lower final loss and require less total training time than models trained with full attention throughout.
AI.conclusion: Lighthouse Attention can substantially reduce the computational cost of training transformers at extreme sequence lengths while still producing a final model that uses standard full attention. Its hierarchical, gradient-free, symmetrical compression strategy offers practical efficiency gains without sacrificing downstream performance, at least in the tested small-scale setups.

title: Long-Horizon Q-Learning: Accurate Value Learning via n-Step Inequalities
summary: Off-policy, value-based reinforcement learning methods such as Q-learning are appealing because they can learn from arbitrary experience, including data collected by older policies or other agents. In practice, however, bootstrapping makes long-horizon learning brittle: estimation errors at later states propagate backward through temporal-difference (TD) updates and can compound over time. We propose long-horizon Q-learning (LQL), which introduces a principled backstop against compounding error when learning the optimal action-value function. LQL builds on a prior optimality tightening observation: any realized action sequence lower-bounds what the optimal policy can achieve in expectation, so acting optimally earlier should not be worse than following the observed actions for several steps before switching to optimal behavior. Our contribution is to turn this inequality into a practical stabilization mechanism for Q-learning by using a hinge loss to penalize violations of these bounds. Importantly, LQL computes these penalties using network outputs already produced for the TD error, requiring no auxiliary networks and no additional forward passes relative to Q-learning. When combined with multiple state-of-the-art methods on a range of online and offline-to-online benchmarks, LQL consistently outperforms both 1-step TD and n-step TD learning at similar runtime.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Long-horizon Q-Learning (LQL), a modification of Q-learning that stabilizes long-horizon learning by enforcing lower-bound constraints derived from observed action sequences, improving performance over standard TD methods without extra computation.
AI.motivation: Standard off-policy, value-based RL methods like Q-learning can in principle learn from arbitrary data, but in long-horizon problems they become brittle because bootstrapped TD targets propagate estimation errors backward over many steps, causing compounding error and unstable learning. There is a need for a principled and computationally efficient way to control or reduce this compounding error while retaining the benefits of off-policy, value-based learning.
AI.method: The paper builds on the optimality tightening idea that the return from any realized action sequence provides a lower bound on what an optimal policy can achieve from the starting state, because acting optimally earlier cannot be worse than first following the observed actions then switching to optimal behavior. They convert this inequality into a practical Q-learning stabilization mechanism by adding a hinge loss term that penalizes Q-values which violate these lower-bound constraints. Crucially, the necessary quantities for the penalties are computed using the same network outputs already used for the TD error, so no extra networks or forward passes are required compared to standard Q-learning. This yields Long-horizon Q-Learning (LQL), which can be plugged into existing value-based RL algorithms.
AI.result: Across a range of online and offline-to-online RL benchmarks, and when combined with multiple state-of-the-art methods, LQL consistently achieves better performance than both standard 1-step TD learning and n-step TD learning, while maintaining similar runtime and computational cost.
AI.conclusion: Enforcing lower-bound constraints derived from realized trajectories via a hinge loss provides an effective backstop against compounding bootstrapping error in long-horizon Q-learning. LQL offers a simple, computationally cheap modification that stabilizes and improves value-based off-policy RL, outperforming standard TD approaches without adding architectural or runtime complexity.

title: Automated Clinical Report Generation for Remote Cognitive Remediation: Comparing Knowledge-Engineered Templates and LLMs in Low-Resource Settings
summary: The growing demand for cognitive remediation therapy, combined with limited speech therapist availability, has accelerated the adoption of remote rehabilitation tools. These systems generate large volumes of interaction data that are difficult for clinicians to review efficiently. This paper investigates automated clinical report generation for avatar-guided, home-based cognitive remediation sessions in a low-resource setting with no reference reports. We present and compare two approaches: (1) a rule-based template system encoding speech therapy domain knowledge as explicit decision rules and validated templates, ensuring clinical reliability and traceability; and (2) a zero-shot LLM-based approach (GPT-4) aimed at more fluent and concise output. Both systems use identical pre-extracted, expert-validated structured variables, enabling a controlled factual comparison. Outputs were evaluated by eight speech therapists and final-year students using a nine-criterion questionnaire. Results reveal a clear trade-off between clinical reliability and linguistic quality. The template-based system scored higher on fluidity, coherence, and results presentation, while GPT-4 produced more concise output. Directional differences are consistent across evaluation dimensions, though no comparison reached statistical significance after correction, reflecting the scale constraints of expert clinical evaluation. Based on evaluator feedback, we derive eight design recommendations for clinical reporting systems in remote rehabilitation settings. More broadly, this work contributes a replicable methodology combining expert elicitation, taxonomy-driven generation, and multi-dimensional human evaluation for clinical NLG in low-resource settings, and illustrates how controlled comparisons can inform the responsible adoption of generative AI in healthcare.

AI.tldr: The paper compares a rule-based template system and a zero-shot GPT-4 approach for automatically generating clinical reports from structured data of remote cognitive remediation sessions, analyzing the trade-off between clinical reliability and linguistic quality and deriving design recommendations for clinical NLG in low-resource healthcare settings.
AI.motivation: Remote, avatar-guided cognitive remediation is expanding due to therapist shortages, generating large amounts of interaction data that clinicians cannot easily review. In low-resource settings, there are no existing reference reports to train or benchmark data-driven NLG systems. There is a need for automated clinical report generation methods that are both clinically reliable and linguistically effective, and for a replicable methodology to evaluate such systems responsibly in healthcare contexts.
AI.method: The authors build two NLG systems that take the same set of pre-extracted, expert-validated structured variables from home-based cognitive remediation sessions. System 1 is a rule-based template system encoding speech therapy domain knowledge via explicit decision rules and validated text templates, prioritizing traceability and reliability. System 2 is a zero-shot GPT-4 configuration prompted to generate concise, fluent reports from the same structured inputs, without fine-tuning or reference reports. Eight speech therapists and advanced students evaluate outputs from both systems using a nine-criterion questionnaire assessing clinical and linguistic aspects. Statistical analyses are conducted to compare scores, and qualitative feedback is used to formulate design recommendations.
AI.result: The evaluation shows a systematic trade-off: the rule-based template system achieves better scores on several linguistic dimensions (fluidity, coherence, and clarity of results presentation) and is inherently more traceable and clinically reliable, while the GPT-4 approach yields more concise reports but does not surpass the template system on the main quality metrics. Directional differences between systems are consistent across all nine criteria, but none reach statistical significance after multiple-comparison correction, likely due to the small expert sample size and evaluation scale limitations. The feedback enables the authors to formulate eight concrete design recommendations for clinical report generation tools in remote rehabilitation.
AI.conclusion: Automated clinical report generation for remote cognitive remediation is feasible using structured interaction data, but there is a clear trade-off between clinical reliability and linguistic conciseness/expressiveness. In this low-resource, no-reference setting, a carefully engineered, taxonomy-driven template system can match or outperform a zero-shot GPT-4 setup on several linguistic criteria while offering higher trustworthiness. The paper proposes a general, replicable methodology integrating expert knowledge elicitation, controlled system comparison, and multi-dimensional human evaluation for clinical NLG. This framework can guide the cautious and responsible adoption of generative AI in healthcare reporting, especially where data and expert evaluation resources are limited.

title: AGPO: Asymmetric Group Policy Optimization for Verifiable Reasoning and Search Ads Relevance at JD
summary: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has demonstrated notable success in enhancing the reasoning performance of large language models (LLMs). However, recent studies reveal that while current RLVR methods improve sampling efficiency towards correct paths, they do not elicit fundamentally new reasoning patterns. Instead, the reasoning capability boundary of trained models often narrows compared to their base models, with base models achieving higher coverage at large sample sizes. In this work, we propose Asymmetric Group Policy Optimization (AGPO) to counteract this boundary shrinkage. AGPO adopts a negative-dominant reinforcement strategy to suppress incorrect reasoning paths, maintaining the base model's exploration capacity. For positive reinforcement, AGPO adopts a group advantage mechanism, which scales positive updates based on intra-group variance, allowing the model to focus on rare correct paths while suppressing updates from trivial paths. Our experiments on five mathematical benchmarks demonstrate that AGPO achieves state-of-the-art accuracy while consistently improving pass@$k$ performance at scale. In a large-scale industrial application for search ads relevance optimization, AGPO effectively enhances the quality of the data annotation, leading to substantial performance gains in downstream student models.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Asymmetric Group Policy Optimization (AGPO), a reinforcement learning method for LLMs that prevents reasoning capability shrinkage while improving accuracy and pass@k via negative-dominant penalties and variance-based group rewards.
AI.motivation: Existing RL with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) methods for LLM reasoning improve efficiency but do not create new reasoning patterns and often narrow the effective reasoning boundary compared to the base model, especially at large sample sizes, where base models can outperform fine-tuned ones in coverage. The authors want a method that preserves exploration and broad reasoning capability while still exploiting verifiable rewards.
AI.method: They propose Asymmetric Group Policy Optimization (AGPO). On the negative side, it uses a negative-dominant reinforcement strategy that strongly penalizes incorrect reasoning paths to avoid reinforcing bad trajectories while preserving the base model’s exploratory capacity. On the positive side, it introduces a group advantage mechanism that aggregates candidate trajectories into groups and scales positive updates according to intra-group variance, so that rare but correct paths receive stronger reinforcement and trivial or easy paths get relatively suppressed updates. This combination aims to maintain diversity while selectively amplifying genuinely informative reasoning behaviors.
AI.result: Across five mathematical reasoning benchmarks, AGPO attains state-of-the-art accuracy and consistently improves pass@k performance as k increases, indicating better utilization of multiple samples without boundary shrinkage. In an industrial search ads relevance optimization setting, AGPO improves the quality of LLM-based data annotations, which then yield substantial performance gains for downstream student models trained on these labels.
AI.conclusion: AGPO addresses the boundary shrinkage problem in RLVR for LLM reasoning by combining strong suppression of incorrect reasoning with variance-aware group-based positive reinforcement. This preserves the base model’s exploratory reasoning ability while more effectively reinforcing rare, correct solution paths, leading to better performance both on standardized math benchmarks and in a real-world industrial application.

title: UniSD: Towards a Unified Self-Distillation Framework for Large Language Models
summary: Self-distillation (SD) offers a promising path for adapting large language models (LLMs) without relying on stronger external teachers. However, SD in autoregressive LLMs remains challenging because self-generated trajectories are free-form, correctness is task-dependent, and plausible rationales can still provide unstable or unreliable supervision. Existing methods mainly examine isolated design choices, leaving their effectiveness, roles, and interactions unclear. In this paper, we propose UniSD, a unified framework to systematically study self-distillation. UniSD integrates complementary mechanisms that address supervision reliability, representation alignment, and training stability, including multi-teacher agreement, EMA teacher stabilization, token-level contrastive learning, feature matching, and divergence clipping. Across six benchmarks and six models from three model families, UniSD reveals when self-distillation improves over static imitation, which components drive the gains, and how these components interact across tasks. Guided by these insights, we construct UniSDfull, an integrated pipeline that combines complementary components and achieves the strongest overall performance, improving over the base model by +5.4 points and the strongest baseline by +2.8 points. Extensive evaluation highlights self-distillation as a practical and steerable approach for efficient LLM adaptation without stronger external teachers.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces UniSD, a unified self-distillation framework for autoregressive LLMs that combines multiple complementary techniques to improve adaptation without external teacher models, demonstrating consistent gains across models and benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Self-distillation can adapt LLMs without relying on stronger external teacher models, which is attractive for efficiency and practicality. However, applying SD to autoregressive LLMs is difficult: generated answers are open-ended, correctness varies by task, and even plausible rationales may be unreliable supervision. Existing SD methods typically tweak one or two design choices in isolation, so it is unclear when SD truly helps, which components matter, and how they interact. The paper aims to systematically analyze and structure this design space and to turn SD into a reliable, steerable tool for LLM adaptation.
AI.method: The authors propose UniSD, a unified self-distillation framework that decomposes SD into complementary components targeting three issues: (1) supervision reliability, (2) representation alignment, and (3) training stability. For reliability, they use multi-teacher agreement (multiple self-generated trajectories act as teachers, and agreement among them determines supervision strength) and an EMA (exponential moving average) teacher to stabilize the target distribution over time. For representation alignment, they introduce token-level contrastive learning and intermediate feature matching between teacher and student representations. For stability, they apply divergence clipping to limit excessively large gradients when the student and teacher distributions diverge too much. They then perform controlled experiments across six benchmarks and six LLMs from three families, ablating and recombining these components to study their individual and joint effects.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation across six benchmarks and six models shows that self-distillation under UniSD can outperform simple static imitation and baselines. The study clarifies when SD yields improvements, identifies which components (e.g., multi-teacher agreement, EMA teacher, contrastive learning, feature matching, divergence clipping) are most beneficial for different tasks, and examines their interactions. Based on these findings, the authors build UniSDfull, a full pipeline that combines the most complementary components. UniSDfull consistently achieves the best results, with an average improvement of +5.4 points over the base models and +2.8 points over the strongest prior baseline.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that, when carefully structured as in UniSD, self-distillation is a practical and controllable strategy for adapting LLMs without stronger external teachers. By jointly addressing supervision reliability, representation alignment, and training stability, UniSD not only improves performance but also provides insight into the design space of SD for autoregressive LLMs. The integrated UniSDfull variant offers a strong default recipe for efficient LLM adaptation, demonstrating that well-designed self-distillation can be a competitive alternative to teacher-based or purely supervised fine-tuning approaches.

title: Algospeak, Hiding in the Open: The Trade-off Between Legible Meaning and Detection Avoidance
summary: As large language models (LLMs) increasingly mediate both content generation and moderation, linguistic evasion strategies known as Algospeak have intensified the coevolution between evaders and detectors. This research formalizes the underlying dynamics grounded in a joint action model: when Algospeak increases, detectability and understandability decrease. Further, the concept of Majority Understandable Modulation (MUM) is introduced and defined as the modulation level at which additional evasive alteration increases detector evasion but loses comprehension for the majority of recipients. To empirically probe this trade-off, we introduce a reproducible framework that can be used to create meaning-preserving, Algospeak-style variants, based on an existing taxonomy and with tunable modulation levels. Using COVID-19 disinformation as a first proof-by-example setting, we construct a reference dataset of 700 modulated items, drawn from twenty base sentences across five modulation levels and seven strategies. We then run two linked evaluations with seven different language models: one testing for interpretation through meaning recovery and one for disinformation detection through classification. Curve fitting over modulation levels yields an estimate of the Majority Understandable Modulation threshold and enables sensitivity analyses across strategies and models, see Figure 1. Results reveal the characteristic relationships between understandability and modulation. This study lays the groundwork for understanding the dynamics behind Algospeak and provides the framework, dataset, and experimental setups described.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how 'Algospeak'—linguistic tricks used to evade automated moderation—affects both detectability by models and understandability by humans, introducing a formal trade-off point where more evasion starts to significantly hurt comprehension.
AI.motivation: As LLMs are now central to both generating and moderating online content, people increasingly use Algospeak (coded language) to avoid detection. However, there is little formal or empirical understanding of how increasing the level of such evasive modulation simultaneously affects moderation systems and human comprehension. The authors aim to model and quantify this trade-off to inform more robust moderation and better understand adversarial language evolution.
AI.method: They formalize the interaction between evaders and detectors using a joint action model relating Algospeak intensity to detectability and understandability. They define Majority Understandable Modulation (MUM), the level of modulation where further evasive changes help evade detectors but start to undermine comprehension for most readers. Empirically, they build a reproducible framework to generate meaning-preserving Algospeak variants based on an existing taxonomy, with tunable modulation levels and different strategies. As a case study, they use COVID-19 disinformation, generating a dataset of 700 items from 20 base sentences, 5 modulation levels, and 7 modulation strategies. They then evaluate seven LLMs on (1) meaning recovery (interpretation) and (2) disinformation detection (classification), and fit curves over modulation levels to estimate MUM thresholds and perform sensitivity analyses.
AI.result: They obtain a reference dataset of systematically modulated Algospeak examples and identify characteristic curves showing how understandability and detectability change with modulation. By fitting these curves, they estimate the MUM threshold for different strategies and models, and show how sensitivity varies across them. The findings empirically confirm a trade-off region where increased modulation helps detector evasion but degrades majority comprehension.
AI.conclusion: The study formalizes the coevolutionary dynamics between Algospeak users and automated detectors and introduces the MUM concept as a key operational threshold in this trade-off. It contributes a framework, dataset, and experimental protocol for generating and evaluating Algospeak-style content, offering tools for future work on robust moderation, adversarial attacks, and model evaluation in the presence of coded evasive language.

title: On the Role of Language Representations in Auto-Bidding: Findings and Implications
summary: Auto-bidding is a crucial task in real-time advertising markets, where policies must optimize long-horizon value under delivery constraints (e.g., budget and CPA). Existing methods for auto-bidding rely on compact numerical state representations: while they can implicitly capture delivery dynamics, they offer limited support for explicitly representing and controlling high-level intent, evolving feedback, and operator-style strategic guidance in real campaigns. Meanwhile, Large Language Models (LLMs) offer a powerful method for encoding semantic information, it remains unclear when LLMs help and how to integrate them without sacrificing numerical precision. Through systematic preliminary studies, we find that (1) LLM embeddings contain bidding-relevant cues yet cannot replace numerical features, and (2) gains emerge only with careful semantic--numeric integration rather than naive concatenation. Motivated by these findings, we propose \textit{SemBid}, a novel auto-bidding framework that injects LLM-encoded semantics into offline bidding trajectories at the token level. SemBid introduces three semantic inputs: \textit{Task}, \textit{History}, and \textit{Strategy}. It injects these semantics as tokens alongside numerical trajectory tokens and uses self-attention to integrate them, improving controllability and generalization across objectives. Across diverse scenarios and budget regimes, SemBid outperforms competitive baselines from offline RL and generative sequence modeling, with more consistent gains in overall performance, constraint satisfaction, and robustness. Our code is available at: \href{https://github.com/AlanYu04/SemBid-KDD2026}{\textcolor{blue}{here}}.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes SemBid, a framework that combines LLM-based semantic tokens with numerical trajectory tokens for auto-bidding, improving controllability, constraint satisfaction, and robustness over existing RL and sequence modeling methods.
AI.motivation: Current auto-bidding approaches mainly use compact numerical states, which are good at implicit delivery dynamics but poor at explicitly handling high-level intent, evolving feedback, and operator-like strategies. At the same time, LLMs can encode semantics but are hard to integrate with precise numerical control. The authors want to understand whether and how LLMs can be effectively combined with numerical features to improve auto-bidding without losing precision.
AI.method: The authors first conduct preliminary studies showing that LLM embeddings contain bidding-relevant information but cannot replace numerical features and that gains only appear with careful semantic–numeric integration. Based on this, they propose SemBid, which augments offline bidding trajectories with three types of LLM-encoded semantic tokens—Task, History, and Strategy—inserted at the token level alongside numerical trajectory tokens. A self-attention model jointly processes all tokens to integrate semantic guidance with numerical dynamics for policy learning.
AI.result: SemBid is evaluated across diverse auto-bidding scenarios and budget regimes, where it is compared with strong baselines from offline reinforcement learning and generative sequence modeling. It achieves better overall performance, improved satisfaction of delivery constraints (such as budget and CPA), and greater robustness, with more consistent gains than competing methods.
AI.conclusion: Integrating LLM-encoded semantic information into auto-bidding is beneficial when done via a structured, token-level fusion with numerical trajectories rather than naive feature concatenation. The SemBid framework demonstrates that semantic tokens representing task description, historical feedback, and operator strategies can significantly enhance controllability, generalization across objectives, and robustness in real-time advertising auto-bidding.

title: Parser agreement and disagreement in L2 Korean UD: Implications for human-in-the-loop annotation
comment: To be published in the 20th Linguistic Annotation Workshop
summary: We propose a simplified human-in-the-loop workflow for second language (L2) Korean morphosyntactic annotation by leveraging agreement between two domain-adapted parsers. We first evaluate whether parser agreement can serve as a proxy for annotation correctness by comparing it with independent human judgments. The results show strong correspondence between parser and human judgments, supporting the feasibility of semi-automatic L2-Korean UD annotation. Further analysis demonstrates that parser disagreements cluster in linguistically predictable domains such as grammatical-relation distinctions and clause-boundary ambiguity. While many disagreement cases are tractable for iterative model refinement, others reflect deeper representational challenges inherent in parsing and tagging L2-Korean corpora.

AI.tldr: They evaluate using agreement between two adapted parsers as a reliability signal for semi-automatic morphosyntactic annotation of L2 Korean, finding good correlation with human judgments but also identifying systematic areas of parser disagreement.
AI.motivation: Manual morphosyntactic annotation of learner (L2) Korean is costly and difficult, and existing fully automatic tools are not accurate enough. A more efficient workflow is needed that combines automatic parsing with targeted human intervention, ideally guided by a principled measure of when automatic outputs are reliable.
AI.method: They adapt two parsers to L2 Korean and compute where their morphosyntactic analyses agree or disagree on learner data. They compare parser agreement to independent human judgments of correctness to test if agreement is a good proxy for annotation quality. They also analyze the types of linguistic constructions that cause parser disagreement, focusing on grammatical relations and clause boundaries.
AI.result: Parser agreement correlates strongly with human judgments of correctness, indicating that agreement can effectively flag high-confidence annotations suitable for semi-automatic inclusion in an L2-Korean UD treebank. Disagreements tend to concentrate in specific, linguistically interpretable areas such as grammatical relation distinctions and ambiguous clause boundaries.
AI.conclusion: A human-in-the-loop workflow that leverages parser agreement is feasible for semi-automatic L2 Korean morphosyntactic annotation. Many disagreement cases can be addressed through iterative model refinement, but some reveal deeper representational issues in L2-Korean parsing and tagging that will require more fundamental theoretical and modeling advances.

title: Taklif.AI: LLM-Powered Platform for Interest-Based Personalized College Assignments
summary: Educators face significant challenges in creating engaging, personalized assignments that accommodate students' diverse interests and cognitive abilities. Traditional one-size-fits-all assignments frequently lead to decreased student engagement and increased reliance on unethical practices such as plagiarism. To address these challenges, we present Taklif.AI, a platform that leverages Large Language Models (LLMs) to automatically generate personalized assignments tailored to individual student interests. Unlike existing AI-powered educational platforms that personalize based on academic performance metrics alone, Taklif.AI incorporates students' extracurricular interests and cultural contexts into the assignment generation process through a structured prompt engineering pipeline with input and output guardrails. The platform employs a serverless architecture on AWS with Next.js, using Llama 3.3 70B as the primary LLM via LiteLLM for multi-provider load balancing and LangChain for prompt orchestration. We describe the system architecture, the prompt design methodology, and the guardrails framework that ensures output quality. Preliminary user acceptance testing with 68 participants (65 students and 3 educators) indicates positive reception, with 84% of participants rating the personalization feature as beneficial. We discuss the platform's current capabilities and limitations, and outline directions for rigorous empirical evaluation of learning outcomes.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Taklif.AI, a platform using large language models to generate personalized student assignments that incorporate individual interests and cultural context, showing positive initial user reception.
AI.motivation: Traditional, uniform assignments often fail to engage diverse learners and can encourage plagiarism; there is a need for scalable, ethically grounded personalization that goes beyond academic metrics to include students' interests and cultural backgrounds.
AI.method: The authors design and implement Taklif.AI, a serverless AWS-based system built with Next.js, using Llama 3.3 70B accessed via LiteLLM and orchestrated with LangChain. They develop a structured prompt-engineering pipeline with input/output guardrails to incorporate students’ extracurricular interests and cultural contexts into automatically generated assignments. They then conduct preliminary user acceptance testing with 68 participants.
AI.result: The system successfully generates personalized assignments, and initial user testing shows strong acceptance: 84% of the 68 participants (65 students, 3 educators) rated the personalization feature as beneficial.
AI.conclusion: Taklif.AI demonstrates the technical feasibility and positive user reception of interest- and culture-aware assignment generation with LLMs. However, rigorous empirical studies on learning outcomes are still needed, and the paper outlines directions for such future evaluations while discussing current limitations and capabilities.

title: Cited but Not Verified: Parsing and Evaluating Source Attribution in LLM Deep Research Agents
summary: Large language models (LLMs) power deep research agents that synthesize information from hundreds of web sources into cited reports, yet these citations cannot be reliably verified. Current approaches either trust models to self-cite accurately, risking bias, or employ retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) that does not validate source accessibility, relevance, or factual consistency. We introduce the first source attribution evaluation framework that uses a reproducible AST parser to extract and evaluate inline citations from LLM-generated Markdown reports at scale. Unlike methods that verify claims in isolation, our framework closes the loop by retrieving the actual cited content, enabling human or model evaluators to judge each citation against its source. Citations are evaluated along three dimensions. (1) Link Works verifies URL accessibility, (2) Relevant Content measures topical alignment, and (3) Fact Check validates factual accuracy against source content. We benchmark 14 closed-source and open-source LLMs across three evaluation dimensions using rubric-based LLM-as-a-judge evaluators calibrated through human review. Our results reveal that even the strongest frontier models maintain link validity above 94% and relevance above 80%, yet achieve only 39-77% factual accuracy, while fewer than half of open-source models successfully generate cited reports in a one-shot setting. Ablation studies on research depth show that Fact Check accuracy drops by approximately 42% on average across two frontier models as tool calls scale from 2 to 150, demonstrating that more retrieval does not produce more accurate citations. These findings reveal a critical disconnect between surface-level citation quality and factual reliability, and our framework provides the evaluation infrastructure to assess the disconnect.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces the first scalable framework to evaluate the quality of citations in LLM-generated research reports by actually retrieving and checking the cited sources.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based research agents generate reports with citations, but there is no robust, scalable way to verify whether these citations are accessible, relevant, or factually consistent with the source, which leads to untrustworthy outputs. Prior work either trusts model self-citations or uses RAG without validating the cited content, leaving a major gap in assessing citation reliability.
AI.method: The authors build a source attribution evaluation framework that: (1) parses LLM-produced Markdown reports with a reproducible AST parser to extract inline citations, (2) retrieves the content from the cited URLs, and (3) evaluates each citation along three dimensions—Link Works (URL accessibility), Relevant Content (topical alignment between citation and source), and Fact Check (factual accuracy of the cited claim against the source). They benchmark 14 open and closed LLMs, using rubric-based LLM-as-a-judge evaluators that are calibrated with human review, and perform ablation on research depth by varying the number of tool calls from 2 to 150.
AI.result: Across 14 models, frontier LLMs achieve high link validity (>94%) and high topical relevance (>80%) for citations, but relatively low factual accuracy (39–77%). Fewer than half of open-source models can even generate properly cited reports in a one-shot setting. As the number of retrieval/tool calls increases from 2 to 150, Fact Check accuracy for two frontier models drops by ~42% on average, indicating that more retrieval is correlated with worse factual reliability of citations.
AI.conclusion: Surface-level citation metrics such as link accessibility and topical relevance significantly overestimate the true reliability of LLM-generated citations, because factual alignment with sources is much weaker. The proposed framework closes the loop between citations and their underlying sources, offering infrastructure to systematically evaluate and expose this disconnect, and to drive future improvements in research agents and source attribution methods.

title: AirQualityBench: A Realistic Evaluation Benchmark for Global Air Quality Forecasting
summary: Air-quality forecasting models are commonly evaluated on regional, preprocessed, and normalized datasets, where missing observations are removed or artificially completed. Such protocols simplify comparison but hide the conditions that dominate real monitoring networks: uneven global coverage, structured missingness, heterogeneous pollutant scales, and deployment cost. We introduce \textbf{AirQualityBench}, a global multi-pollutant benchmark designed to evaluate forecasting models under these realistic conditions. The benchmark contains hourly observations from 3,720 monitoring stations over 2021--2025, covers six major pollutants, and preserves provider-native observation masks. Rather than imputing a dense data tensor, AirQualityBench exposes missingness as part of the forecasting problem and reports errors on valid future observations after inverse transformation to physical concentration scales. Evaluating representative spatio-temporal models under this unified protocol shows that strong performance on sanitized datasets does not reliably transfer to global, fragmented monitoring streams. AirQualityBench therefore serves as a realistic testbed for scalable, mask-aware, and physically interpretable air-quality forecasting. All benchmark data, code, evaluation scripts, and baseline implementations are available at \href{https://github.com/Star-Learning/AirQualityBench}{GitHub}.

AI.tldr: Introduces AirQualityBench, a realistic global benchmark for air-quality forecasting that preserves missing data patterns, multiple pollutants, and physical scales to better evaluate forecasting models.
AI.motivation: Existing air-quality forecasting models are usually evaluated on clean, regional, and heavily preprocessed datasets where missing values are removed or imputed and data are normalized. This makes comparison easier but fails to reflect the messy, fragmented, and heterogeneous nature of real-world air-quality monitoring networks. There is a need for a benchmark that incorporates realistic missingness, global coverage, and multiple pollutants so that model performance in practical deployment conditions can be properly assessed.
AI.method: The authors construct AirQualityBench, a benchmark built from hourly data collected between 2021 and 2025 from 3,720 monitoring stations worldwide, spanning six major pollutants. They retain the original observation masks from data providers instead of imputing or densifying the data, and define an evaluation protocol that treats missingness as an inherent part of the forecasting task. Forecast errors are computed only on observed future values and after inverting any normalization so they are in physical concentration units. They then apply and compare representative spatio-temporal forecasting models under this consistent, realistic protocol.
AI.result: Under the AirQualityBench protocol, models that perform strongly on traditional sanitized datasets do not necessarily perform well on the global, sparse, and fragmented monitoring data. This reveals a gap between performance reported in prior work and robustness in real deployment scenarios.
AI.conclusion: AirQualityBench provides a more realistic and challenging testbed for air-quality forecasting, encouraging the development of models that are scalable, explicitly handle complex missing data patterns, and produce predictions that are interpretable in physical pollutant concentration units. The benchmark, along with its data, code, evaluation scripts, and baselines, is made publicly available for the research community.

title: StraTA: Incentivizing Agentic Reinforcement Learning with Strategic Trajectory Abstraction
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used as interactive agents, but optimizing them for long-horizon decision making remains difficult because current methods are largely purely reactive, which weakens both exploration and credit assignment over extended trajectories. In this work, we present Strategic Trajectory Abstraction (StraTA), a simple framework that introduces an explicit trajectory-level strategy into agentic reinforcement learning (RL). StraTA samples a compact strategy from the initial task state, conditions subsequent actions on that strategy, and trains strategy generation and action execution jointly with a hierarchical GRPO-style rollout design, further enhanced by diverse strategy rollout and critical self-judgment. Experiments on ALFWorld, WebShop, and SciWorld show that StraTA consistently improves both sample efficiency and final performance over strong baselines. StraTA reaches success rates of 93.1% on ALFWorld and 84.2% on WebShop. On SciWorld, StraTA attains a 63.5% overall score, outperforming frontier closed-source models.

AI.tldr: StraTA is a framework that adds an explicit, compact, trajectory-level strategy to LLM agents and jointly trains strategy generation and execution, leading to better long-horizon performance and sample efficiency on ALFWorld, WebShop, and SciWorld.
AI.motivation: LLM-based agents struggle with long-horizon decision making because they are typically optimized in a purely reactive way. This limits effective exploration and makes it hard to assign credit over long trajectories. The authors want a way for LLM agents to plan and commit to higher-level strategies that guide subsequent actions, improving performance on complex, multi-step tasks.
AI.method: StraTA introduces a trajectory-level strategic abstraction in an RL framework. From the initial task state, the model samples a compact strategy description. Subsequent actions are conditioned on this strategy. Training jointly optimizes the strategy generator and the action executor using a hierarchical GRPO-style rollout scheme. The framework adds diverse strategy rollouts to encourage exploration of different high-level plans, and incorporates critical self-judgment to refine strategies and actions based on feedback.
AI.result: On three benchmark environments (ALFWorld, WebShop, SciWorld), StraTA achieves consistently higher performance and better sample efficiency than strong LLM-agent baselines. It reaches 93.1% success on ALFWorld and 84.2% on WebShop. On SciWorld, StraTA obtains a 63.5% overall score, surpassing competitive closed-source models.
AI.conclusion: Introducing explicit, learnable trajectory-level strategies into LLM-based RL agents substantially improves their ability to handle long-horizon tasks. The hierarchical training design and added mechanisms for strategy diversity and self-critique lead to better exploration, credit assignment, and ultimately higher success rates than existing reactive methods.

title: SANEmerg: An Emergent Communication Framework for Semantic-aware Agentic AI Networking
comment: Accepted at IEEE/IFIP WiOpt Workshop, Columbus, OH, USA, June 2026
summary: Future networking systems are envisioned to become part of an agentic AI-native ecosystem in which a vast number of heterogeneous and specialized AI agents cooperate seamlessly to fulfill complex user requirements in real time. However, traditional networking paradigms are characterized by a rigid decoupling of communication and computation, which often leads to significant inefficiencies in large-scale agentic AI networking (AgentNet) systems. Emergent communication offers a novel solution by enabling autonomous agents that support task-specific signaling protocols for information exchange and collaborative coordination. In this paper, we consider a multi-agent emergent communication framework, tailored for semantic-aware AgentNet systems in which the user's semantic intent can be automatically detected, inferred, and linked to a set of sub-tasks to be assigned to a set of agents. We investigate how communication and signaling protocols can emerge among collaborative agents with computationally bounded intelligence under stringent bandwidth constraints. Our proposed framework, called SANEmerg, is designed to facilitate the emergence of communication for collaborative task fulfillment while adhering to the physical limits of AgentNet. SANEmerg incorporates a bandwidth-adaptable importance-filter that dynamically prioritizes the transmission of higher-contribution message dimensions, ensuring robust performance in bandwidth-limited environments. Furthermore, SANEmerg integrates a complexity-regularizer grounded in the Minimum Description Length (MDL) principle to facilitate the emergence of computationally bounded signaling. Evaluated via an AgentNet prototype and extensive experimentation, SANEmerg demonstrates significant performance improvements over state-of-the-art solutions, achieving superior task accuracy while significantly reducing bandwidth and computational overhead.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes SANEmerg, a framework for emergent communication in multi-agent, semantic-aware AI-native networks (AgentNet), improving collaborative task performance with less bandwidth and computation.
AI.motivation: Future AI-native networks will involve many heterogeneous AI agents that must collaborate in real time. Traditional networking separates communication from computation, causing inefficiencies when many agents with limited bandwidth and bounded intelligence must coordinate to fulfill complex user tasks. There is a need for communication protocols that emerge adaptively among agents, are aware of user semantics, and respect physical limits like bandwidth and computational capacity.
AI.method: The authors design SANEmerg, a multi-agent emergent communication framework tailored to semantic-aware AgentNet systems. It models user intent as a set of semantic sub-tasks distributed among agents and lets agents learn task-specific signaling protocols. Two key mechanisms are introduced: (1) a bandwidth-adaptable importance filter that ranks and selectively transmits higher-contribution dimensions of messages under bandwidth constraints; and (2) a complexity regularizer based on the Minimum Description Length (MDL) principle that penalizes overly complex communication, encouraging computationally bounded signaling. The framework is implemented in an AgentNet prototype and evaluated via extensive experiments under varying bandwidth and computation constraints.
AI.result: Experiments on the AgentNet prototype show that SANEmerg enables agents to develop effective emergent communication protocols that respect bandwidth and computation limits. Compared with state-of-the-art baselines, SANEmerg achieves higher task accuracy while significantly reducing bandwidth usage and computational overhead. The importance filter maintains strong performance in bandwidth-constrained settings, and the MDL-based regularizer controls signaling complexity without sacrificing accuracy.
AI.conclusion: SANEmerg demonstrates that emergent, semantic-aware communication among AI agents can be engineered to operate effectively within the physical constraints of future AI-native networks. By combining bandwidth-adaptive message filtering with MDL-based complexity regularization, SANEmerg yields more accurate and efficient collaborative task fulfillment than existing methods. This suggests a promising direction for building scalable, agentic networking systems where communication and computation are tightly integrated rather than rigidly separated.

title: Beyond Negative Rollouts: Positive-Only Policy Optimization with Implicit Negative Gradients
summary: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR), due to the deterministic verification, becomes a dominant paradigm for enhancing the reasoning ability of large language models (LLMs). The community witnesses the rapid change from the Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) to Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), in which GRPO reduces the complicated advantage estimation with simple estimation over grouped positive and negative rollouts. However, we note that negative rollouts may admit no gradation of failure severity, and the combinatorial vastness makes penalizing a few sampled negatives unlikely to cover a meaningful reward signal under sparse binary rewards. In this work, we propose Positive-Only Policy Optimization (POPO), a novel RLVR framework in which learning can occur exclusively via online positive rollouts. Specifically, POPO utilizes bounded importance sampling over the positive rollout set. Thus, no disjoint negative rollouts are used for the gradient guidance. We show that implicit negative gradients can emerge naturally through reinforcing the positive probability via rollouts redistribution. Next, POPO stabilizes the policy optimization through two mechanisms. First, it applies a siamese policy network with a momentum-based adaptation law for stabilized policy evolution. Second, we replace the KL-divergence with a bounded similarity penalty term in the siamese representation space. We conduct extensive experiments using publicly available, well-established text-LLM models, e.g., the Qwen family, across all-level mathematical benchmarks. Our experiment demonstrates that POPO achieves performance comparable to, or even superior to GRPO. Notably, we show that POPO can achieve 36.67% in AIME 2025 with Qwen-Math-7B, outperforming GRPO 30.00%. Our ablation and sweep studies further illustrate the necessity and robustness of POPO components.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Positive-Only Policy Optimization (POPO), a new reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) method that learns solely from positively verified rollouts, matching or surpassing GRPO on math reasoning benchmarks while improving stability and sample efficiency under sparse binary rewards.
AI.motivation: Existing RLVR methods for improving LLM reasoning, such as GRPO, rely on both positive and negative rollouts. Under sparse, binary, verifiable rewards, negative rollouts provide little information about how bad a failure is, and sampling only a few negatives barely covers the vast space of possible errors. This limits learning efficiency and can destabilize training. The authors aim to design an RLVR framework that can learn effectively using only positive rollouts, avoiding the issues of uninformative and combinatorially sparse negative signals, while still providing strong gradient guidance and stable optimization.
AI.method: The authors propose Positive-Only Policy Optimization (POPO), an RLVR algorithm that operates exclusively on online positive rollouts. It uses bounded importance sampling over the set of positive trajectories, removing the need for explicitly sampled negative rollouts. Implicit negative gradients emerge because increasing the probability mass of positive rollouts implicitly decreases probability on unobserved or low-probability failures. To stabilize optimization, POPO employs (1) a siamese policy network architecture with a momentum-based adaptation rule, acting like a slowly-updating target policy to smooth learning, and (2) a bounded similarity penalty in the siamese representation space instead of the usual KL-divergence constraint, controlling policy drift in a more robust manner.
AI.result: Using public text-based LLMs (notably the Qwen family) across a suite of mathematical reasoning benchmarks, POPO attains performance that is comparable to or better than GRPO. On the AIME 2025 benchmark, POPO with Qwen-Math-7B achieves 36.67% accuracy, exceeding GRPO's 30.00%. The authors perform ablation and hyperparameter sweep studies that show each of POPO's components (positive-only optimization, bounded importance sampling, siamese+momentum architecture, and similarity penalty) contributes meaningfully to performance and robustness.
AI.conclusion: POPO demonstrates that effective RLVR for LLM reasoning does not require explicit negative rollouts: learning from positive rollouts alone, with carefully designed importance sampling and stability mechanisms, can implicitly encode negative feedback and yield state-of-the-art or superior performance compared to GRPO. This challenges the prevalent reliance on grouped positive/negative rollouts and suggests that positive-only optimization is a viable, robust alternative for improving LLM reasoning under verifiable, sparse, binary rewards.

title: XDecomposer: Learning Prior-Free Set Decomposition for Multiphase X-ray Diffraction
summary: Multiphase powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) analysis remains a fundamental bottleneck in structure identification, as real-world synthesis often produces complex mixtures whose constituent phases (components) cannot be reliably disentangled. While recent advances in representation-based crystal retrieval and generation suggest the possibility of inferring structures directly from PXRD, existing approaches largely assume single-phase inputs and break down in multiphase settings. Here, we present XDecomposer, a prior-free framework for joint decomposition and identification of multiphase XRD patterns without requiring candidate phase lists, structural templates, or prior knowledge of phase number. We formulate multiphase diffraction analysis as a set prediction problem, where the model infers an unordered set of phase-resolved components, their mixture proportions, and corresponding structural representations within a unified architecture. A phase-query-driven decomposition mechanism, together with diffraction-consistent physical reconstruction, enables accurate source separation while preserving crystallographic fidelity. Extensive experiments on both simulated and experimental datasets show that XDecomposer substantially improves reconstruction accuracy and phase identification across diverse chemical systems, while maintaining strong generalization to unseen mixtures. These results provide a practical route toward data-driven, source-resolved multiphase XRD analysis and reduce long-standing dependence on prior-guided iteratively phase matching. The code is openly available at https://github.com/Licht0812/XDecomposer

AI.tldr: XDecomposer is a prior-free, data-driven framework that decomposes multiphase powder XRD patterns into individual phase components and identifies their structures without needing prior phase lists or knowing the number of phases.
AI.motivation: Multiphase PXRD patterns from real syntheses are hard to analyze because they contain overlapping signals from multiple crystal phases. Existing machine-learning methods for structure inference from PXRD mostly assume single-phase patterns and rely on prior knowledge such as candidate phase lists, templates, or known phase counts. This creates a bottleneck for automated structure identification in realistic settings, where mixtures are common and priors may be incomplete or unavailable.
AI.method: The authors cast multiphase diffraction analysis as a set prediction problem: given a PXRD pattern, the model predicts an unordered set of phase-resolved components, each with a mixture proportion and a structural representation. They design a unified architecture with phase queries that drive the decomposition of the observed pattern into source phases, and enforce diffraction-consistent physical reconstruction to ensure that the predicted phases recombine to match the observed PXRD. The framework is prior-free in that it does not require candidate phase lists, structural templates, or pre-specified number of phases, and learns to perform joint source separation and structure identification directly from data.
AI.result: On both simulated and experimental datasets, XDecomposer achieves significantly higher reconstruction accuracy of the observed PXRD patterns and more accurate phase identification compared to existing methods, across a wide range of chemical systems. It also generalizes well to unseen mixtures, indicating robustness to distribution shifts in phase composition and structure.
AI.conclusion: XDecomposer provides a practical, prior-free, and data-driven solution to multiphase PXRD analysis, enabling automatic decomposition and structural identification of complex mixtures without reliance on iterative, prior-guided phase matching. This has the potential to streamline and accelerate structure characterization workflows in materials science and crystallography, especially in realistic synthesis scenarios where multiphase products are the norm.

title: EMO: Pretraining Mixture of Experts for Emergent Modularity
summary: Large language models are typically deployed as monolithic systems, requiring the full model even when applications need only a narrow subset of capabilities, e.g., code, math, or domain-specific knowledge. Mixture-of-Experts (MoEs) seemingly offer a potential alternative by activating only a subset of experts per input, but in practice, restricting inference to a subset of experts for a given domain leads to severe performance degradation. This limits their practicality in memory-constrained settings, especially as models grow larger and sparser. We introduce EMO, an MoE designed for modularity-the independent use and composition of expert subsets-without requiring human-defined priors. Our key idea is to encourage tokens from similar domains to rely on similar experts. Since tokens within a document often share a domain, EMO restricts them to select experts from a shared pool, while allowing different documents to use different pools. This simple constraint enables coherent expert groupings to emerge during pretraining using document boundaries alone. We pretrain a 1B-active, 14B-total EMO on 1T tokens. As a full model, it matches standard MoE performance. Crucially, it enables selective expert use: retaining only 25% (12.5%) of experts incurs just a 1% (3%) absolute drop, whereas standard MoEs break under the same setting. We further find that expert subsets in EMO specialize at semantic levels (e.g., domains such as math or code), in contrast to the low-level syntactic specialization observed in standard MoEs. Altogether, our results demonstrate a path toward modular, memory-efficient deployment of large, sparse models and open new opportunities for composable architectures.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces EMO, a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) language model that learns modular expert groups aligned with semantic domains, enabling selective use of expert subsets with minimal performance loss.
AI.motivation: Conventional large language models must be loaded in full even when applications require only specific capabilities (e.g., coding, math, or a domain area), which is expensive in memory and compute. While Mixture-of-Experts architectures promise conditional computation by activating only a few experts per token, in practice restricting inference to a subset of experts for a domain causes large performance drops. This prevents effective modular or memory-constrained deployment, especially as models get larger and sparser. The paper aims to design an MoE that can be decomposed into meaningful expert subsets that can be independently used and recomposed without substantial degradation, and without hand-crafted priors about domains.
AI.method: The authors design EMO, an MoE model whose routing is encouraged to align experts with semantic or domain-level structure. The core mechanism is a training-time constraint: tokens within the same document must all choose experts from a shared expert pool, while different documents can use different pools. Since tokens in a document are likely to come from the same domain, this pushes the router to cluster domain-related behavior into particular expert groups. The model is pretrained at scale (1T tokens) with a 14B total parameter MoE, of which 1B parameters are active per token, using this document-level pool restriction as the only additional structural bias. At inference, subsets of experts can be retained or dropped to test modular use and the degree of semantic specialization that emerges.
AI.result: The 1B-active, 14B-total EMO model, when used as a full model, matches the performance of a comparable standard MoE baseline, so the modularity constraint does not harm overall quality. Under expert pruning/selective use, EMO is far more robust: keeping only 25% of the experts causes around a 1% absolute performance drop, and keeping only 12.5% of experts results in about a 3% drop. In contrast, standard MoEs degrade severely under the same subset restriction. Analysis of expert behavior shows that the expert subsets in EMO specialize in high-level semantic domains (e.g., math, code) rather than the mostly syntactic, low-level specializations seen in conventional MoEs.
AI.conclusion: EMO demonstrates that a simple training-time constraint based on document-level shared expert pools can induce semantically coherent, modular expert groupings in MoE language models, without manual domain labels. This yields a large, sparse model that preserves standard MoE quality yet supports effective deployment with only a fraction of experts loaded, making it memory-efficient and composable. The work suggests a promising direction for building modular, domain-specialized LMs where expert subsets can be independently deployed or combined to match application needs.

title: Agentic, Context-Aware Risk Intelligence in the Internet of Value
summary: The Internet of Value (IoV) is a heterogeneous, partially-trusted network in which the dominant marginal risk is composite (route, sentiment, liquidity, and the policy a system is willing to commit to) rather than a property of any single chain. We argue that a risk primitive adequate for this regime is a composition of five engines: a prediction engine over price, liquidity, volatility, and route health; a Bittensor verification subnet that decentralises and economically scores prediction outputs; a sentiment-fusion engine over text, on-chain flow, and grey-literature feeds; an agentic engine under constitutional, role-bound action constraints; and an API-risk and scenario engine that converts forecasts into pre-committed action programs in the sense of Monte-Carlo scenario generation. We anchor the architecture in two empirical artefacts: a 27-hour policy-constrained liquidity stress-response experiment on Solana, and a 168-hour prediction-router calibration arc reported with explicit class-imbalance honesty. The case study supports deployability; the validator-loss decomposition is stated formally and is falsifiable.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a unified “risk primitive” for the Internet of Value (IoV) that treats risk as a composite of route, sentiment, liquidity, and policy, and implements it via five coordinated engines tested on Solana experiments.
AI.motivation: Existing approaches often treat risk as a property of individual chains or isolated dimensions (e.g., price or liquidity alone). In the IoV, where assets and information move across many chains and routes under partial trust, the main risks are composite and systemic. The authors aim to define and implement a risk primitive that can operate in this heterogeneous, cross-chain environment and support practical, pre-committed risk-aware actions.
AI.method: They define a risk architecture composed of five engines: (1) a prediction engine modeling price, liquidity, volatility, and route health; (2) a Bittensor-based verification subnet that decentralizes and economically scores model predictions; (3) a sentiment-fusion engine that ingests text, on-chain flow, and grey literature to estimate sentiment; (4) an agentic engine that takes actions under constitutional, role-bound constraints; and (5) an API-risk and scenario engine that converts forecasts into pre-committed executable policies using Monte Carlo scenario generation. They then instantiate and test this architecture in two empirical case studies on Solana.
AI.result: They conduct a 27-hour policy-constrained liquidity stress-response experiment on Solana, demonstrating how the system responds to liquidity shocks under pre-committed policies, and a 168-hour prediction-router calibration study that evaluates and calibrates the prediction and routing components while explicitly reporting class imbalance effects. They formally decompose validator losses and provide a structure that can be empirically falsified.
AI.conclusion: The proposed five-engine risk primitive is practically deployable in IoV settings, captures composite risk more appropriately than single-chain or single-metric models, and yields a formal, falsifiable framework for understanding and managing validator and routing risk across heterogeneous, partially-trusted networks.

title: Null Space Constrained Contrastive Visual Forgetting for MLLM Unlearning
summary: The core challenge of machine unlearning is to strike a balance between target knowledge removal and non-target knowledge retention. In the context of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs), this challenge becomes even more pronounced, as knowledge is further divided into visual and textual modalities that are tightly intertwined. In this paper, we introduce an MLLM unlearning approach that aims to forget target visual knowledge while preserving non-target visual knowledge and all textual knowledge. Specifically, we freeze the LLM backbone and achieve unlearning by fine-tuning the visual module. First, we propose a Contrastive Visual Forgetting (CVF) mechanism to separate target visual knowledge from retained visual knowledge, guiding the representations of target visual concepts toward appropriate regions in the feature space. Second, we identify the null space associated with retained knowledge and constrain the unlearning process within this space, thereby significantly mitigating degradation in knowledge retention. Third, beyond static unlearning scenarios, we extend our approach to continual unlearning, where forgetting requests arrive sequentially. Extensive experiments across diverse benchmarks demonstrate that our approach achieves a strong balance between effective forgetting and robust knowledge retention.

AI.tldr: An MLLM unlearning method that forgets specific visual knowledge while preserving other visual and all textual knowledge using contrastive forgetting and null-space constraints, extended to continual unlearning.
AI.motivation: Machine unlearning must remove specified knowledge without harming remaining capabilities; this is harder in multimodal LLMs where visual and textual knowledge are entangled. There is a need for an approach that can selectively erase visual concepts while keeping other knowledge, and that can handle repeated unlearning requests over time.
AI.method: Freeze the LLM backbone and fine-tune only the visual module. Introduce Contrastive Visual Forgetting (CVF) to pull representations of target visual concepts away from those of retained knowledge in feature space. Compute the null space corresponding to retained knowledge and restrict parameter updates for unlearning to this null space to avoid damaging preserved capabilities. Extend the framework from a one-shot setting to a continual unlearning scenario where multiple forgetting requests are handled sequentially.
AI.result: Experiments on multiple benchmarks show the method can effectively erase target visual knowledge while maintaining high performance on non-target visual tasks and preserving textual capabilities, outperforming baselines in the trade-off between forgetting and retention. The continual unlearning setting also maintains this balance over sequences of forgetting requests.
AI.conclusion: Targeted unlearning in MLLMs can be achieved by operating only on the visual module with contrastive forgetting and null-space constrained updates, yielding strong forgetting-retention trade-offs and supporting continual unlearning. This offers a practical path toward controllable, fine-grained forgetting in multimodal foundation models.

title: PREFER: Personalized Review Summarization with Online Preference Learning
summary: Product reviews significantly influence purchasing decisions on e-commerce platforms. However, the sheer volume of reviews can overwhelm users, obscuring the information most relevant to their specific needs. Current e-commerce summarization systems typically produce generic, static summaries that fail to account for the fact that (i) different users care about different product characteristics, and (ii) these preferences may evolve with interactions. To address the challenge of unknown latent preferences, we propose an online learning framework that generates personalized summaries for each user. Our system iteratively refines its understanding of user preferences by incorporating feedback directly from the generated summaries over time. We provide a case study using the Amazon Reviews'23 dataset, showing in controlled simulations that online preference learning improves alignment with target user interests while maintaining summary quality.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an online learning framework to generate personalized, dynamically updated summaries of product reviews based on evolving user preferences, showing improved alignment with user interests while preserving summary quality.
AI.motivation: Traditional review summarization on e-commerce platforms produces generic, one-size-fits-all summaries that ignore that different users prioritize different product attributes and that their preferences can change over time. With massive volumes of reviews, users struggle to find information most relevant to their individual needs, creating a need for adaptive, preference-aware summarization.
AI.method: The authors design an online learning framework that models user-specific, latent preferences and updates them iteratively through user feedback on generated summaries. As users interact with summaries, the system refines its estimate of their preferences and uses these updated preferences to produce more tailored summaries. They evaluate this approach via controlled simulations on the Amazon Reviews'23 dataset.
AI.result: In simulated experiments on Amazon Reviews'23, the proposed online preference learning approach yields summaries that better match target user interests compared to static, generic baselines, while overall summary quality remains high and is not sacrificed for personalization.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating online preference learning into review summarization enables personalized, evolving summaries that better capture individual user interests than static summaries. The case study suggests that such frameworks can significantly enhance user-centric review exploration on e-commerce platforms without degrading core summary quality.

title: Wisteria: A Unified Multi-Scale Feature Learning Framework for DNA Language Model
summary: DNA language model aims to decipher the regulatory grammar and semantic of genomes by capturing long range dependencies in DNA sequences. Existing methods emphasize long range token interactions but often ignore the interplay between local motifs and global dependencies. In this paper, we propose Wisteria, a genomic language model that integrates multi scale feature learning within a unified framework for DNA sequence. Specifically, Wisteria augments the Mamba based architecture with gated dilated convolutions to capture local motifs and regulatory patterns, while gated multilayer perceptrons refine global dependencies. We further introduce a Fourier based attention mechanism to support frequency domain modeling, periodic extension and length generalization. Across four experimental settings with both short and long range dependencies, Wisteria demonstrates strong performance on downstream benchmarks against competitive DNA language model baselines. These results indicate that Wisteria effectively unifies local and global dependency modeling for multi scale genomic sequence analysis.

AI.tldr: A new DNA language model, Wisteria, jointly models local motifs and long‑range dependencies using Mamba, gated dilated convolutions, MLPs, and Fourier-based attention, achieving strong performance on genomic benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing DNA language models focus mainly on long-range token interactions and often fail to simultaneously capture local motifs (like transcription factor binding sites) and global regulatory dependencies within genomic sequences. There is a need for a unified framework that can effectively learn and integrate multi-scale genomic features—both local and global—to better decipher regulatory grammar and semantics in DNA.
AI.method: The paper proposes Wisteria, a genomic language model that augments a Mamba-based sequence architecture with several components: (1) gated dilated convolutions to capture local motifs and short-range regulatory patterns at multiple receptive fields; (2) gated multilayer perceptrons (MLPs) to refine and model global dependencies across the sequence; and (3) a Fourier-based attention mechanism that performs frequency-domain modeling, enabling periodic extension and better length generalization. These components are integrated into a unified, multi-scale feature learning framework tailored for DNA sequences.
AI.result: On four experimental setups covering both short- and long-range dependency tasks in genomic data, Wisteria consistently outperforms or matches strong, competitive DNA language model baselines on downstream benchmarks. Performance gains are observed in settings requiring accurate modeling of local motifs, long-range interactions, or both, indicating improved multi-scale representation learning.
AI.conclusion: Wisteria successfully unifies local and global dependency modeling for DNA sequences within a single framework. By combining Mamba-based sequence modeling with gated dilated convolutions, gated MLPs, and Fourier-based attention, it provides effective multi-scale genomic sequence analysis and leads to strong empirical performance on diverse genomic benchmarks, suggesting it is a promising approach for decoding regulatory grammar and semantics in genomes.

title: Intentmaking and Sensemaking: Human Interaction with AI-Guided Mathematical Discovery
summary: Artificial intelligence offers powerful new tools for scientific discovery, but the interaction paradigms required to effectively harness these systems remain underexplored. In this paper, we present findings from a formative user study with 11 expert mathematicians who used AlphaEvolve, an evolutionary coding agent, to tackle advanced problems in their fields of expertise. We identify and characterize a distinct workflow we term intentmaking, the iterative process of discovering, defining, and refining one's experimental goals through active system interaction. We frame this as a natural extension to sensemaking, the cognitive process of building an understanding of complex or novel data. We suggest that users enter a cycle of intentmaking (defining and updating their experiment) and sensemaking (interpreting the results) which repeats many times during the course of an investigation. Our documentation of these themes suggests an approach to designing AI tools for scientific discovery that goes beyond the existing question/answer model of many current systems, treating them as collaborative instruments rather than opaque black-box assistants.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how expert mathematicians interact with an AI coding agent and introduces the concept of 'intentmaking' as a key part of their workflow.
AI.motivation: AI systems like AlphaEvolve can help with scientific discovery, but we lack understanding of how experts should best interact with these tools, beyond simple question/answer paradigms. The authors want to uncover real workflows and cognitive processes that arise when experts collaborate with an AI agent on complex problems.
AI.method: The authors conducted a formative user study with 11 expert mathematicians using AlphaEvolve, an evolutionary coding agent, to solve advanced problems in their respective domains. They observed and analyzed the participants’ interactions, focusing on patterns in how they defined, refined, and pursued their experimental goals through ongoing interaction with the system.
AI.result: They identified a distinct workflow pattern they call 'intentmaking': an iterative process in which users discover, define, and refine their experimental goals through active interaction with the AI. They also observed that this intentmaking interleaves with sensemaking—interpreting results—forming a repeated cycle during investigations.
AI.conclusion: Scientific AI tools should be designed not just as question/answer systems but as collaborative instruments that support iterative cycles of intentmaking and sensemaking. This requires interaction designs that help users formulate, adjust, and clarify their goals while interpreting evolving outputs, moving beyond treating AI as a black-box assistant.

title: Which Are the Low-Resource Languages of the Semantic Web?
comment: ESWC 2026 - 23rd European Semantic Web Conference, May 2026, Dubrovnik, Croatia
summary: Emerging digital technologies are exacerbating the existing divide in Open Access Data (OAD) between high-and low-resource languages, excluding many communities from the global digital transformation. Multilingual Linked Open Data Knowledge Graphs (LOD KGs) could contribute to mitigating this divide through cross-lingual transfer; however, no clear quantitative definition of low-resource languages has yet been established in the context of LOD KGs. In this poster, we present a methodology to analyze the distribution of languages across LOD KGs and propose a preliminary multi-level categorization based on DBpedia, BabelNet, and Wikidata. This categorization is leveraged to bring a formal definition of low-, high-, and medium-resource languages that could be later leveraged to select cross-lingual transfer candidates.

AI.tldr: The paper defines what counts as low-, medium-, and high-resource languages in multilingual Linked Open Data Knowledge Graphs and proposes a quantitative, multi-level categorization based on DBpedia, BabelNet, and Wikidata.
AI.motivation: Digital technologies and Open Access Data disproportionately benefit high-resource languages, leaving speakers of low-resource languages behind.
