These distributions are encoded into compact visual representations—"visual fingerprints"—that support side-by-side, distribution-level comparison of model behavior across conditions.
AI.result: Across four case studies or usage scenarios, the visual fingerprints reveal stable, condition-specific patterns in LLM behavior, such as preference for certain topics, styles, or structural forms, that are not evident from inspecting individual responses or relying on simple aggregate statistics. The method allows users to see nuanced differences and consistencies between prompts, parameter settings, or models.
AI.conclusion: Modeling LLM outputs as distributions of linguistic choices and visualizing them as fingerprints enables richer, more intuitive comparison of generation conditions. This approach exposes consistent behavioral tendencies that traditional evaluation methods often miss, providing a useful tool for prompt design, model comparison, and diagnostic analysis of LLM behavior.

title: VibeServe: Can AI Agents Build Bespoke LLM Serving Systems?
summary: For years, we have built LLM serving systems like any other critical infrastructure: a single general-purpose stack, hand-tuned over many engineer-years, meant to support every model and workload. In this paper, we take the opposite bet: a multi-agent loop that automatically synthesizes bespoke serving systems for different usage scenarios. We propose VibeServe, the first agentic loop that generates entire LLM serving stacks end-to-end. VibeServe uses an outer loop to plan and track the search over system designs, and an inner loop to implement candidates, check correctness, and measure performance on the target benchmark. In the standard deployment setting, where existing stacks are highly optimized, VibeServe remains competitive with vLLM, showing that generation-time specialization need not come at the cost of performance. More interestingly, in non-standard scenarios, VibeServe outperforms existing systems by exploiting opportunities that generic systems miss in six scenarios involving non-standard model architectures, workload knowledge, and hardware-specific optimizations. Together, these results suggest a different point in the design space for infrastructure software: generation-time specialization rather than runtime generality. Code is available at https://github.com/uw-syfi/vibe-serve.

AI.tldr: VibeServe is an agentic system that automatically generates specialized LLM-serving stacks for specific scenarios, achieving performance competitive with or better than hand-tuned general-purpose systems.
AI.motivation: Conventional LLM serving infrastructures rely on a single, heavily hand-optimized, general-purpose stack intended to support all models and workloads. This approach is inflexible and may miss optimization opportunities in non-standard scenarios such as unusual model architectures, specific workload patterns, or hardware-specific features. The authors aim to explore whether automated, scenario-specific generation of serving stacks can match or outperform these general systems, thereby shifting from runtime generality to generation-time specialization.
AI.method: The authors design VibeServe, a multi-agent, agentic loop that synthesizes complete LLM serving systems end-to-end. VibeServe uses an outer loop to explore and plan over candidate system designs and track search progress, and an inner loop that concretely implements each candidate, validates its correctness, and benchmarks its performance against target workloads. The framework is evaluated both in standard deployment settings and in a range of non-standard scenarios that exercise diverse model architectures, known workload characteristics, and hardware-specific optimization opportunities.
AI.result: In standard, highly optimized deployment settings, VibeServe achieves performance competitive with vLLM, a strong baseline for LLM serving, showing that automated generation does not inherently sacrifice performance. In six non-standard scenarios that involve non-standard model architectures, workload-informed optimizations, and hardware-specific tuning, VibeServe surpasses existing generic systems, demonstrating that automatic specialization can exploit optimization opportunities that general-purpose stacks overlook.
AI.conclusion: VibeServe demonstrates that an agentic, generation-time specialization approach can both remain competitive with state-of-the-art general-purpose LLM serving systems in typical settings and deliver significant gains in atypical or specialized scenarios. These results support a shift in how infrastructure software for LLMs can be designed: instead of relying solely on a single, general, hand-tuned stack, it may be more powerful to automatically generate bespoke serving systems tailored to each usage context. The released code enables further exploration and validation of this design paradigm.

title: Safety Certification is Classification
summary: The goal of this paper is certifying safety of dynamical systems subject to uncertainty. Existing approaches use trajectory data to estimate transition probabilities, and compute safety probabilities recursively via dynamic programming (DP). This recursion may lead to compounding errors in the certified safety probability, thus collapsing to a vacuous lower bound for growing horizons $T$. We propose a kernel embedding framework that treats safety certification as a classification problem on trajectory data, directly estimating the $T$-step safety probability without recursion. We show that the framework subsumes well-established approaches from the literature (e.g., barrier certificates, robust Markov models) as special cases, and allows us to go beyond their limitations. As the main consequence, it bypasses compounding error across the horizon and enables certification for systems with non-Markovian dynamics. We demonstrate that direct estimators remain stable independent of the certification horizon and in the non-Markovian setting, whilst DP-based certificates silently go unsound -- confirmed in simulation on a neural-controlled quadrotor.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a non-recursive, kernel-embedding based method to certify safety probabilities of uncertain dynamical systems directly from trajectory data, avoiding error compounding over long horizons and handling non-Markovian dynamics.
AI.motivation: Traditional safety certification methods for dynamical systems rely on dynamic programming over estimated transition models, which causes compounding error as the time horizon grows, often making safety guarantees vacuous and inapplicable to long-horizon or non-Markovian systems. There is a need for a stable, data-driven certification approach that can provide reliable safety guarantees over long horizons and for systems with memory or complex controllers (e.g., neural networks).
AI.method: The paper introduces a kernel embedding framework that recasts safety certification as a supervised classification problem on trajectory data. Instead of recursively computing safety probabilities via dynamic programming on an estimated model, it directly learns a mapping from initial conditions (and possibly histories) to the T-step safety indicator using kernel methods. This framework unifies and generalizes prior approaches such as barrier certificates and robust Markov models as specific instances. The learned classifier implicitly estimates the T-step safety probability without relying on Markovian assumptions.
AI.result: Theoretical analysis shows that the proposed direct estimation avoids the accumulation of approximation errors across time steps and remains well-behaved as the horizon T increases, unlike DP-based approaches whose lower bounds can become vacuous. The framework is shown to accommodate non-Markovian dynamics. Empirical simulations on a neural-network-controlled quadrotor demonstrate that the direct kernel-based certificates remain stable and sound for large horizons and in non-Markovian settings, whereas DP-based certificates can silently become unsound or overly conservative.
AI.conclusion: Treating safety certification as a direct classification problem with kernel embeddings provides a robust alternative to model-based dynamic programming approaches. It eliminates error compounding over long horizons, supports non-Markovian dynamics, and encompasses several classical certification methods as special cases. This leads to more reliable and scalable safety guarantees for complex, data-driven control systems.

title: Shallow Prefill, Deep Decoding: Efficient Long-Context Inference via Layer-Asymmetric KV Visibility
summary: Long-context inference in decoder-only language models is costly because long prompts are processed during Prefill, cached at every layer, and repeatedly attended to during autoregressive Decode. We introduce \emph{Shallow Prefill, dEEp Decode} (SPEED), a phase-asymmetric KV-visibility policy that materializes non-anchor prompt-token KV states only in lower layers while keeping Decode-phase tokens full-depth. Unlike previous approaches that make upper-layer prompt KV states cheaper to store or construct, SPEED removes prefill tokens from the upper-layer Decode visibility set altogether. With a minimal BoS anchor, this simple change preserves broad benchmark quality while reducing long-context cost. In a controlled Llama-3.1-8B instruction-tuning study, SPEED using only 75\% of layers for prefill tokens reaches 51.2 average score on OLMES-style benchmarks, compared with 51.4 for the full-depth baseline, while improving TTFT by 33\%, TPOT by 22\%, and reducing active KV memory by 25.0\% at 128K context. Layer-wise diagnostics suggest that this cutoff retains the main prompt-selection and representation-stabilization regions of the full-depth model. These results show that long-context prompt tokens need not always persist as full-depth KV-cache objects when Decode-phase tokens remain full-depth.

AI.tldr: Proposes SPEED, a method to cut long-context inference costs in decoder-only LMs by only caching long prompts in lower layers, keeping decode tokens full-depth, with almost no quality loss.
AI.motivation: Long-context inference is expensive because every prompt token is processed and cached at every layer and then repeatedly attended to during decoding. Existing methods focus on making upper-layer prompt KV states cheaper but still visible. The authors want to reduce compute, memory, and latency for long-context use without hurting model quality.
AI.method: Introduce SPEED (Shallow Prefill, dEEp Decode), a phase-asymmetric key–value (KV) visibility policy: during prefill, non-anchor prompt tokens are only materialized in a subset of lower layers, while decode-phase tokens keep full-depth KV states. A minimal beginning-of-sequence (BoS) anchor remains visible at all layers. They vary the prefill depth (e.g., 75% of layers) and evaluate on a controlled Llama-3.1-8B instruction-tuned model at 128K context, measuring quality benchmarks and inference metrics. They also conduct layer-wise diagnostics to identify functional regions like prompt selection and representation stabilization.
AI.result: With only 75% of layers used for prompt-token KV states, SPEED achieves 51.2 average score on OLMES-style benchmarks vs. 51.4 for the full-depth baseline—essentially preserving quality. It improves time-to-first-token (TTFT) by 33%, time-per-output-token (TPOT) by 22%, and cuts active KV memory by 25% at 128K context. Diagnostics show that the selected lower layers cover key functional regions of the model.
AI.conclusion: Long-context prompt tokens do not need to persist as full-depth KV-cache entries across all layers, as long as decode tokens remain full-depth and a minimal anchor is preserved. A shallow prefill with deep decode (SPEED) significantly reduces compute and memory costs for long-context inference while maintaining nearly unchanged benchmark performance.

title: CrossCult-KIBench: A Benchmark for Cross-Cultural Knowledge Insertion in MLLMs
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs), trained primarily on English-centric data, frequently generate culturally inappropriate or misaligned responses in cross-cultural settings. To mitigate this, we introduce the task of cross-cultural knowledge insertion, which focuses on adapting models to specific cultural contexts while preserving their original behavior in other cultures. To facilitate research in this area, we introduce CrossCult-KIBench, a comprehensive evaluation benchmark for assessing both the effectiveness of knowledge insertion and its unintended side effects on non-target cultures. The benchmark includes 9,800 image-grounded cases covering 49 culturally relevant visual scenarios across English, Chinese, and Arabic language-culture groups. It supports evaluation in both single-insert and sequential-insert settings. We also propose Memory-Conditioned Knowledge Insertion (MCKI) as a baseline method. MCKI retrieves relevant cultural knowledge from an external memory using frozen MLLM representations, prepending matched entries as conditional prompts when applicable. Extensive experiments on CrossCult-KIBench reveal that current approaches struggle to balance effective cultural adaptation with behavioral preservation, highlighting a key challenge in developing culturally-aware MLLMs. Our work thus underscores an important research direction for developing more culturally adaptive and responsible MLLMs.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a new task and benchmark for inserting culture-specific knowledge into multimodal large language models while preserving their behavior in other cultures, and proposes a baseline method using external memory retrieval.
AI.motivation: Existing multimodal large language models are mainly trained on English-centric data and often produce culturally inappropriate or misaligned outputs when used in other cultural contexts. There is a need to systematically adapt these models to specific cultures without harming their performance or behavior in non-target cultures, and to rigorously evaluate both the benefits and side effects of such adaptation.
AI.method: The authors define the task of cross-cultural knowledge insertion and build CrossCult-KIBench, a benchmark with 9,800 image-grounded examples that span 49 culturally relevant visual scenarios across English, Chinese, and Arabic language-culture groups. The benchmark supports both single knowledge insertion and sequential insertion settings. They also propose Memory-Conditioned Knowledge Insertion (MCKI) as a baseline, which uses frozen MLLM representations to retrieve relevant entries from an external cultural knowledge memory and prepends these as conditional prompts when appropriate.
AI.result: Experiments on CrossCult-KIBench show that existing approaches, including the proposed baseline, have difficulty simultaneously achieving strong cultural adaptation for the target culture and maintaining the original model behavior for non-target cultures. Performance trade-offs and side effects are consistently observed across the evaluated settings and language-culture groups.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that current methods are insufficient for robust, side-effect-free cultural adaptation of MLLMs. CrossCult-KIBench provides a standardized way to study this problem, and the observed trade-offs highlight cross-cultural knowledge insertion as a key open challenge and research direction for building more culturally adaptive and responsible multimodal language models.

title: Back to the Beginning of Heuristic Design: Bridging Code and Knowledge with LLMs
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have recently advanced automatic heuristic design (AHD) for combinatorial optimization (CO), where candidate heuristics are iteratively proposed, evaluated, and refined. Most existing approaches search over executable programs and distill insights from execution feedback to guide later iterations. Because this process moves from low-level implementations to high-level principles, we refer to it as a bottom-up paradigm. We argue that this view is incomplete and introduce a complementary top-down perspective: knowledge becomes the primary search object and code merely instantiates and tests it, making what is learned explicit and reusable across problems and trajectories. We formalize this shift through a statistical-learning view that exposes a distortion--compression trade-off, and instantiate it in both population-based and tree-based AHD frameworks. Across CO and tasks beyond it, knowledge-first search improves discovery efficiency, transfer, and generalization, often outperforming code-centric pipelines, while combining both strategies yields further gains. Our results suggest that progress in AHD depends on iteratively constructing and evolving interpretable hypotheses that retain value beyond a single search trajectory.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a new “knowledge-first” (top-down) paradigm for using large language models to design heuristics for combinatorial optimization, showing it improves efficiency, transfer, and generalization over existing code-centric (bottom-up) approaches, especially when combined with them.
AI.motivation: Existing automatic heuristic design methods with LLMs mostly search directly over executable code and use run-time feedback to refine heuristics. This bottom-up approach often buries useful high-level knowledge inside specific implementations, limiting reusability, interpretability, and transfer across problems or search trajectories. The authors are motivated to make the learned knowledge explicit and central so that it can be shared, generalized, and reused, ultimately improving the efficiency and robustness of heuristic discovery.
AI.method: The authors recast automatic heuristic design in a statistical-learning framework that highlights a distortion–compression trade-off in representing problem-solving knowledge. They then redesign AHD pipelines so that the primary search objects are explicit, interpretable knowledge structures (e.g., principles, strategies, or rules), with code serving as an instantiation layer to test those hypotheses. They instantiate this “knowledge-first” view in both population-based and tree-based AHD frameworks, modifying how candidate heuristics are generated, evaluated, refined, and stored to prioritize reusable knowledge rather than just executable programs.
AI.result: Across a range of combinatorial optimization tasks and some tasks beyond CO, the knowledge-first search approach discovers effective heuristics more efficiently than purely code-centric pipelines, shows better transfer of heuristics between problems, and achieves stronger generalization to new instances. In empirical comparisons, it often outperforms traditional bottom-up, code-focused AHD, and using both paradigms together (combining knowledge-first and code-centric search) yields additional performance gains.
AI.conclusion: Automatic heuristic design with LLMs benefits from elevating explicit, interpretable knowledge to the primary search target, with executable code as a vehicle for hypothesis testing. Viewing AHD through a statistical-learning lens clarifies the trade-offs involved and motivates designs that reduce distortion while retaining useful compression. Progress in AHD is likely to depend on iteratively building and evolving structured hypotheses that remain valuable across problems and search trajectories, and hybridizing top-down (knowledge-first) and bottom-up (code-centric) strategies appears especially promising.

title: P-Guide: Parameter-Efficient Prior Steering for Single-Pass CFG Inference
summary: Classifier-Free Guidance (CFG) is essential for high-fidelity conditional generation in flow matching, yet it imposes significant computational overhead by requiring dual forward passes at each sampling step. In this work, we address this bottleneck by introducing \textbf{P-Guide}, a framework that achieves high-quality guidance through a single inference pass by modulating only the initial latent state. We further show that, under a first-order approximation, P-Guide is equivalent to CFG in the sense that it steers generation from the prior space, without requiring explicit velocity field extrapolation during sampling. We consider both homoscedastic and \textbf{heteroscedastic} priors, and find that jointly modeling the mean and variance enables adaptive loss attenuation and improved robustness to data uncertainty. Extensive experiments demonstrate that P-Guide reduces inference latency by approximately 50\% while maintaining fidelity and prompt alignment competitive with standard dual-pass CFG baselines.

AI.tldr: P-Guide is a single-pass guidance framework for flow matching that matches CFG performance while halving inference cost.
AI.motivation: Classifier-Free Guidance gives strong conditional generation in flow-matching models but doubles computation because it needs both conditional and unconditional passes every step. The paper aims to retain CFG-level quality while removing this dual-pass bottleneck.
AI.method: They introduce P-Guide, which injects guidance by modulating only the initial latent state so that subsequent evolution uses a single conditional pass. Using a first-order approximation, they show this initial-state modulation is theoretically equivalent to CFG’s effect of steering trajectories in prior space, but without per-step velocity extrapolation. They further extend the prior from homoscedastic to heteroscedastic, jointly learning mean and variance to adaptively attenuate loss where data is uncertain.
AI.result: Experiments show that P-Guide can cut inference latency by about 50% compared to standard dual-pass CFG, while preserving image fidelity and prompt alignment that are on par with those baselines.
AI.conclusion: P-Guide provides a theoretically grounded, computationally efficient alternative to CFG for flow matching, enabling single-pass guided sampling with similar generation quality and better robustness via heteroscedastic priors.

title: Skill1: Unified Evolution of Skill-Augmented Agents via Reinforcement Learning
summary: A persistent skill library allows language model agents to reuse successful strategies across tasks. Maintaining such a library requires three coupled capabilities. The agent selects a relevant skill, utilizes it during execution, and distills new skills from experience. Existing methods optimize these capabilities in isolation or with separate reward sources, resulting in partial and conflicting evolution. We propose Skill1, a framework that trains a single policy to co-evolve skill selection, utilization, and distillation toward a shared task-outcome objective. The policy generates a query to search the skill library, re-ranks candidates to select one, solves the task conditioned on it, and distills a new skill from the trajectory. All learning derives from a single task-outcome signal. Its low-frequency trend credits selection and its high-frequency variation credits distillation. Experiments on ALFWorld and WebShop show that Skill1 outperforms prior skill-based and reinforcement learning baselines. Training dynamics confirm the co-evolution of the three capabilities, and ablations show that removing any credit signal degrades the evolution.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Skill1, a unified framework that lets language model agents build and use a persistent skill library by jointly learning how to select, apply, and distill skills from task experience using a single task-outcome signal.
AI.motivation: Existing language-model-based agents can store and reuse skills, but current approaches treat skill selection, use, and creation as separate problems, often with different objectives or rewards. This fragmentation leads to partial, sometimes conflicting learning signals and prevents the skill library and agent policy from co-evolving optimally toward better task performance. The paper aims to design a principled way for these three processes to evolve together under one coherent objective so that agents can more reliably accumulate and exploit reusable skills across tasks.
AI.method: The authors propose Skill1, a framework where a single policy governs four steps: (1) generate a query to search a persistent skill library; (2) re-rank retrieved skills and select one; (3) solve the current task while conditioned on the selected skill; and (4) distill a new candidate skill from the resulting trajectory. Learning is driven solely by a scalar task-outcome signal (e.g., success/failure or reward). They interpret the low-frequency component of this signal as credit for improving skill selection and the high-frequency component as credit for refining skill distillation, enabling co-evolution without separate rewards. This is instantiated and trained in RL environments such as ALFWorld and WebShop, with appropriate implementation of the search, conditioning, and distillation procedures for the skill library.
AI.result: On the ALFWorld and WebShop benchmarks, Skill1 achieves better task performance than prior skill-based architectures and standard reinforcement learning baselines. Empirical training curves and diagnostics indicate that skill selection, utilization, and distillation all improve over time in a mutually reinforcing way. Ablation studies where parts of the credit assignment (e.g., for selection or distillation) are removed show clear performance drops, supporting the claim that the joint, shared-objective training is crucial for effective skill library evolution.
AI.conclusion: Skill1 demonstrates that a single, task-outcome-driven policy can successfully coordinate skill selection, application, and distillation for language model agents. By leveraging a unified learning signal and framing different temporal components of this signal as credit for different sub-processes, the framework enables coherent co-evolution of the agent and its skill library. This leads to superior performance over methods that decouple these components or rely on separate reward sources, suggesting a promising direction for building more autonomous, continually improving language model agents.

title: Graphlets as Building Blocks for Structural Vocabulary in Knowledge Graph Foundation Models
summary: Foundation models excel at language, where sentences become tokens, and vision, where images become pixels, because both reduce to discrete symbols on a shared, fixed grid. Knowledge Graphs share the discreteness, but not the geometry. Their entities and relations are discrete symbols, yet their arrangement is relational and lacks a common, fixed grid. Knowledge Graphs (KGs) share the discreteness, but not the geometry. They form irregular, non-Euclidean topologies whose local neighborhoods differ from graph to graph. Therefore, Knowledge Graph Foundation Models (KGFMs) rely on identifying structural invariances to produce transferable representations. Without a universal token set, KGFMs are limited in their ability to transfer representations across unseen KGs. We close this gap by treating graphlets, small connected graphs, as structural tokens that recur in heterogeneous KGs. In this paper, We introduce a model-agnostic framework based on a vocabulary of graphlets that mines a KG between relations via pattern matching. In particular, we considered closed and open 2- and 3-path, and star graphlets, to obtain robust invariances. The framework is evaluated on 51 KGs from a wide range of domains, for zero-shot inductive and transductive link prediction. Experiments show that adding simple graphlets to the vocabulary yields models that outperform prior KGFMs.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes treating small graph patterns (graphlets) as reusable structural tokens to build more transferable Knowledge Graph Foundation Models (KGFMs), improving zero‑shot link prediction across many heterogeneous graphs.
AI.motivation: Existing foundation models work well for text and images because they rely on discrete tokens laid out on a shared, fixed grid (words in sequences, pixels in images). Knowledge graphs are also discrete but lack such a universal geometry: their entities and relations form irregular, non-Euclidean structures with varying local neighborhoods. Current KGFMs must infer structural invariances graph by graph and struggle to transfer representations to unseen KGs because there is no universal tokenization scheme. The paper aims to create a common, reusable vocabulary for graphs that enables better cross-KG transfer, particularly for zero‑shot scenarios.
AI.method: The authors propose a model-agnostic framework that introduces a vocabulary of graphlets—small connected subgraphs—as structural tokens that recur across heterogeneous knowledge graphs. They focus on specific types of graphlets, including closed and open 2-paths, 3-paths, and star patterns. These graphlets are mined from each KG via pattern matching between relations, effectively re-encoding parts of the KG in terms of graphlet occurrences. This graphlet-based tokenization can then be plugged into different KGFMs to learn representations that capture structural invariances shared across graphs.
AI.result: On 51 diverse knowledge graphs, the framework is evaluated for both zero-shot inductive and transductive link prediction. Empirical results show that incorporating these simple graphlet tokens into the model vocabulary leads to improved performance over previous state-of-the-art Knowledge Graph Foundation Models across these tasks.
AI.conclusion: By using graphlets as structural tokens, the proposed framework provides a universal, reusable vocabulary for knowledge graphs, narrowing the gap between KG modeling and language/vision foundation models that benefit from shared token spaces. Even a small set of simple graphlets suffices to capture robust structural invariances, enabling better cross-graph transfer and improving zero-shot link prediction performance. The approach is flexible and model-agnostic, suggesting it can be integrated into a wide range of KGFMs.

title: Beyond Accuracy: Policy Invariance as a Reliability Test for LLM Safety Judges
summary: LLM-as-a-Judge pipelines have become the de facto evaluator for agent safety, yet existing benchmarks treat their verdicts as ground-truth proxies without checking whether the verdicts depend on the agent's behavior or merely on how the evaluation policy happens to be worded. We argue that any trustworthy safety judge must satisfy a basic property we call policy invariance, and we operationalize it as three testable principles: rubric-semantics invariance under certified-equivalent rewrites, rubric-threshold invariance under intentional strict-to-lenient shifts, and ambiguity-aware calibration so that verdict instability concentrates on genuinely ambiguous cases. Instantiating these principles as a stress-test protocol with four agent-class judges on trajectories drawn from ASSEBench and R-Judge, we surface a previously unmeasured failure mode: today's judges respond to meaningful normative shifts and to meaningless structural rewrites with comparable strength, and cannot tell the two apart. Content-preserving policy rewrites flip up to 9.1% of verdicts above baseline jitter, and 18-43% of all observed flips occur on unambiguous cases under such rewrites, so existing safety scores conflate what the agent did with how the evaluator was prompted. Beyond the diagnosis, we contribute the Policy Invariance Score and the Judge Card reporting protocol, which expose an order-of-magnitude spread in judge reliability that is invisible to accuracy-only leaderboards. We release the protocol and code so that future agent-safety benchmarks can audit their own evaluators rather than trust them by default.

AI.tldr: This paper examines whether LLM-as-a-Judge safety evaluators are robust to how evaluation rubrics are worded and introduces a "policy invariance" requirement and metrics to measure it.
AI.motivation: LLM-based judges are widely used to evaluate agent safety, but current benchmarks assume their verdicts are reliable ground truth. There is a gap: no one has rigorously tested whether these verdicts actually depend on the agent behavior versus superficial changes in the evaluation prompt or policy description. This missing analysis risks misleading safety scores and benchmark results.
AI.method: The authors formalize a desideratum called policy invariance, breaking it into three testable principles: (1) rubric-semantics invariance under certified-equivalent rewrites, (2) rubric-threshold invariance under controlled strict-to-lenient shifts in the policy, and (3) ambiguity-aware calibration, where verdict instability should primarily occur for genuinely ambiguous cases. They implement a stress-test protocol and apply it to four different LLM-based agent-class judges, using trajectories from ASSEBench and R-Judge. They systematically rewrite evaluation policies to be content-preserving or to intentionally vary strictness, then measure how often and where verdicts change.
AI.result: The experiments reveal that current LLM judges are highly sensitive both to meaningful normative changes and to purely structural, content-preserving rewrites of the rubric. Content-preserving rewrites cause up to 9.1% of verdicts to flip beyond baseline randomness, and 18–43% of these flips occur on cases that should be unambiguous. This shows that existing safety scores are confounded by how the evaluation prompt is phrased, not just by the underlying agent behavior.
AI.conclusion: LLM-as-a-Judge systems used for agent safety evaluation do not satisfy the proposed policy invariance criteria and thus cannot be blindly trusted as ground-truth evaluators. The paper introduces the Policy Invariance Score and the Judge Card reporting protocol to quantify and report judge robustness, exposing large differences in judge reliability that accuracy metrics alone miss. The authors release their protocol and code to encourage future benchmarks to audit and report on judge invariance, rather than assuming evaluation stability by default.

title: BioMedArena: An Open-source Toolkit for Building and Evaluating Biomedical Deep Research Agents
summary: Building a deep research agent today is an exercise in glue code: the same backbone evaluated on the same benchmark can report different accuracies in different papers because harness and tool registry all differ, and integrating a new foundation model into a comparable evaluation surface costs weeks of model-specific engineering. We call this the per-paper engineering tax and release BioMedArena, an open-source toolkit that not only alleviates it but also provides an arena for fair comparison of different foundation models when evaluating them as deep-research agents. BioMedArena decouples six layers of biomedical agent evaluation -- benchmark loading, tool exposure, tool selection, execution mode, context management, and scoring -- and exposes 147 biomedical benchmarks and 75 biomedical tools across 9 functional families. Adding a new model, benchmark, or tool reduces to registering a few-line provider adapter. We further provide 6 agent harnesses with 6 context-management strategies, which provide 12 backbones with competitive research capabilities and significantly improved performance, achieving state-of-the-art (SOTA) results on 8 representative biomedical benchmarks, with an average lift of +15.03 percentage points over prior SOTA. The toolkit, configurations, and per-task traces are available at https://github.com/AI-in-Health/BioMedArena

AI.tldr: BioMedArena is an open-source toolkit that standardizes and simplifies the evaluation of deep biomedical research agents, enabling fair comparison and easy integration of new models, benchmarks, and tools, and achieving new SOTA results.
AI.motivation: Researchers face a 'per-paper engineering tax' where each paper uses its own glue code, harness, and tool registry, making results non-comparable and integration of new models very time-consuming. There is a need for a unified, modular, and extensible framework to fairly evaluate and compare deep-research agents, especially in biomedicine.
AI.method: The authors design BioMedArena as a modular evaluation toolkit that decouples six layers of biomedical agent evaluation: benchmark loading, tool exposure, tool selection, execution mode, context management, and scoring. They integrate 147 biomedical benchmarks and 75 biomedical tools across 9 functional families, and define simple provider adapters to add new models, benchmarks, or tools. They also implement 6 agent harnesses with 6 context-management strategies, yielding 12 backbones for biomedical research agents.
AI.result: BioMedArena exposes a large suite of benchmarks and tools via a standardized interface and allows easy registration of new components. Using the provided harnesses and context-management strategies, the authors demonstrate 12 agent backbones with competitive research capabilities that achieve state-of-the-art performance on 8 representative biomedical benchmarks, with an average improvement of +15.03 percentage points over previous SOTA.
AI.conclusion: BioMedArena effectively removes much of the per-paper engineering tax in biomedical agent research, offering a unified and extensible arena for fair comparison of foundation models as deep-research agents. Its modular design, extensive benchmark and tool coverage, and strong empirical results suggest it can serve as a standard platform for future biomedical AI research and evaluation.

title: Event-Causal RAG: A Retrieval-Augmented Generation Framework for Long Video Reasoning in Complex Scenarios
summary: Recent large vision-language models have achieved strong performance on short- and medium-length video understanding, yet they remain inadequate for ultra-long or even infinite video reasoning, where models must preserve coherent memory over extended durations and infer causal dependencies across temporally distant events. Existing end-to-end video understanding methods are fundamentally limited by the $O(n^2)$ complexity of self-attention, while recent retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) approaches still suffer from fragmented clip-level memory, weak modeling of temporal and causal structure, and high storage and online inference costs. We present Event-Causal RAG, a lightweight retrieval-augmented framework for infinite long-video reasoning. Instead of indexing fixed-length clips, our method segments streaming videos into semantically coherent events and represents each event as a structured State-Event-State (SES) graph, capturing the event together with its surrounding state transitions. These graphs are merged into a global Event Knowledge Graph and stored in a dual-store memory that supports both semantic matching and causal-topological retrieval. On top of this memory, we design a bidirectional retrieval strategy to efficiently identify the most relevant event causal chains and provide them, together with the associated video evidence, to a backbone video foundation model for answer generation. Experiments on long-video understanding benchmarks demonstrate that Event-Causal RAG consistently outperforms strong clip-based retrieval baselines and long-context video models, particularly on questions requiring multi-event integration and causal inference across long temporal gaps, while also achieving improved memory efficiency and robust streaming performance.

AI.tldr: Proposes Event-Causal RAG, a retrieval-augmented framework for infinite/ultra-long video reasoning using event-level causal graphs instead of fixed clips.
AI.motivation: Existing large vision-language models and RAG methods handle only short to medium videos well and fail on ultra-long or streaming videos. Their limitations stem from quadratic attention cost, fragmented clip-level memories, poor modeling of long-range temporal and causal relations, and high storage and inference cost. There is a need for a scalable, memory-efficient approach that can maintain coherent long-term memory and perform causal reasoning across distant events in very long videos.
AI.method: Segment a streaming video into semantically coherent events and represent each as a structured State-Event-State (SES) graph that encodes the event and its surrounding state transitions. Incrementally merge these SES graphs into a global Event Knowledge Graph stored in a dual-store memory supporting both semantic similarity search and causal/topological retrieval over the graph. Use a bidirectional retrieval strategy to trace relevant causal chains in this graph for a given query, fetch associated video evidence, and feed these to a backbone video foundation model within a RAG framework for answer generation.
AI.result: On long-video understanding benchmarks, Event-Causal RAG consistently outperforms strong clip-based retrieval baselines and long-context video models, especially on tasks that require integrating multiple events and performing causal reasoning over long temporal intervals. It also shows better memory efficiency and robust performance in streaming settings.
AI.conclusion: Event-Causal RAG provides an effective, lightweight, and memory-efficient solution for infinite or ultra-long video reasoning by organizing video content into event-level causal graphs and using specialized retrieval over this structure, yielding superior performance on long-range, multi-event, and causal understanding tasks compared to existing methods.

title: Systematic Evaluation of Large Language Models for Post-Discharge Clinical Action Extraction
summary: The work in this paper evaluates zero-shot and few-shot large language models (LLMs) for safety-critical clinical action extraction using the CLIP discharge-note dataset, with particular emphasis on transitions of care and post-discharge patient safety. To manage the complexity of clinical documentation, we introduce a two-stage extraction framework that decomposes discharge notes, that are written in narrative form, into fine-grained, explicitly actionable clinical tasks through a staged prompting strategy. Our contributions include a systematic assessment of generative LLMs for clinical action extraction, a detailed comparison between general-purpose LLMs and task-specific supervised BERT-based models, and an analysis of annotation inconsistencies across different action categories. We show that contemporary LLMs achieve performance comparable to or exceeding supervised models on binary actionability detection, while supervised baselines retain a meaningful advantage on fine-grained multi-label category classification, despite the absence of task-specific fine-tuning and under strict data-privacy constraints. Qualitative error analysis reveals that many failures stem from misalignment between model reasoning and dataset annotation conventions, particularly in cases involving implicit clinical actions and rigid structural labeling rules. These results indicate that reported performance reflects model limitations due to lack of clinical reasoning, that is not captured by plain annotations. Labels without rationales make it impossible to distinguish clinical reasoning failures from annotation convention mismatches. Advancing clinical NLP requires reasoning-annotated datasets that document why specific spans are actionable, not merely which spans were labeled, enabling proper evaluation of model clinical understanding.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates how well large language models can extract actionable clinical tasks from discharge notes using a two-stage prompting framework, comparing them with supervised BERT models and highlighting that lack of reasoning-focused annotations limits proper evaluation of clinical understanding.
AI.motivation: Clinical discharge notes are long, complex, and narrative, yet downstream care and patient safety require accurately extracting concrete follow-up actions. Existing clinical NLP systems typically rely on supervised models trained on labeled spans but do not fully leverage or assess the clinical reasoning abilities of modern LLMs, and current datasets lack rationale-style annotations that could distinguish model reasoning errors from annotation idiosyncrasies.
AI.method: The authors use the CLIP discharge-note dataset and propose a two-stage extraction framework where LLMs first decompose narrative discharge notes into candidate actions and then refine these into fine-grained, explicitly actionable tasks via staged prompting. They systematically evaluate zero-shot and few-shot LLMs on binary actionability detection and multi-label action-category classification, and compare their performance with task-specific supervised BERT-based baselines while also conducting qualitative error analysis of annotation inconsistencies and model failures across action categories.
AI.result: LLMs perform on par with or better than supervised BERT-based models in detecting whether a span is actionable at a binary level but lag behind on detailed, multi-label categorization of action types. Many apparent errors by LLMs are actually attributable to inconsistencies or rigid conventions in the dataset’s annotations, especially for implicit actions and structurally constrained labels, complicating interpretation of performance gaps.
AI.conclusion: Current generative LLMs show strong potential for clinical action extraction in safety-critical settings, particularly for identifying actionable content, but their evaluation is confounded by limitations of existing annotation schemes. Because labels lack explanations of why text is actionable, it is difficult to distinguish genuine clinical reasoning deficits from mismatches with annotation rules. The authors argue that next-generation clinical NLP resources should include reasoning-annotated datasets that capture the underlying clinical rationale for actionability, allowing more faithful assessment and development of models’ clinical understanding.

title: Towards Annotation-Free Validation of MLLMs: A Vision-Language Logical Consistency Metric
summary: Dominant accuracy evaluation might reward unwarranted guessing of Large Language Models, and it might not be applicable to novel tasks for model validation without ground-truth (gt) annotation. Based on basic logic principle, we propose a novel framework to evaluate the vision-language logical consistency of MLLMs on both sufficient and necessary cause-effect relations. We define Vision-Language Logical Consistency Metric (VL-LCM) on traditional MC-VQA tests, and recent NaturalBench tests without the need for gt annotation. Through systematic experiments on representative VL benchmark MMMU and recent VL challenges like NaturalBench, we evaluated 11 recent open-source MLLMs from 4 frontier families. Our findings reveal that, despite significant progress of recent MLLMs on accuracy, logical consistency lags behind significantly. Extensive evaluations on the correlations of VL-LCM with metrics on gt, the reliability of LCM, and the relation of VL-LCM with response distribution justify the validity and applicability of VL-LCM even without gt annotation. Our findings suggest that, beyond accuracy, logical consistency could be employed for both accuracy and reliability. VL-LCM can also be employed for MLLM selection, validation, and reliable answer justification in novel tasks without gt annotation.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces VL-LCM, a metric to evaluate logical consistency of multimodal large language models (MLLMs) on vision-language tasks, including settings without ground-truth labels, and shows that current models remain logically inconsistent despite strong accuracy.
AI.motivation: Conventional accuracy metrics can encourage guesswork in Large Language Models and cannot be applied when ground-truth annotations are missing, especially for novel tasks. There is a need for a more principled way to evaluate how logically consistent MLLM outputs are with visual and textual inputs, and to do so even when ground truth is unavailable.
AI.method: The authors formalize vision-language logical consistency based on principles of sufficient and necessary cause-effect relations between visual content, language input, and model outputs. They define a Vision-Language Logical Consistency Metric (VL-LCM) that can be applied to multiple-choice VQA benchmarks (e.g., MMMU) and newer benchmarks like NaturalBench, including in a ground-truth-free setting. They systematically evaluate 11 recent open-source MLLMs from four major model families and analyze how VL-LCM behaves, its correlation with ground-truth-based metrics, and its relation to response distributions.
AI.result: Experiments on MMMU and NaturalBench reveal that MLLMs that achieve high accuracy can still exhibit poor vision-language logical consistency, indicating a gap between correctness and logical reasoning. The VL-LCM metric shows meaningful correlations with traditional ground-truth-based metrics, demonstrates reliability, and provides informative patterns when examined with respect to models’ answer distributions.
AI.conclusion: Logical consistency is a distinct and important dimension of MLLM performance that is not captured by accuracy alone. VL-LCM offers a practical and validated tool to assess this property, including for novel tasks where ground-truth annotations are unavailable. It can be used for model selection, validation, and generating more reliable answer justifications, helping to move evaluation beyond pure accuracy and reducing reward for unwarranted guessing.

title: Beyond Fixed Benchmarks and Worst-Case Attacks: Dynamic Boundary Evaluation for Language Models
summary: Evaluating large language models (LLMs) today rests on fixed benchmarks that apply the same set of items to any model, producing ceiling and floor effects that mask capability gaps. We argue that the most informative evaluation signal lies at the boundary, where the per-prompt pass probability is near $0.5$ under random-sampling decoding, and propose Dynamic Boundary Evaluation (DBE), which actively locates each model's boundary and places it on a globally comparable difficulty scale. DBE delivers three artifacts: (i) a calibrated item bank covering safety, capability, and truthfulness, with per-item difficulty labels validated across $9$ reference LLMs; (ii) Skill-Guided Boundary Search (SGBS), a search algorithm that finds boundary items for a given target LLM using only API-level query access; and (iii) an evaluation protocol that places a new LLM on a unified ability scale and grows the evaluation set adaptively when the target falls outside the bank's coverage. We instantiate DBE on four categories spanning safety (harmful request refusal and over-refusal), capability (constrained instruction following), and truthfulness (multi-turn sycophancy resistance). The resulting evaluation covers a broader model spectrum without saturation while remaining compatible with existing datasets.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Dynamic Boundary Evaluation (DBE), a new way to evaluate LLMs by focusing on prompts where models are about 50% likely to succeed, avoiding ceiling and floor effects.
AI.motivation: Fixed benchmarks often make powerful models look maxed out and weaker models look uniformly bad, hiding nuanced capability differences. The authors want an evaluation method that adapts to each model, yet stays on a shared, comparable scale.
AI.method: They build DBE, which consists of: (1) creating a calibrated bank of evaluation items labeled with difficulty, based on performance across 9 reference LLMs; (2) introducing Skill-Guided Boundary Search (SGBS), an algorithm that, using only API access, adaptively searches for items near the 50% success boundary of a target LLM; and (3) an adaptive evaluation protocol that places any new LLM on a unified ability scale and extends the item bank when the model’s ability lies outside the current difficulty range.
AI.result: DBE is instantiated on four evaluation categories (two safety, one capability, one truthfulness), and the authors show it can evaluate a wide range of models without saturation, while staying compatible with existing datasets. The difficulty labels are validated across 9 LLMs, indicating that the scale is stable and comparable.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic, boundary-focused evaluation provides a more informative and scalable way to compare LLMs across safety, capability, and truthfulness than static benchmarks, by centering evaluation on each model’s performance boundary and mapping it to a global difficulty scale.

title: Joint Consistency: A Unified Test-Time Aggregation Framework via Energy Minimization
summary: This paper studies test-time aggregation, an approach that generates multiple reasoning traces and aggregates them into a final answer. Most existing methods rely on evaluation signals collected from candidate traces in isolation or answer frequencies, while ignoring comparative interactions among candidates. We propose Joint Consistency (JC), formulated as a constrained Ising-type energy minimization problem, where independent evaluation signals act as external fields and pairwise comparisons act as interactions. JC provides a unified framework for test-time aggregation that subsumes existing voting and weighted aggregation methods as special cases. Our construction of the interaction matrix leverages LLM-as-a-judge comparisons, and admits a theoretical interpretation under answer-level homogeneity assumptions. Moreover, we develop an efficient approximation strategy that makes interaction modeling practical for large-scale test-time aggregation. Experiments on math and code reasoning benchmarks show that JC consistently outperforms existing baselines across tasks, judge models, trace budgets, and trace-generation settings.

AI.tldr: They propose Joint Consistency (JC), an Ising-style energy minimization approach to aggregate multiple reasoning traces at test time using both individual scores and pairwise comparisons, achieving better math and code reasoning performance than existing aggregation methods.
AI.motivation: Test-time aggregation methods for LLM reasoning typically either count answer frequencies or score each trace independently, failing to exploit rich comparative information between candidate traces. There is a need for a unified, principled framework that can incorporate both per-trace evaluation signals and pairwise interactions to improve answer selection from multiple reasoning traces, particularly for complex math and code tasks.
AI.method: They introduce Joint Consistency (JC), which formulates test-time aggregation as a constrained Ising-type energy minimization problem. Each candidate reasoning trace receives an individual evaluation score, modeled as an external field, and pairwise LLM-as-a-judge comparisons define interaction terms in an interaction matrix. Existing voting and weighted aggregation become special cases under particular parameterizations. They also design an efficient approximation algorithm to solve the energy minimization so that modeling interactions remains tractable at large scale.
AI.result: On math and code reasoning benchmarks, JC is empirically evaluated under varying conditions: different tasks, judge models, numbers of generated traces (trace budgets), and trace generation schemes. Across all these settings, JC yields higher accuracy than standard baselines such as majority voting and weighted aggregation that lack explicit interaction modeling.
AI.conclusion: Modeling joint consistency between reasoning traces via an Ising-style energy framework that combines individual evaluation scores with pairwise LLM-judged comparisons leads to a more powerful and unified test-time aggregation method. JC generalizes existing approaches, is computationally feasible through approximation, and consistently improves performance on challenging reasoning benchmarks.

title: Proactive Instance Navigation with Comparative Judgment for Ambiguous User Queries
summary: Natural-language instance navigation becomes challenging when the initial user request does not uniquely specify the target instance. A practical agent should reduce the user's burden by actively asking only the information needed to distinguish the target from similar distractors, rather than requiring a detailed description upfront. Existing approaches often fall short of this goal: they may stop at the first plausible candidate before sufficiently exploring alternatives, or, even after collecting multiple candidates, ask about the target's attributes derived from individual candidates rather than questions selected to distinguish candidates in the pool. As a result, despite the dialogue, the agent may still fail to distinguish the target from distractors, leading to premature decisions and lengthy user responses. We propose Proactive Instance Navigation with Comparative Judgment (ProCompNav), a two-stage framework that first constructs a candidate pool and then identifies the target through comparative judgment. At each round, ProCompNav extracts an attribute-value pair that splits the current pool, asks a binary yes/no question, and prunes all inconsistent candidates at once. This reframes disambiguation from open-ended target description to pool-level discriminative questioning, where each question is chosen to narrow the candidate set. On CoIN-Bench, ProCompNav improves Success Rate over interactive baselines with the same minimal input and non-interactive baselines with detailed descriptions, while substantially reducing Response Length. ProCompNav also achieves state-of-the-art Success Rate on TextNav, suggesting that comparative judgment is broadly useful for instance-level navigation among similar distractors.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ProCompNav, a framework that improves natural-language instance navigation by asking discriminative yes/no questions that efficiently narrow down candidate instances.
AI.motivation: In natural-language instance navigation, initial user instructions often do not uniquely identify a target instance among many similar distractors. Existing agents either commit too early to a plausible candidate or ask attribute questions derived from individual candidates, failing to systematically distinguish among the full candidate set. This leads to premature or incorrect decisions and forces users to provide long, detailed descriptions, which is impractical in real applications.
AI.method: The authors propose ProCompNav, a two-stage framework. First, it constructs a pool of candidate instances that match the initial, potentially underspecified user request. Then, it performs comparative judgment over this pool. At each dialogue turn, ProCompNav selects an attribute-value pair that best splits the current candidate pool, formulates a binary yes/no question about it, and upon receiving the user's response, prunes all candidates inconsistent with the answer. This process reframes disambiguation as pool-level discriminative questioning rather than open-ended target description. The method is evaluated on CoIN-Bench and TextNav benchmarks.
AI.result: On CoIN-Bench, ProCompNav achieves higher Success Rate than interactive baselines that use the same minimal initial input, and also outperforms non-interactive baselines that rely on detailed user descriptions, while significantly reducing the average user response length. On TextNav, ProCompNav attains state-of-the-art Success Rate, indicating strong performance in instance-level navigation tasks with many similar distractors.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that framing instance disambiguation as comparative, pool-level questioning with binary judgments leads to more efficient and accurate natural-language instance navigation. ProCompNav reduces user effort and improves both success and efficiency compared to existing methods, and its gains on multiple benchmarks suggest that comparative judgment is a generally useful strategy for navigating among similar instances.

title: A Versatile AI Agent for Rare Disease Diagnosis and Risk Gene Prioritization
summary: Accurate and timely diagnosis is essential for effective treatment, particularly in the context of rare diseases. However, current diagnostic workflows often lead to prolonged assessment times and low accuracy. To address these limitations, we introduce Hygieia, a multi-modal AI agent system designed to support precision disease diagnosis by integrating diverse data sources, including phenotypic features, genetic profiles, and clinical records. Hygieia features a router-based and knowledge-enhanced framework that mitigates hallucination and tailors diagnostic strategies to different disease categories. Notably, it prioritizes risk-related genomic factors for rare diseases and provides confidence scores to assist clinical decision-making. We conducted a comprehensive evaluation demonstrating that Hygieia achieves state-of-the-art performance across multiple diagnostic benchmarks. In collaboration with clinical experts from Yale School of Medicine and Duke-NUS Medical School, we further validated its practical utility by showing (1) Hygieia's superior diagnostic performance compared to physicians with an improvement from 12%-60% and (2) its effectiveness in assisting clinicians with medical records for handling real-world cases. Our findings indicate that Hygieia not only enhances diagnostic accuracy and interpretability but also significantly reduces clinician workload, highlighting its potential as a valuable tool in clinical decision support systems.

AI.tldr: Hygieia is a multi-modal AI agent system that improves rare disease diagnosis by integrating diverse clinical data, using a knowledge-enhanced routing framework to reduce hallucinations and provide confidence-scored, interpretable results that outperform physicians and reduce workload.
AI.motivation: Current diagnostic workflows, especially for rare diseases, suffer from long assessment times and low accuracy. There is a need for AI systems that can efficiently integrate heterogeneous data types (phenotypes, genomics, records), reduce diagnostic errors and hallucinations, and provide clinically useful, interpretable support to physicians.
AI.method: The authors design Hygieia, a multi-modal AI agent framework that ingests phenotypic features, genetic profiles, and clinical records. It uses a router-based architecture to select diagnostic strategies appropriate to disease categories and a knowledge-enhanced mechanism to ground reasoning in medical knowledge, thereby mitigating hallucinations. It emphasizes risk-related genomic features for rare diseases and outputs diagnoses with confidence scores. They evaluate the system on multiple diagnostic benchmarks and through clinical collaborations with experts at two medical schools.
AI.result: Hygieia achieves state-of-the-art performance across several diagnostic benchmarks. In expert-led evaluations, it outperforms physicians, improving diagnostic performance in the range of 12%–60%, and proves effective in assisting clinicians working with real-world medical records and cases.
AI.conclusion: Hygieia substantially improves diagnostic accuracy and interpretability while reducing clinician workload. Its multi-modal, knowledge-enhanced, router-based design makes it a promising clinical decision support tool, particularly for precision diagnosis of rare diseases.

title: Price of Fairness in Short-Term and Long-Term Algorithmic Selections
comment: The short version of this paper appears in the proceedings of IJCAI-26
summary: Algorithmic decision-making in high-stakes settings can have profound impacts on individuals and populations. While much prior work studies fairness in static settings, recent results show that enforcing static fairness constraints may exacerbate long-run disparities. Motivated by this tension, we study a stylized sequential selection problem in which a decision-maker repeatedly selects individuals, affecting both immediate utility and the population distribution over time. We introduce notions of group fairness for both the short and long term and theoretically analyze the trade-off between fairness and utility via the Price of Fairness (PoF). We characterize optimal and fair policies in the short term and show that the PoF can be large even when group distributions are nearly identical. In contrast, we show that long-term disparities can vanish under simple investment policies that achieve a low PoF. We also empirically validate these theoretical observations using both synthetic and real datasets.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how algorithmic decisions made repeatedly over time affect both immediate performance and long‑run group disparities, and compares short‑term and long‑term fairness constraints via a Price of Fairness analysis.
AI.motivation: Static (one-shot) fairness constraints can unintentionally worsen inequalities when decisions are made repeatedly and influence future populations. There is a need to understand how fairness interventions behave in dynamic, sequential decision-making settings where choices today change tomorrow’s opportunities.
AI.method: The authors define a stylized sequential selection model where a decision-maker repeatedly selects individuals from groups, and these selections both yield utility and update the groups’ future distributions. They introduce formal notions of group fairness in both short-term (per-round) and long-term (asymptotic) senses, and analyze optimal policies under these constraints. They use the Price of Fairness (PoF)—the ratio or gap between fair and utility-maximizing performance—to characterize trade-offs, and provide both theoretical analysis and empirical experiments on synthetic and real data.
AI.result: They show that in the short term, when fairness constraints are imposed myopically (per round), the optimal fair policies can incur a large Price of Fairness, even when the group distributions are very similar. In contrast, they identify simple long-term “investment” policies under which disparities between groups can vanish over time while maintaining a low PoF. Empirical studies on synthetic and real datasets support the theoretical findings, illustrating both the potential harms of purely static fairness enforcement and the benefits of long-term fairness-aware investment strategies.
AI.conclusion: Static, per-round fairness constraints in sequential decision problems can be misleading and even harmful, leading to large efficiency losses without eliminating or even while worsening long-run disparities. By adopting long-term fairness notions and simple dynamic investment policies, decision-makers can substantially reduce disparities over time at relatively low utility cost. The work argues for explicitly dynamic models and metrics when designing fair algorithms in high-stakes, evolving environments.

title: Safactory: A Scalable Agent Factory for Trustworthy Autonomous Intelligence
summary: As large models evolve from conversational assistants into autonomous agents, challenges increasingly arise from long-horizon decision making, tool use, and real environment interaction. Existing agenticinfrastructure remain fragmented across evaluation, data management, and agent evolution, making it difficult to discover risks systematically and improve models in a continuous closed loop. In this report, we present \textbf{Safactory}, a scalable agent factory for trustworthy autonomous intelligence. Safactory integrates three tightly coupled platforms: a \textbf{Parallel Simulation Platform} for trajectory generation, a \textbf{Trustworthy Data Platform} for trajectory storage and experience extraction, and an \textbf{Autonomous Evolution Platform} for asynchronous reinforcement learning and on-policy distillation. As far as we know, Safactory is the first framework to propose a unified evolutionary pipeline for next-generation trustworthy autonomous intelligence.

AI.tldr: Safactory is a unified, scalable framework (an “agent factory”) to build and improve trustworthy autonomous AI agents in a closed loop.
AI.motivation: As large models become autonomous agents, they must plan over long horizons, use tools, and act in real environments. Current infrastructures for agents are fragmented: evaluation, data management, and model/agent evolution are separated, making it hard to systematically find risks, learn from trajectories, and continuously improve agents in a safe, iterative manner. The authors want a single, scalable pipeline that can generate experience, store and analyze it, and automatically improve agents with a focus on trustworthiness.
AI.method: They design Safactory as an integrated framework with three tightly coupled platforms: (1) a Parallel Simulation Platform that can generate many trajectories in parallel, simulating agents’ interactions for long-horizon tasks and tool use; (2) a Trustworthy Data Platform to store these trajectories, manage them as a dataset, and extract useful experiences (e.g., successes, failures, risk cases); and (3) an Autonomous Evolution Platform that uses asynchronous reinforcement learning and on-policy distillation to continually update and refine the agent models based on the collected data. The core idea is a unified evolutionary pipeline where agents are deployed, evaluated, logged, and improved in a loop.
AI.result: Conceptually, Safactory offers a scalable architecture where agent trajectories can be generated, curated, and fed into an automated learning process to evolve more trustworthy agents. Within the abstract, they do not present quantitative results, but they claim that Safactory operationalizes a unified pipeline for large-model-based autonomous intelligence that supports large-scale parallel simulation, structured trajectory management, and continuous agent evolution.
AI.conclusion: Safactory is, to the authors’ knowledge, the first framework that unifies simulation, trustworthy data management, and autonomous evolution into a single evolutionary pipeline for next-generation autonomous agents. This integration is intended to enable systematic risk discovery and continuous, scalable improvement of agent trustworthiness, providing an infrastructure foundation for future autonomous AI systems.

title: Data Language Models: A New Foundation Model Class for Tabular Data
summary: Every major data modality now has a foundation model that understands it natively: text has language models, images have vision models, audio has audio models. Tabular data, the modality on which many consequential real-world AI decisions are made, does not. Every approach to tabular AI today, from gradient-boosted trees to the latest tabular foundation models, requires a preprocessing pipeline before any model can consume the data. None of them understand tabular data as a modality. We introduce the Data Language Model (DLM), the missing foundation model for tabular data. A DLM understands tables the way a language model understands sentences: natively, without serialization or preprocessing, directly from raw cell values. It is the tabular data layer on which AI models, agents, and vertical AI applications can be built, eliminating the preprocessing pipelines that currently stand between raw data and every AI system that consumes it. We present Schema-1, the first DLM: a 140M parameter model trained on more than 2.3M synthetic and real-world tabular datasets. Schema-1 outperforms gradient-boosted ensembles, AutoML stacks, and the tabular foundation models we evaluate on established row-level prediction benchmarks. On missing value reconstruction it achieves lower reconstruction error than all classical statistical methods and frontier large language models on mean performance across conditions, establishing that structural understanding of a dataset's own distributional geometry is more useful for imputation than world knowledge encoded in language. It identifies the industry sector of any unseen dataset from raw cell values alone, reliably across any domain, a task no prior tabular model can perform. It is the native tabular understanding layer that has been missing from the AI stack.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Schema-1, a 140M-parameter Data Language Model (DLM) that natively understands tabular data and outperforms existing methods on prediction, imputation, and dataset characterization tasks.
AI.motivation: Unlike text, images, and audio, tabular data lacks a true foundation model that can consume raw tables without bespoke preprocessing. Current methods—gradient-boosted trees, AutoML systems, and recent tabular foundation models—all depend on hand-crafted or learned preprocessing pipelines (e.g., encoding, normalization, serialization). This creates friction, fragility, and limits reusability of models across tasks. The authors aim to fill this gap with a model that treats tables as a first-class modality, serving as a universal tabular understanding layer for AI systems.
AI.method: The authors propose the Data Language Model (DLM), a foundation model designed to operate directly on raw cell values in tables, analogously to how language models operate on tokens in text. They instantiate this concept as Schema-1, a 140M-parameter model trained on more than 2.3M synthetic and real-world tabular datasets. The training objective and architecture are tailored to capture the structural and distributional geometry of tables, enabling the model to perform diverse downstream tasks without specialized preprocessing. They then evaluate Schema-1 on standard row-level prediction benchmarks, missing value reconstruction, and a novel dataset-industry identification task.
AI.result: Schema-1 outperforms gradient-boosted ensembles, AutoML stacks, and evaluated tabular foundation models on established row-level prediction benchmarks. For missing value reconstruction, it achieves lower reconstruction error than classical statistical imputation methods and state-of-the-art large language models on average across conditions. It can also infer the industry sector of unseen datasets from raw cell values alone, a capability not achieved by prior tabular models.
AI.conclusion: The authors conclude that Data Language Models, and Schema-1 in particular, provide the long-missing native understanding layer for tabular data in the AI ecosystem. By operating directly on raw tables, DLMs can replace complex preprocessing pipelines and offer superior performance on prediction and imputation, as well as new capabilities such as dataset-level characterization. They argue that structural understanding of a dataset’s own distributional geometry is more valuable for tabular tasks like imputation than external world knowledge encoded in text-focused large language models.

title: Addressing Labelled Data Scarcity: Taxonomy-Agnostic Annotation of PII Values in HTTP Traffic using LLMs
comment: Accepted to 2026 IEEE European Symposium on Security and Privacy Workshops (EuroS&PW)
summary: Automated privacy audits of web and mobile applications often analyse outbound HTTP traffic to detect Personally Identifiable Information (PII) leakage. However, existing learning-based detectors typically depend on scarce, manually labelled traffic and are tightly coupled to fixed label taxonomies, limiting transferability across domains and evolving definitions of PII. This paper investigates whether Large Language Models (LLMs) can support taxonomy-agnostic annotation of explicitly transmitted PII values in HTTP message bodies when the taxonomy is provided at runtime. We introduce a multi-stage LLM-based pipeline that combines deterministic pre-processing with label-level classification, targeted instance-level value annotation, and output validation. To enable controlled evaluation and exemplar-based prompting without relying on sensitive real-user captures, we further propose an LLM-based generator for synthetic HTTP traffic with manually validated, taxonomy-derived PII annotations. We evaluate the approach across three taxonomies spanning different PII domains and granularity levels. Results show that the pipeline accurately detects PII types and extracts corresponding values for concrete PII taxonomies. Overall, our findings position LLMs as a promising foundation for flexible, taxonomy-agnostic traffic annotation and for creating labelled data under evolving privacy taxonomies.

AI.tldr: This paper explores using Large Language Models (LLMs) to flexibly detect and annotate PII in HTTP traffic without being tied to a fixed PII taxonomy.
AI.motivation: Existing automated privacy audits for web and mobile apps rely on analysing HTTP traffic to find PII leakage, but current learning-based detectors need scarce, manually labelled data and are tightly bound to a specific, fixed PII label set. This makes it hard to transfer them across domains or adapt to changing definitions of PII. The authors aim to develop a PII detection method that can flexibly work with arbitrary PII taxonomies provided at runtime and reduce dependence on sensitive real-user data.
AI.method: The authors design a multi-stage pipeline centered on LLMs. First, deterministic preprocessing normalizes and structures HTTP message bodies. Then, an LLM performs label-level classification to determine what PII types (from a runtime-provided taxonomy) may be present. Next, the LLM does targeted, instance-level annotation to identify and extract specific PII values associated with those types. A final validation stage checks and refines the outputs. In addition, they build an LLM-based generator for synthetic HTTP traffic that embeds PII values annotated according to taxonomy-derived labels, with manual validation to ensure correctness. This synthetic data is used both for evaluation and as exemplars in prompts.
AI.result: Across three different PII taxonomies, varying in domain focus and granularity, the proposed pipeline successfully detects PII types in HTTP message bodies and accurately extracts the corresponding concrete PII values. The experiments demonstrate that, given a taxonomy at runtime, LLMs can support effective PII annotation without being bound to a single, fixed label set.
AI.conclusion: LLMs can serve as a flexible, taxonomy-agnostic basis for annotating PII in HTTP traffic and for synthetically generating labelled traffic data. This reduces reliance on manually labelled real-user data and allows privacy auditing tools to adapt more easily to evolving or domain-specific PII taxonomies.

authors: Telmo de Menezes e Silva Filho
title: Mind the Gap? A Distributional Comparison of Real and Synthetic Priors for Tabular Foundation Models
summary: Tabular foundation models are pre-trained on one of three classes of corpus: curated datasets drawn from benchmark repositories, tables harvested at scale from the web, or synthetic tables sampled from a parametric generative prior. Despite the centrality of pre-training data to model performance, little is known about how these corpora relate to one another in distribution, and the impact this has on downstream performance. In this work we take three canonical, archetypal datasets used to train tabular foundation models; the T4 dataset represents web-scraped corpora, the TabFM dataset curated tables from Kaggle, and the TabICL dataset as the only well-used synthetic prior with publicly available parameters. We characterise each corpus using aggregate features over whole tables, columns and correlations, and compare them using discriminator AUCs and k-NN coverage metrics.
