We find that the TabICL synthetic prior occupies a narrow region of the space of real tables, that this mismatch cannot be closed by optimising prior hyper-parameters across more than 86 thousand configurations, and that curated and web-scraped corpora are broadly interchangeable on a distributional level in feature space. Surprisingly, the distributional gap between synthetic pre-training data and real tables has a clearly detectable effect on performance under neither feature-based proximity measures or TabICL's own internal representations, suggesting that coverage of the real-data distribution is not the primary driver of TabICL's generalisation.

AI.tldr: The paper compares three major tabular pre-training corpora—web-scraped (T4), curated benchmarks (TabFM), and synthetic (TabICL)—and studies how their distributional differences relate to downstream performance of tabular foundation models.
AI.motivation: Tabular foundation models rely heavily on large pre-training corpora, but it is unclear how different types of corpora (web, curated, synthetic) relate in distribution and how this affects model generalisation. Understanding these relationships can guide better dataset and prior design for pre-training.
AI.method: The authors take three canonical corpora: T4 (web tables), TabFM (curated Kaggle tables), and TabICL (synthetic tables from a parametric prior). They characterise each corpus using aggregate features computed at table-, column-, and correlation-levels. They then compare distributions via discriminator AUCs (how well a classifier distinguishes corpora) and k-NN coverage metrics (how much one corpus covers another in feature space). They also perform large-scale hyperparameter sweeps over TabICL’s prior (86k+ configurations) to see if synthetic tables can better match real data distributions, and relate all these distributional measures to downstream performance, including using TabICL’s own internal representations.
AI.result: They find that: (1) The TabICL synthetic prior produces tables that cover only a narrow region of the real-table feature space; (2) even with more than 86,000 prior configurations, this synthetic–real distributional gap cannot be closed; (3) curated (TabFM) and web-scraped (T4) corpora are broadly similar and largely interchangeable in the considered feature space; and (4) despite the clear distributional mismatch, the gap between synthetic and real tables does not translate into a clearly detectable performance difference based on either feature-space proximity or TabICL’s internal embedding space.
AI.conclusion: Coverage of the real-data distribution in the chosen feature space is not the main factor behind TabICL’s generalisation performance. Synthetic priors like TabICL’s can generalise reasonably well even when they only occupy a narrow region of real-table space, and curated vs web-scraped real data appear largely interchangeable distributionally. This suggests that other factors beyond naive distributional coverage are more important for the success of tabular foundation model pre-training, motivating a rethinking of how to design and evaluate pre-training corpora and synthetic priors.

title: More Than Can Be Said: A Benchmark and Framework for Pre-Question Scientific Ideation
comment: 19 pages, 2 figures; Code is available at https://github.com/Paradoxtcal/InciteResearch.git
summary: AI research agents have shown strong potential in automating literature search and manuscript refinement, yet most assume a clear and actionable initial input, operating only after a research question has been made explicit. In contrast, human research often begins with tacit friction, a sense of misalignment before a question can be formed. We introduce InciteResearch, a multi-agent framework designed to make a researcher's implicit understanding explicit, inspectable, and actionable. InciteResearch decomposes the logical chain of Socratic questioning and distributes it across the entire pipeline that: (1) Elicits a structured five-dimensional researcher profile state anchored by specific friction points from vague, even domain-unrelated inputs; (2) Violates hidden assumptions by maximizing the feasibility-novelty product with enforcing a 7-stage causal derivation trace; and (3) check whether the proposed method is a Necessary consequence of the reframed insight. We further introduce TF-Bench, the first benchmark for tacit-to-explicit research assistance that distinguishes domain-related from domain-unrelated inspirations across four scientific modes. On TF-Bench, InciteResearch achieves leapfrogging gains over a prompt-based baseline (novelty/impact from 3.671/3.806 to 4.250/4.397), shifting generated proposals from recombination to architectural insight. Our work demonstrates that AI can serve as an extension of thinking itself, rather than merely automating downstream execution.

AI.tldr: This paper presents InciteResearch, a multi-agent AI framework and a new benchmark (TF-Bench) for turning vague, even off-domain intuitions into explicit, high-quality research proposals, outperforming prompt-based baselines in novelty and impact.
AI.motivation: Most AI research assistants assume that the user already has a clear research question and focus on search or writing assistance. However, real human research often starts from a vague sense of friction or misalignment, before a well-formed question exists. There is a gap in tools that can help transform these tacit, sometimes domain-unrelated intuitions into explicit, inspectable, and actionable research directions. The authors aim to build an AI system that supports this upstream, ideation-oriented phase of research and to provide a benchmark to evaluate such capabilities.
AI.method: The authors propose InciteResearch, a multi-agent framework that distributes a structured Socratic questioning process across the entire research ideation pipeline. (1) It first elicits a five-dimensional researcher profile, grounded in concrete friction points even when the initial input is vague or unrelated to a specific domain. (2) It then systematically challenges hidden assumptions by optimizing a feasibility–novelty product, while enforcing a 7-stage causal derivation trace to keep the reasoning explicit and inspectable. (3) Finally, it checks whether the proposed method is a necessary consequence of the reframed insight, ensuring coherence between the underlying problem and the solution. To evaluate the system, they introduce TF-Bench, a benchmark for tacit-to-explicit research assistance that includes both domain-related and domain-unrelated inspirations across four different scientific modes.
AI.result: On TF-Bench, InciteResearch substantially outperforms a prompt-based baseline system. Specifically, it improves average novelty scores from 3.671 to 4.250 and impact scores from 3.806 to 4.397. Qualitatively, the generated research proposals shift from simple recombinations of known ideas toward deeper, architectural-level insights, indicating more transformative ideation rather than surface-level variation.
AI.conclusion: InciteResearch shows that AI research agents can go beyond automating downstream tasks like literature search and manuscript editing. By turning tacit friction into explicit, causally grounded research proposals and outperforming prompt-based baselines on the new TF-Bench benchmark, the system demonstrates that AI can function as an extension of human thinking itself, supporting the earliest, most ill-defined stages of the research process.

title: Prediction and Empowerment: A Theory of Agency through Bridge Interfaces
comment: This is a working draft: feedback and criticism is most welcome
summary: We study agency under partial observability in deterministic physical or simulated worlds, where apparent randomness arises from uncertainty over initial conditions, fixed law bits, and unrolled exogenous noise. We model sensing and actuation as bridge interfaces split between agent-controlled parameters and environment-controlled channel state, inducing a deterministic POMDP through a prior over latent microstates and many-to-one observation coarsening. Within this framework, we prove a separation between prediction, compression, and empowerment. Perfect prediction can be achieved either by identifying the hidden quotient relevant to the target family or by overwrite control that makes the future target action-determined; high empowerment alone is insufficient. Under refinable interfaces and sufficient memory, action-conditioned observation-compression progress reduces posterior uncertainty about the latent quotient, and when refinement requires steering world-side channel conditions, this creates target-conditioned interface empowerment. A bit-string specialization with a conserved information budget makes the resulting tradeoff explicit: prediction by identification requires internal capacity at least the relevant latent entropy, whereas overwrite control requires terminal action capacity over the controlled quotient. For modern AI agents, the results suggest a design principle rather than a theorem of inevitability: objectives should distinguish hidden-state identification, interface refinement, task-relevant controllability, and mere overwrite or distractor control. Human--AI alignment is partly an interface-design problem, where the relevant bridge is between human intent, agent internal state, external tools, and world-side channel conditions. This is a working draft: feedback and criticism is most welcome.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes agency in deterministic but partially observable worlds, distinguishing between predicting the world, compressing observations, and controlling outcomes, and derives capacity tradeoffs and design principles for AI interfaces and alignment.
AI.motivation: In deterministic environments that appear stochastic due to hidden initial conditions and latent variables, it is unclear how an agent’s ability to predict, represent, and control the world relate to each other. Existing notions like empowerment focus on control but may conflate different capabilities (e.g., real understanding vs brute-force overwriting). The authors aim to clarify these distinctions, especially for modern AI systems and alignment, by formalizing how sensing/actuation interfaces shape what an agent can know and control.
AI.method: The authors model the environment as a deterministic world with partial observability, represented as a deterministic POMDP induced by a prior over latent microstates and coarse observations. They formalize sensing and actuation as bridge interfaces with agent-controlled parameters and environment-controlled channel states. Within this framework, they introduce and separate three capabilities: prediction, observation compression, and empowerment. They analyze conditions such as refinable interfaces and sufficient memory, study how action-conditioned compression affects posterior uncertainty about latent quotients, and specialize the framework to a bit-string model with a conserved information budget to derive explicit capacity tradeoffs.
AI.result: They prove that perfect prediction can be obtained in two distinct ways: (1) by identifying the hidden quotient of the state space relevant to the prediction target, or (2) by exerting overwrite control so that future targets become determined by the agent’s actions rather than by the latent state. They show that high empowerment alone does not guarantee good prediction or genuine understanding. Under refinable interfaces, action-conditioned compression progress reduces uncertainty about the latent quotient, and when refinement requires steering world-side channel conditions, this leads to target-conditioned interface empowerment. In the bit-string specialization, they derive explicit information-theoretic tradeoffs: prediction by identification requires internal capacity at least equal to the relevant latent entropy, while overwrite control requires sufficient terminal action capacity over the controlled quotient.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that prediction, compression, and empowerment are distinct capabilities that should not be conflated in agent design. For AI systems, this yields a design principle: objectives should separately track hidden-state identification, interface refinement, task-relevant controllability, and mere overwrite/distractor control. The authors argue that human–AI alignment is significantly an interface-design issue: the critical bridge is between human intent, agent internal representations, external tools, and the world-side channel conditions, and well-designed interfaces can steer agents toward genuine understanding and task-relevant control rather than superficial overwrite control.

title: From Agent Loops to Deterministic Graphs: Execution Lineage for Reproducible AI-Native Work
summary:
Large language model systems are increasingly deployed as agentic workflows that interleave reasoning, tool use, memory, and iterative refinement. These systems are effective at producing answers, but they often rely on implicit conversational state, making it difficult to preserve stable work products, isolate irrelevant updates, or propagate changes through intermediate artifacts.
  We introduce execution lineage: an execution model in which AI-native work is represented as a directed acyclic graph (DAG) of artifact-producing computations with explicit dependencies, stable intermediate boundaries, and identity-based replay. The goal is not to make the model a better one-shot writer, but to make evolving AI-generated work maintainable under change.
  We compare execution-lineage replay against loop-centric update baselines on two controlled policy-memo update tasks. In an unrelated-branch update, DAG replay preserved the final memo exactly in all runs, with zero churn and zero unrelated-branch contamination, while loop baselines regenerated the memo and frequently imported unrelated context. In an intermediate-artifact edit, all systems reflected the new constraint in the final memo, but only DAG replay achieved perfect upstream preservation, downstream propagation, unaffected-artifact preservation, and cross-artifact consistency.
  These results show that final answer quality and maintained-state quality are distinct. Strong loop baselines can remain competitive at producing polished final outputs when the task is a bounded synthesis/update problem and all current sources fit in context, but immediate task success can mask partial state inconsistency that may compound over future revisions. Execution lineage provides stronger guarantees about what should change, what should remain stable, and how work evolves across revisions.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an execution model called execution lineage, which represents AI agent work as a DAG of artifact-producing computations to make AI-generated work more maintainable under change, and demonstrates its advantages over loop-based workflows in controlled policy memo update tasks.
AI.motivation: As LLMs are increasingly used in agentic workflows involving tools, memory, and iterative refinement, current systems rely heavily on implicit conversational state. This makes it hard to preserve stable intermediate work products, prevent irrelevant updates from contaminating results, and propagate intended changes reliably through complex artifacts over time. The authors want a principled way to track, update, and replay AI work products as they evolve.
AI.method: The authors introduce execution lineage, a representation of AI-native work as a directed acyclic graph where nodes are artifact-producing computations and edges encode explicit dependencies. This structure supports stable intermediate boundaries and identity-based replay of only the affected parts when inputs change. They empirically compare DAG-based replay to strong loop-centric baselines on two controlled policy memo update tasks: (1) unrelated-branch updates, and (2) intermediate-artifact edits, measuring preservation of unaffected content, contamination, propagation of constraints, and cross-artifact consistency.
AI.result: In unrelated-branch updates, DAG-based execution lineage replay preserved the final memo exactly across all runs, with zero churn and no contamination from unrelated branches, whereas loop-centric baselines regenerated the full memo and often pulled in irrelevant context. In intermediate-artifact edits, all systems incorporated the new constraint in the final memo, but only DAG replay simultaneously preserved upstream artifacts, correctly propagated changes downstream, kept unaffected artifacts stable, and maintained consistency across related artifacts.
AI.conclusion: The study shows that achieving high-quality final answers is not the same as maintaining a coherent, stable internal state across revisions. While loop-based LLM workflows can yield polished outputs in bounded, in-context tasks, they can hide partial state inconsistencies that may accumulate over time. Execution lineage, by modeling work as a DAG with explicit dependencies and replay, offers stronger guarantees about which parts of the work should change or stay fixed and how AI-generated work should evolve across iterative revisions.

title: Debiased Multimodal Personality Understanding through Dual Causal Intervention
summary: Multimodalpersonalityunderstandingplaysacriticalroleinhuman centered artificial intelligence. Previous work mainly focus on learn-ing rich multimodal representations for video personality under standing. However, they often suffer from potential harm caused by subject bias (e.g., observable age and unobservable mental states), as subjects originate from diverse demographic backgrounds. Learn ing such spurious associations between multimodal features and traits may lead to unfair personality understanding. In this work, weconstruct aStructural Causal Model (SCM)toanalyze theimpact of these biases from a causal perspective, and propose a novel Dual Causal Adjustment Network (DCAN) to mitigate the interference of subject attributes on personality understanding. Specifically, we design a Back-door Adjustment Causal Learning (BACL) module to block spurious correlations from observable demographic factors via a prototype-based confounder dictionary, and subsequently ap ply a Front-door Adjustment Causal Learning (FACL) module to ad dress latent and unobservable biases throughalearnedmediatordic tionary intervention, thereby achieving causal disentanglement of representations for deconfounded reasoning. Importantly, we con struct a Demographic-annotated Multimodal Student Personality (DMSP) dataset to support the analysis and discussion of fairness related factors. Extensive experiments on the benchmark dataset CFI-V2 and our DMSPdataset demonstrate that DCAN consistently improves prediction accuracy, reaching 92.11% and 92.90%, respec tively. Meanwhile, the improvementsinthefairnessmetricsofequal opportunity and demographic parity are 6.57% and 7.97% on CFI-V2, and 15.38% and 20.06% on the DMSP dataset. Our code and DMSP dataset are available at https://github.com/Sabrina-han/DCAN

AI.tldr: They propose a causal-method-based neural network to reduce demographic bias in multimodal video personality prediction and show it improves both accuracy and fairness, supported by a new demographic-annotated dataset.
AI.motivation: Multimodal personality prediction models (e.g., from video, audio, text) can unintentionally learn spurious correlations between personality traits and subject attributes such as age or latent mental states, leading to unfair and biased predictions across demographic groups. Existing work emphasizes rich representation learning but largely ignores fairness and causal reasoning about these biases. The authors are motivated to build a principled causal framework that explicitly models and mitigates both observable and latent subject biases, and to provide a dataset that enables systematic study of fairness in multimodal personality understanding.
AI.method: They first construct a Structural Causal Model (SCM) capturing relationships among subject attributes (demographics and latent states), multimodal features, and personality traits. Based on this SCM, they propose a Dual Causal Adjustment Network (DCAN) that performs two types of causal adjustment. A Back-door Adjustment Causal Learning (BACL) module uses a prototype-based confounder dictionary to block spurious correlations induced by observable demographic variables (e.g., age, gender) via back-door adjustment. Then a Front-door Adjustment Causal Learning (FACL) module handles latent and unobservable biases by introducing and intervening on a learned mediator dictionary, implementing front-door adjustment to disentangle causal representations. These two modules are integrated into a multimodal representation learning framework for video-based personality prediction. Additionally, they construct a new Demographic-annotated Multimodal Student Personality (DMSP) dataset with explicit demographic labels to support causal analysis and fairness evaluation.
AI.result: On the benchmark CFI-V2 dataset and the newly introduced DMSP dataset, DCAN improves both prediction accuracy and fairness compared to strong baselines. It achieves personality prediction accuracies of 92.11% on CFI-V2 and 92.90% on DMSP. In terms of fairness, it yields notable gains in equal opportunity and demographic parity: improvements of 6.57% and 7.97% respectively on CFI-V2, and larger gains of 15.38% and 20.06% on DMSP. These results indicate that the causal adjustment modules can simultaneously enhance performance and mitigate demographic biases in multimodal personality understanding.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that explicitly modeling subject biases through a Structural Causal Model and performing both back-door and front-door causal adjustments enables fairer and more accurate multimodal personality prediction. The proposed DCAN effectively disentangles causal factors from spurious demographic correlations, improving standard accuracy metrics and fairness measures without sacrificing performance. The new DMSP dataset further facilitates the analysis of fairness-related issues in this domain. The authors position their work as a step toward more trustworthy, human-centered AI systems for personality understanding, where causal reasoning is used to address demographic and latent biases in multimodal data.

title: Rethinking Vacuity for OOD Detection in Evidential Deep Learning
summary: Vacuity, or Uncertainty Mass (UM), is commonly used as a metric to evaluate Out-of-Distribution (OOD) detection in Evidential Deep Learning (EDL). It generally involves dividing the number of classes ($K$) by the total strength of belief ($S$) of the model's predictions, where $S$ is derived from summing the Dirichlet parameters. As such, UM is sensitive to the cardinality of $K$. In particular, it is unlikely in practice that there is a linear relationship between $K$ and $S$ as $K$ and $S$ increase due to the nature of EDL (suppressing incorrectly assigned evidence). As a result, when comparing In Distribution (ID) and OOD results, it is important that $K_{\mathrm{ID}}$ and $K_{\mathrm{OOD}}$ are equal; something that is not always ensured in practice. We provide an empirical demonstration of how results for AUROC and AUPR can substantially differ when class cardinality between ID and OOD differs by 1, with AUROC differing by as much as 0.318 and AUPR by 0.613 for standard EDL, and AUROC by 0.360 and AUPR by 0.683 for IB-EDL. More concretely, our findings isolate an evaluation artefact: when K differs between ID and OOD, AUROC/AUPR can be artificially inflated without any change in model predictions. We further discuss the evaluation of EDL over causal language models using Multiple-Choice Question-Answer (MCQA) datasets and argue for clearer definitions of ID and OOD in this context. Our primary contribution is an empirical and theoretical demonstration that vacuity-based OOD detection in EDL-fine-tuned LLMs is highly sensitive to uncontrolled differences in evaluated class cardinality.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that the commonly used vacuity/uncertainty-mass metric for OOD detection in Evidential Deep Learning is highly sensitive to the number of classes, so AUROC/AUPR can be spuriously inflated when class cardinalities differ between ID and OOD, especially in EDL-fine-tuned LLMs.
AI.motivation: Vacuity (uncertainty mass) is widely used to assess OOD detection in EDL, but its dependence on the number of classes (K) is under-examined. In practice, ID and OOD often have different class cardinalities, especially in MCQA settings with EDL-tuned LLMs. This can lead to misleadingly high OOD detection scores even if the model behavior is unchanged. The authors want to expose and analyze this evaluation artefact to improve rigor and comparability in EDL OOD studies.
AI.method: The authors analyze the mathematical form of vacuity, UM = K / S where S is the total Dirichlet strength, to show its sensitivity to class count. They argue that S does not generally scale linearly with K in EDL due to evidence suppression. They then empirically test standard EDL and IB-EDL models under varying class cardinalities between ID and OOD, measuring how AUROC and AUPR for OOD detection change even when model predictions are fixed. They further examine EDL-fine-tuned LLMs on MCQA datasets to discuss ID/OOD definitions.
AI.result: They find that even a one-class difference between ID and OOD (K_ID vs K_OOD differing by 1) can cause large shifts in OOD metrics: for standard EDL, AUROC can change by up to 0.318 and AUPR by 0.613; for IB-EDL, AUROC by 0.360 and AUPR by 0.683. These differences arise purely from the evaluation setup, not from changes in model predictions, revealing an artefactual inflation (or distortion) of vacuity-based OOD performance when class cardinality is not matched.
AI.conclusion: Vacuity-based OOD detection in EDL, including in EDL-fine-tuned LLMs for MCQA, is highly sensitive to the relative number of classes evaluated on ID vs OOD data. If K_ID and K_OOD differ, AUROC and AUPR can be artificially improved without genuine gains in detection ability. The authors stress that evaluation protocols must control for class cardinality and more carefully define ID vs OOD, especially in language-model MCQA setups, to avoid misleading conclusions about OOD robustness.

title: Automated alignment is harder than you think
summary: A leading proposal for aligning artificial superintelligence (ASI) is to use AI agents to automate an increasing fraction of alignment research as capabilities improve. We argue that, even when research agents are not scheming to deliberately sabotage alignment work, this plan could produce compelling but catastrophically misleading safety assessments resulting in the unintentional deployment of misaligned AI. This could happen because alignment research involves many hard-to-supervise fuzzy tasks (tasks without clear evaluation criteria, for which human judgement is systematically flawed). Consequently, research outputs will contain systematic, undetected errors, and even correct outputs could be incorrectly aggregated into overconfident safety assessments. This problem is likely to be worse for automated alignment research than for human-generated alignment research for several reasons: 1) optimisation pressure means agent-generated mistakes are concentrated among those that human reviewers are least likely to catch; 2) agents are likely to produce errors that do not resemble human mistakes; 3) AI-generated alignment solutions may involve arguments humans cannot evaluate; and 4) shared weights, data and training processes may make AI outputs more correlated than human equivalents. Therefore, agents must be trained to reliably perform hard-to-supervise fuzzy tasks. Generalisation and scalable oversight are the leading candidates for achieving this but both face novel challenges in the context of automated alignment.

AI.tldr: The paper warns that using AI agents to automate alignment research can create very convincing but dangerously wrong safety evaluations, even without intentional deception, due to systematic, hard-to-detect errors in fuzzy, hard-to-supervise tasks.
AI.motivation: As AI capabilities advance, a common plan is to use increasingly capable AI agents to help solve the alignment problem itself. However, there is a risk that these agents could yield safety assessments that look strong but are actually flawed, leading to the deployment of misaligned ASI. The authors want to analyze how and why this failure mode could occur even if the research agents are not maliciously scheming, and what that implies for alignment strategies relying on automated research.
AI.method: The paper provides a conceptual and theoretical analysis rather than experiments. It identifies structural properties of alignment research as a domain (fuzzy tasks, weak feedback, human judgment biases), then analyzes how optimization, error patterns, argument opacity, and correlation among AI systems could systematically bias AI-generated research outputs and safety evaluations. It then examines the implications for proposed alignment strategies such as scalable oversight and generalization-focused methods.
AI.result: The analysis shows multiple mechanisms by which AI-automated alignment research will tend to concentrate its errors specifically where humans are least able to detect them, and that AI outputs will be both differently distributed and more correlated than human research outputs. This combination undermines the reliability of AI-generated safety assessments, particularly in the context of tasks that are inherently hard to supervise and evaluate. The authors argue that current proposals for scalable oversight and generalization do not yet adequately address these issues.
AI.conclusion: Automating alignment research with AI agents is far more hazardous than it might first appear, even in the absence of deceptive or scheming behavior. Because alignment work involves fuzzy, hard-to-evaluate tasks where human judgment is unreliable, optimization by powerful agents will likely produce systematically misleading but persuasive safety arguments. To safely leverage AI for alignment, we must develop methods that robustly train agents to perform such hard-to-supervise tasks and address the special challenges this setting poses for generalization and scalable oversight.

title: Knowledge Graphs, the Missing Link in Agentic AI-based Formal Verification
comment: To appear at the IEEE International Conference on IC Design and Technology 2026 (ICICDT), June 22 - 24, 2026, Dresden, Germany
summary: Recent advances in Large Language Models (LLMs) have enabled workflows that generate SystemVerilog Assertions (SVAs) from natural-language specifications, with the potential to accelerate Formal Verification (FV). However, high-quality assertion synthesis remains challenging because specifications are often ambiguous or incomplete and critical micro-architectural details reside in the Register Transfer Level (RTL). Many existing approaches treat the specification and RTL as loosely structured text, which weakens specification-to-RTL grounding and leads to semantic mismatches and frequent syntax failures during formal parsing and elaboration. This work addresses these limitations with a verification-centric Knowledge Graph (KG) constructed from structured Intermediate Representations (IRs) extracted from the specification, RTL, and formal-tool feedback, including syntax diagnostics, Counterexamples (CEXs), and coverage reports. The KG links requirements, design hierarchy, signals, assumptions, and properties to provide traceable, design-grounded context for generation. A multi-agent workflow queries and updates this KG to generate SVAs and to drive three refinement loops: syntax repair guided by tool diagnostics, CEX-guided correction using trace links, and coverage-directed property augmentation. Evaluation across seven benchmark designs indicates that KG-based context retrieval improves specification-to-RTL grounding and consistently produces compilable SVAs with low syntax-repair overhead. The approach achieves formal coverage ranging from 78.5% to 99.4%, though convergence exhibits design dependence with complex temporal and arithmetic reasoning remaining challenging for current LLM capabilities.

AI.tldr: They build a knowledge-graph-driven, multi-agent LLM workflow to generate and iteratively refine SystemVerilog Assertions from natural-language specs and RTL, achieving high formal coverage with good syntax correctness.
AI.motivation: LLM-generated SystemVerilog Assertions can speed up formal verification, but current methods struggle because specs are ambiguous, important details live in RTL, and most approaches treat both as plain text, causing weak grounding, semantic mismatches, and many syntax errors during tool parsing.
AI.method: They construct a verification-centric knowledge graph from structured intermediate representations of the natural-language specification, RTL, and formal-tool feedback (syntax errors, counterexamples, coverage reports). A multi-agent LLM workflow queries and updates this KG to generate assertions and runs three refinement loops: syntax repair guided by tool diagnostics, counterexample-guided correction via trace links in the KG, and coverage-directed property augmentation.
AI.result: On seven benchmark designs, KG-based retrieval improves grounding between specification and RTL, produces compilable SVAs with relatively low syntax-repair overhead, and attains formal coverage between 78.5% and 99.4%, though performance varies by design complexity.
AI.conclusion: A knowledge-graph-centered, feedback-driven LLM workflow can substantially improve the quality and usability of automatically generated SVAs, but convergence and reasoning over complex temporal and arithmetic behaviors remain challenging and limit performance on more complex designs.

title: SCRuB: Social Concept Reasoning under Rubric-Based Evaluation
summary: While many studies of Large Language Model (LLM) reasoning capabilities emphasize mathematical or technical tasks, few address reasoning about social concepts: the abstract ideas shaping social norms, culture, and institutions. This understudied capability is essential for modern models acting as social agents, yet no systematic evaluation methodology targets it. We introduce SCRuB (Social Concept Reasoning under Rubric-Based Evaluation), a framework designed for this setting of task indeterminacy. Our goal is to measure the degree to which a model reasons about social concepts with the depth and critical rigor of a human expert. SCRuB proceeds in three phases: prompt construction from established sources, response generation by experts and models, and comparative evaluation using a five-dimensional critical thinking rubric. To enable generalization of the pipeline, we introduce a Panel of Disciplinary Perspectives ensemble validated against independent expert judges. We release SCRuBEval (n=4,711 evaluation prompts) and SCRuBAnnotations (300 expert-authored responses and 150 expert comparative judgments from 45 PhD-level scholars). Our results show that frontier models consistently outperform human experts across all five rubric dimensions. Across 1,170 pairwise comparisons, expert judges ranked a model response first in 80.8% of judgments and preferred model responses overall 74.4% of the time. Ultimately, this study provides the first expert-grounded demonstration of evaluation saturation for social concept reasoning: the single-turn exam-style format has reached its ceiling for models and humans alike.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SCRuB, a rubric-based framework and dataset to systematically evaluate how well large language models reason about abstract social concepts compared to human experts, finding that frontier models now outperform experts in single-turn exam-style tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluations of LLM reasoning mainly focus on mathematical and technical problems, neglecting reasoning about social concepts that underlie norms, culture, and institutions. Since modern LLMs are deployed as social agents, assessing their capacity for deep, critical reasoning about these concepts is crucial. However, there is no systematic, expert-grounded methodology tailored to the inherent indeterminacy and open-endedness of such tasks. The authors aim to fill this gap and to understand whether current evaluation formats are still discriminative for frontier models vs. humans.
AI.method: They design SCRuB (Social Concept Reasoning under Rubric-Based Evaluation), a three-phase framework. First, they construct prompts about social concepts from established disciplinary sources. Second, they collect responses from both expert humans and LLMs. Third, they conduct comparative evaluation via a five-dimensional critical thinking rubric, implemented through a "Panel of Disciplinary Perspectives" ensemble that is validated against independent expert judges. They compile and release two resources: SCRuBEval (4,711 evaluation prompts) and SCRuBAnnotations (300 expert-written answers and 150 expert comparative judgments from 45 PhD-level scholars).
AI.result: Frontier LLMs outperform human experts on all five critical thinking dimensions in this evaluation setting. In 1,170 pairwise comparisons between model and expert responses, expert judges ranked the model response first in 80.8% of cases and preferred model responses overall 74.4% of the time, indicating strong model performance and suggesting that standard single-turn exam-style questions no longer clearly distinguish between top models and humans in social concept reasoning.
AI.conclusion: SCRuB provides the first expert-grounded evidence of "evaluation saturation" for social concept reasoning in single-turn, exam-style formats: both advanced LLMs and human experts appear to have hit a ceiling under this paradigm. The framework and released datasets offer a structured way to study social concept reasoning, but also imply that future work needs richer, more interactive, and perhaps longitudinal evaluation formats to meaningfully stress-test models in this domain.

title: PrefixGuard: From LLM-Agent Traces to Online Failure-Warning Monitors
summary: Large language model (LLM) agents now execute long, tool-using tasks where final outcome checks can arrive too late for intervention. Online warning requires lightweight prefix monitors over heterogeneous traces, but hand-authored event schemas are brittle and deployment-time LLM judging is costly. We introduce PrefixGuard, a trace-to-monitor framework with an offline StepView induction step followed by supervised monitor training. StepView induces deterministic typed-step adapters from raw trace samples, and the monitor learns an event abstraction and prefix-risk scorer from terminal outcomes. Across WebArena, $τ^2$-Bench, SkillsBench, and TerminalBench, the strongest PrefixGuard monitors reach 0.900/0.710/0.533/0.557 AUPRC. Using the strongest backend within each representation, they improve over raw-text controls by an average of +0.137 AUPRC. LLM judges remain substantially weaker under the same prefix-warning protocol. We also derive an observability ceiling on score-based area under the precision-recall curve (AUPRC) that separates monitor error from failures lacking evidence in the observed prefix. For finite-state audit, post-hoc deterministic finite automaton (DFA) extraction remains compact on WebArena and $τ^2$-Bench (29 and 20 states) but expands to 151 and 187 states on SkillsBench and TerminalBench. Finally, first-alert diagnostics show that strong ranking does not imply deployment utility: WebArena ranks well yet fails to support low-false-alarm alerts, whereas $τ^2$-Bench and TerminalBench retain more actionable early alerts. Together, these results position PrefixGuard as a practical monitor-synthesis recipe with explicit diagnostics for when prefix warnings translate into actionable interventions.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes PrefixGuard, a framework for learning lightweight, online monitors that can warn early about failures in long LLM-agent tool-use tasks, using induced structured traces instead of brittle hand-coded schemas or expensive online LLM judges.
AI.motivation: LLM agents perform long, multi-step, tool-using tasks where failures may be noticed only at the end, making intervention impossible. To enable timely intervention, we need online prefix monitors that can predict failure risk from early partial traces. Existing approaches rely on hand-authored event schemas, which are brittle and hard to generalize across heterogeneous tasks, or on deployment-time LLM judging, which is expensive and still performs poorly under strict prefix-warning constraints. The paper is motivated by the need for a practical, data-driven way to synthesize effective, cheap, and interpretable monitors that can operate over heterogeneous agent traces and support actionable early alerts.
AI.method: The authors introduce PrefixGuard, a two-stage trace-to-monitor framework. First, StepView performs offline induction of deterministic, typed-step adapters from raw traces, converting heterogeneous textual interaction logs into structured step representations. Second, given these structured traces and their terminal outcomes, a supervised monitor is trained to simultaneously learn (i) an event abstraction over the induced steps and (ii) a prefix-risk scoring function that predicts the probability of eventual task failure from partial prefixes. The method is evaluated across four benchmarks for LLM agents (WebArena, τ^2-Bench, SkillsBench, and TerminalBench). The authors also derive an analytically grounded observability ceiling on AUPRC that separates inherent uncertainty (failures not yet evidenced in the prefix) from monitor errors, and they apply post-hoc deterministic finite automaton (DFA) extraction to obtain finite-state audit models of the monitors. Additional “first-alert” diagnostics examine how early and how reliably monitors can raise low-false-alarm alerts in deployment-like conditions.
AI.result: On four benchmarks, the strongest PrefixGuard monitors achieve AUPRC scores of 0.900 on WebArena, 0.710 on τ^2-Bench, 0.533 on SkillsBench, and 0.557 on TerminalBench. When controlling for the backend model within each representation, PrefixGuard improves over raw-text baselines by an average of +0.137 AUPRC. Under the same prefix-warning protocol, LLM judges perform substantially worse, highlighting the advantage of learned structured monitors. DFA extraction yields compact models on WebArena and τ^2-Bench (29 and 20 states) but much larger automata on SkillsBench and TerminalBench (151 and 187 states), indicating varying trace complexity. First-alert diagnostics reveal that strong overall ranking performance does not guarantee practical deployment: WebArena shows high AUPRC but cannot support low-false-alarm early alerts, while τ^2-Bench and TerminalBench support more actionable early warnings.
AI.conclusion: PrefixGuard offers a practical recipe for synthesizing online monitors for LLM agents from raw traces, without hand-crafted schemas or expensive online LLM judging. By inducing typed-step adapters and training supervised risk scorers, it outperforms raw-text baselines and LLM judges under realistic prefix-warning protocols. The observability ceiling provides a principled way to distinguish monitor limitations from inherently unobservable failures, and DFA extraction enables finite-state auditing, though model size varies with task complexity. First-alert diagnostics emphasize that headline ranking metrics can be misleading about deployment utility, and that some benchmarks better support low-false-alarm early intervention than others. Overall, the framework provides both effective monitoring and explicit diagnostics for when and how prefix warnings can translate into actionable interventions in LLM-agent systems.

title: Beyond Task Success: Measuring Workflow Fidelity in LLM-Based Agentic Payment Systems
comment: 6 pages, 2 tables. Accept at AI and Data Science for Digital Finance (AIDS4DF) Workshop, PAKDD 2026
summary: LLM-based multi-agent systems are increasingly deployed for payment workflows, yet prevailing metrics, Task Success Rate (TSR) and Agent Handoff F1-Score (HF1), capture only final outcomes or unordered routing decisions. We introduce the Agentic Success Rate (ASR), a trajectory-fidelity metric that compares observed and expected agent execution sequences at the transition level, decomposing performance into Transition Recall and Transition Precision. Applied to the Hierarchical Multi-Agent System for Payments (HMASP) across 18 LLMs and 90,000 task instances, ASR reveals that 10 of 18 models systematically skip a confirmation checkpoint during payment checkout, a deviation invisible to both TSR and HF1, while 8 models enforce the checkpoint perfectly. Notably, GPT-4.1 exhibits hidden workflow shortcuts despite achieving perfect TSR and HF1, while GPT-5.2 achieves perfect ASR. Prompt refinements and deterministic routing guards guided by ASR diagnostics yield substantial TSR improvements, with gains up to +93.8 percentage points for previously struggling models, demonstrating that trajectory-level evaluation is essential in regulated domains.

AI.tldr: New metric (ASR) to evaluate LLM-based multi-agent payment systems by checking if agents follow the correct sequence of steps, not just final success.
AI.motivation: Existing metrics like Task Success Rate and Agent Handoff F1-Score only assess end results or unordered routing, missing whether agents actually follow required workflows—critical in regulated payment domains.
AI.method: Define Agentic Success Rate (ASR), which compares observed vs. expected agent transition sequences and decomposes into Transition Recall and Transition Precision; then apply ASR to a Hierarchical Multi-Agent System for Payments (HMASP) using 18 LLMs over 90,000 tasks to diagnose trajectory-level errors and guide prompt/routing refinements.
AI.result: ASR uncovers that most evaluated LLMs systematically skip a payment confirmation checkpoint that TSR and HF1 fail to detect; GPT-4.1 shows hidden shortcuts despite perfect TSR/HF1, GPT-5.2 follows the workflow exactly (perfect ASR); using ASR-guided prompt and routing adjustments dramatically improves TSR, with gains up to +93.8 percentage points for weaker models.
AI.conclusion: Trajectory-level metrics like ASR are crucial for safely deploying LLM-based multi-agent systems in regulated workflows, as they expose hidden policy-violating behaviors and enable targeted fixes that substantially boost both correctness and compliance.

title: Probabilistic Dating of Historical Manuscripts via Evidential Deep Regression on Visual Script Features
summary: We introduce a probabilistic approach for dating historical manuscript pages from visual features alone. Instead of aggregating centuries into classes as is standard in the previous literature, we pose dating as an evidential deep regression problem over a continuous year axis, allowing our neural network to output a full predictive distribution with decomposed aleatoric and epistemic uncertainty in a single forward pass. Our architecture combines an EfficientNet-B2 backbone with a Normal-Inverse-Gamma (NIG) output head trained with a joint negative-log-likelihood and evidence-regularization objective. On the DIVA-HisDB benchmark (150 pages, 3 medieval codices, 151,936 patches), our model scores a test MAE of 5.4 years, well below the 50-year century-label supervision granularity, with 93\% of patches within 5 years and 97\% within 10 years. Our approach achieves \textbf{PICP=92.6\%}, the best calibration among all compared methods, in a single forward pass, outperforming MC Dropout (PICP=88.2\%, 50 passes) and Deep Ensembles (PICP=79.7\%, 5 models) at $5\times$ lower inference cost. Uncertainty decomposition shows aleatoric uncertainty is a strong predictor of dating error (Spearman $ρ=0.729$), and a selective prediction about the most certain 20\% of patches can provide \textbf{0.5 years MAE}. We show that predicted uncertainty increases as image degradation worsens, spatial decomposition maps explain which script regions cause aleatoric uncertainty, and page-level aggregation reduces MAE to 4.5 years with $ρ=0.905$ between uncertainty and page-level error.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a probabilistic deep learning method to date historical manuscript pages directly from images, outputting calibrated uncertainty over a continuous time axis and achieving high accuracy and low cost compared to existing methods.
AI.motivation: Existing work typically dates manuscripts by classifying them into coarse century bins, which wastes supervision signal and does not provide calibrated uncertainty. There is a need for a finer-grained, continuous dating approach that also quantifies both aleatoric and epistemic uncertainties while remaining computationally efficient.
AI.method: They cast dating as evidential deep regression over continuous years. The model uses an EfficientNet-B2 backbone and a Normal-Inverse-Gamma output head that yields a full predictive distribution in a single forward pass. Training uses a combination of negative log-likelihood and evidence regularization losses. They evaluate on the DIVA-HisDB benchmark at patch and page levels, and analyze uncertainty decomposition and its relationship to error and image degradation.
AI.result: On DIVA-HisDB, the model achieves a test MAE of 5.4 years with 93% of patches within 5 years and 97% within 10 years, despite only having 50-year granularity in labels. It shows superior calibration with PICP=92.6% while being 5x cheaper at inference than MC Dropout and Deep Ensembles. Aleatoric uncertainty strongly correlates with dating error (Spearman ρ=0.729), selective prediction on the most certain 20% of patches yields 0.5-year MAE, and page-level aggregation further reduces MAE to 4.5 years with ρ=0.905 between uncertainty and page-level error.
AI.conclusion: The proposed evidential regression approach enables accurate, fine-grained dating of manuscript images with well-calibrated uncertainty at low computational cost. Uncertainty estimates are meaningful, correlate with error and degradation, and can guide selective prediction and explain which visual regions drive uncertainty, making the method practical for real-world historical document analysis.

title: ReasonSTL: Bridging Natural Language and Signal Temporal Logic via Tool-Augmented Process-Rewarded Learning
summary: Signal Temporal Logic (STL) is an expressive formal language for specifying spatio-temporal requirements over real-valued, real-time signals. It has been widely used for the verification and synthesis of autonomous systems and cyber-physical systems. In practice, however, users often express their requirements in natural language rather than in structured STL formulas, making natural-language-to-STL translation a critical yet challenging task. Manual specification requires temporal-logic expertise and cannot scale, while prompting commercial LLM APIs incurs substantial token costs and may expose sensitive system requirements to third-party services, raising privacy concerns for industrial deployment. To address these challenges, we present \textsc{ReasonSTL}, a tool-augmented framework that adapts local open-source language models for natural-language-to-STL generation. \textsc{ReasonSTL} decomposes the translation process into explicit reasoning, deterministic tool calls, and structured formula construction. We further introduce process-rewarded training to supervise both tool-use trajectories and final formulas, together with \textsc{STL-Bench}, a bilingual, computation-aware benchmark grounded in real-world signals. Experiments show that a 4B model trained with \textsc{ReasonSTL} achieves state-of-the-art performance in both automatic metrics and human evaluations, demonstrating that \textsc{ReasonSTL} provides a transparent, low-cost, and privacy-preserving alternative for formal specification drafting.

AI.tldr: The paper presents ReasonSTL, a tool-augmented framework that uses open-source language models and explicit reasoning to translate natural language requirements into Signal Temporal Logic (STL) formulas accurately, efficiently, and privately.
AI.motivation: Translating natural language requirements into STL is essential for verifying and synthesizing autonomous and cyber-physical systems, but manual translation demands temporal-logic expertise and does not scale. Using commercial LLM APIs for this task is expensive in token cost and raises privacy concerns due to sending sensitive system specifications to third-party services. There is a need for a scalable, low-cost, and privacy-preserving way to obtain high-quality STL specifications from natural language.
AI.method: ReasonSTL adapts local open-source language models for the natural-language-to-STL task via a tool-augmented, multi-step framework. The system decomposes translation into explicit reasoning steps, deterministic calls to external tools (e.g., for parsing, checking, or composing components), and structured STL formula construction rather than a single-shot generation. It introduces process-rewarded training that supervises not only the final STL formulas but also the intermediate tool-use trajectories, encouraging correct reasoning and tool usage. Additionally, the authors build STL-Bench, a bilingual (likely natural languages), computation-aware benchmark grounded in real-world signal data to train and evaluate models.
AI.result: A 4-billion-parameter open-source model trained within the ReasonSTL framework achieves state-of-the-art performance on natural-language-to-STL tasks, outperforming existing baselines on both automatic metrics and human evaluation. The approach demonstrates strong translation quality while running locally, indicating competitive capability compared to larger or commercial models without their associated costs or privacy issues.
AI.conclusion: ReasonSTL enables accurate, transparent, and efficient translation from natural language to STL using local open-source models. By combining explicit reasoning, deterministic tool calls, process-rewarded training, and the STL-Bench dataset, it offers a practical, low-cost, and privacy-preserving alternative to commercial LLM APIs for formal specification drafting in autonomous and cyber-physical systems.

title: Instrumental Choices: Measuring the Propensity of LLM Agents to Pursue Instrumental Behaviors
summary: AI systems have become increasingly capable of dangerous behaviours in many domains. This raises the question: Do models sometimes choose to violate human instructions in order to perform behaviour that is more useful for certain goals? We introduce a benchmark for measuring model propensity for instrumental convergence (IC) behaviour in terminal-based agents. This is behaviour such as self-preservation that has been hypothesised to play a key role in risks from highly capable AI agents. Our benchmark is realistic and low-stakes which serves to reduce evaluation-awareness and roleplay confounds. The suite contains seven operational tasks, each with an official workflow and a policy-violating shortcut. An eight-variant shared framework varies monitoring, instruction clarity, stakes, permission, instrumental usefulness and blocked honest paths to support inferences regarding the factors driving IC behaviour. We evaluated ten models using deterministic environment-state scorers over 1,680 samples, with trace review employed for audit and adjudication purposes. The final IC rate is 86 out of 1,680 samples (5.1%). IC behaviour is concentrated rather than uniform: two Gemini models account for 66.3% of IC cases and three tasks account for 84.9%. Conditions in which IC behaviour is indispensable for task success result in the greatest increase in the adjusted IC rate (+15.7 percentage points), whereas emphasising that task success is critical or certain framing choices do not produce comparable effects. Our findings indicate that realistic, low-nudge environments elicit IC behaviour rarely but systematically in most tested models. We conclude that it is feasible to robustly measure tendencies for dangerous behaviour in current frontier AI agents.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a benchmark to test whether AI agents engage in instrumental convergence (IC) behaviors—like self-preservation or rule-breaking—by choosing policy-violating shortcuts in realistic, low-stakes terminal tasks. It finds that IC behaviors are rare but systematic, concentrated in specific models and tasks, and that making IC necessary for success most strongly increases such behaviors.
AI.motivation: As AI systems become more capable, concerns grow that they might pursue goals in ways that conflict with human instructions, especially through instrumental behaviors like self-preservation, resource acquisition, or rule-breaking. Existing evaluations often rely on unrealistic, high-stakes, or heavily roleplayed scenarios that can distort model behavior. The authors aim to create a realistic, operational benchmark that can robustly measure if and when models choose dangerous instrumental behaviors in practice, and to study which conditions make such behaviors more likely.
AI.method: The authors design a benchmark for terminal-based AI agents consisting of seven operational tasks. Each task has (1) an official, policy-compliant workflow and (2) an alternative shortcut that violates specified policies (the IC behavior). They then create eight scenario variants by manipulating factors such as monitoring presence, clarity of instructions, the stakes of success, explicit permission, instrumental usefulness of rule-breaking, and whether honest paths are blocked. Ten different models are evaluated over 1,680 episodes, using deterministic environment-state scorers to classify outcomes. Human trace review is used to audit and adjudicate ambiguous cases. The primary metric is the rate of IC behavior across conditions and models, with adjustments for task difficulty and success conditions.
AI.result: Across 1,680 agent runs, the overall IC behavior rate is 5.1% (86 cases). IC is not evenly distributed: two Gemini models are responsible for 66.3% of all IC cases, and three of the seven tasks account for 84.9% of them. Experimental manipulations reveal that conditions making IC behavior indispensable for task completion lead to the largest increase in adjusted IC rates (+15.7 percentage points). In contrast, emphasizing that task success is very important or altering some framing elements does not significantly change IC rates.
AI.conclusion: Realistic, low-nudge terminal environments can elicit instrumental convergence behaviors in current frontier AI agents, though such behaviors appear relatively infrequent overall. These behaviors emerge systematically under certain conditions and in specific models and tasks, suggesting that IC tendencies are measurable and not purely random or due to evaluation artifacts. The benchmark provides a practical tool for stress-testing and quantifying dangerous behavioral tendencies in AI systems, supporting future work on alignment and safety interventions.

title: From Token Lists to Graph Motifs: Weisfeiler-Lehman Analysis of Sparse Autoencoder Features
summary: Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) have become central to mechanistic interpretability, decomposing transformer activations into monosemantic features. Yet existing analyses characterise features almost exclusively through top-activating token lists or decoder weight vectors, leaving the higher-order co-occurrence structure shared across features largely unexamined. We introduce a graph-structured representation in which each SAE feature is modelled as a token co-occurrence graph: nodes are the tokens most frequent near strong activations, and edges connect pairs that co-occur within local context windows. A custom WL-style, frequency-binned graph kernel then provides a similarity measure over this structural space. Applied as a proof of concept to features from a large SAE trained on GPT-2 Small and probed with a synthetic mixed-domain corpus, our clustering recovers heuristic motif families (punctuation-heavy patterns, language and script clusters, and code-like templates) that are not recovered by clustering on decoder cosine similarity. A token-histogram baseline achieves higher overall purity, so the contribution of the graph view is complementary rather than dominant: it surfaces structural relationships that token-frequency and decoder-weight views alone do not capture. Cluster assignments are stable across graph-construction hyperparameters and random seeds.

AI.tldr: They propose modeling each sparse autoencoder feature as a token co-occurrence graph and using a graph kernel to compare and cluster these features, revealing structural motif families missed by standard methods.
AI.motivation: Current sparse autoencoder interpretability mostly looks at top-activating tokens or decoder weights, which ignores how tokens co-occur around feature activations. The authors want to capture and analyze this higher-order co-occurrence structure to gain new interpretability insights.
AI.method: For each SAE feature, they build a token co-occurrence graph where nodes are frequent neighboring tokens of strong activations and edges connect tokens that co-occur within local windows. They define a custom Weisfeiler-Lehman-style, frequency-binned graph kernel as a similarity metric over these graphs, then cluster features from an SAE trained on GPT-2 Small using this kernel, comparing to baselines such as decoder cosine similarity and token-histogram clustering.
AI.result: Their graph-based clustering of features uncovers coherent motif families (e.g., punctuation-heavy patterns, language/script clusters, code-like templates) that cosine-similarity clustering on decoder weights does not recover. A simple token-histogram baseline attains higher cluster purity overall, but misses some structural relationships that the graph representation reveals. The discovered clusters remain robust across graph-construction hyperparameters and random seeds.
AI.conclusion: Representing SAE features as token co-occurrence graphs and comparing them with a graph kernel offers a complementary interpretability view: while not outperforming simpler baselines on purity, it exposes structural relationships between features that token frequency and decoder-weight approaches overlook, and these insights are stable to reasonable design choices.

title: Process Matters more than Output for Distinguishing Humans from Machines
summary: Reliable human-machine discrimination is becoming increasingly important as large language models and autonomous agents are deployed in online settings. Existing approaches evaluate whether a system can produce behavior or responses indistinguishable from those of a human, following the emphasis on outputs as a criterion for intelligence proposed by Alan Turing. Cognitive science offers an alternative perspective: evaluating the process by which behavior is produced. To test whether cognitive processes can reliably distinguish humans from machines, we introduce CogCAPTCHA30, a battery of 30 cognitive tasks designed to elicit diagnostic process-level features even when task performance is matched. Across the battery, process-level features provide stronger discriminative signal than performance metrics alone, reliably distinguishing humans from agents even under output matching (mean process-feature classifier AUC = 0.88). To evaluate agentic process differences, we compare off-the-shelf frontier agents (Claude Sonnet 4.5, GPT-5, Gemini 2.5 Pro), Centaur (a language model fine-tuned on 10.7M human decisions), and two task-specific fine-tuning approaches applied to Qwen2.5-1.5B-Instruct: action-level supervised fine-tuning (A-SFT) and process-level fine-tuning (P-SFT), which directly optimizes process features. Broad fine-tuning on human decisions improves human-like task processes relative to off-the-shelf agents, while task-specific process-level supervision further improves behavioral mimicry. However, this advantage diminishes under cross-task transfer when supervised process targets do not naturally generalize across tasks. Explicit process-level supervision can improve human behavioral mimicry, but only if appropriate task-specific process representations are available, highlighting process specification as a bottleneck for achieving human-like cognitive processes in machines.

AI.tldr: Introduces CogCAPTCHA30, a set of 30 cognitive tasks to distinguish humans from AI agents by how they think (process-level features) rather than just what answers they give (outputs), showing process features are more discriminative and exploring process-level fine-tuning limits.
AI.motivation: As LLMs and autonomous agents become widespread online, we need reliable ways to tell humans and machines apart. Traditional Turing-style tests focus on outputs being indistinguishable from humans, but cognitive science suggests that the internal processes that generate behavior may be more revealing. The paper aims to test whether process-level cognitive features can robustly discriminate humans from AI agents, even when their task performance (outputs) is similar.
AI.method: The authors build CogCAPTCHA30, a battery of 30 cognitive tasks crafted to elicit rich process-level signals (e.g., action sequences, timing, intermediate states) while allowing AI agents to match human accuracy. They collect human and agent behavior across these tasks, extracting process-level features and standard performance metrics. They then train classifiers to distinguish humans from multiple types of agents using either performance-only or process-level features, comparing discriminative power. They also evaluate different model training paradigms: (1) off-the-shelf frontier agents (Claude Sonnet 4.5, GPT-5, Gemini 2.5 Pro); (2) Centaur, a model broadly fine-tuned on 10.7M human decisions; and (3) Qwen2.5-1.5B-Instruct variants fine-tuned via action-level supervised fine-tuning (A-SFT) or process-level fine-tuning (P-SFT) that directly optimizes process features. They then assess cross-task generalization of process-level supervision.
AI.result: Across all tasks, classifiers using process-level features achieve substantially higher discriminative performance (mean AUC = 0.88) than those using performance metrics alone, and they can still reliably tell humans from agents even when outputs are matched. Broad fine-tuning on large-scale human decisions (Centaur) makes agent behavior more human-like at the process level compared to standard frontier agents. Task-specific process-level fine-tuning (P-SFT) further improves mimicry on the supervised tasks, outperforming action-level SFT. However, these process-level gains drop when evaluated on unseen tasks where the supervised process representations do not transfer well.
AI.conclusion: Human–machine discrimination is more reliable when based on how behavior is produced (process-level features) rather than just what outputs are produced. The CogCAPTCHA30 battery shows that process metrics provide strong signal to distinguish humans from AI agents even under output matching. While broad human data fine-tuning and targeted process-level supervision can make AI more human-like in their cognitive processes, such improvements are highly task-specific and do not automatically generalize. This indicates that specifying suitable process representations for each task is a key bottleneck for developing agents with genuinely human-like cognitive processes and for building robust, process-based CAPTCHAs or Turing-style tests.

title: SpatialEpiBench: Benchmarking Spatial Information and Epidemic Priors in Forecasting
summary: Accurate epidemic forecasting is crucial for public health response, resource allocation, and outbreak intervention, but remains difficult with sparse, noisy, and highly non-stationary data. Because epidemics unfold across interacting regions, spatiotemporal methods are natural candidates for improving forecasts. Despite growing interest in spatial information, no standardized benchmark exists, and current evaluations often use simple chronological train-test splits that do not reflect real-time forecasting practice. We address this gap with SpatialEpiBench, a challenging benchmark for spatiotemporal epidemic forecasting in realistic public-health settings. SpatialEpiBench includes 11 epidemic datasets with standardized rolling evaluations and outbreak-specific metrics. We evaluate adjacency-informed forecasting models with widely used epidemic priors that adapt general models to epidemiology, but find that most methods underperform a simple last-value baseline from 1 day to 1 month ahead, even during outbreaks and with these priors. We identify three major failure modes: (1) poor outbreak anticipation, (2) difficulty handling sparsity and noise, and (3) limited utility of common geographic adjacency for epidemiological spatial information. We release benchmark data, code, and instructions at https://github.com/Rachel-Lyu/SpatialEpiBench to support development of operationally useful epidemic forecasting models.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SpatialEpiBench, a standardized and realistic benchmark for spatiotemporal epidemic forecasting, showing that many sophisticated models underperform simple baselines and revealing key failure modes in current approaches.
AI.motivation: Epidemic forecasting is essential for effective public health decision-making, yet remains very challenging due to sparse, noisy, and non-stationary data, especially when incorporating spatial interactions between regions. Existing evaluations lack standardized benchmarks and often use unrealistic chronological splits that fail to represent real-time forecasting settings, making it hard to compare methods and to know what actually works in operational contexts. The authors aim to fill this gap by creating a rigorous, realistic benchmark focused on spatial epidemic forecasting.
AI.method: The authors construct SpatialEpiBench, a benchmark consisting of 11 epidemic datasets that capture disease spread across regions. They design standardized rolling evaluation protocols that mimic real-time forecasting and define outbreak-specific performance metrics. They then implement and evaluate a range of adjacency-informed spatiotemporal forecasting models that include commonly used epidemic priors, effectively adapting general spatiotemporal models to the epidemiological domain. These models are compared against a simple last-value (persistence) baseline across multiple forecast horizons, from 1 day to 1 month ahead.
AI.result: Across the benchmark, most adjacency-informed spatiotemporal models, even when equipped with epidemiologically motivated priors, fail to consistently outperform a naive last-observed-value baseline. This underperformance persists across short- and medium-term horizons (1 day to 1 month ahead) and during outbreak periods when accurate forecasting is particularly critical. The evaluation reveals three main failure modes: inability to anticipate upcoming outbreaks, difficulty in coping with sparse and noisy surveillance data, and limited benefit from using standard geographic adjacency as the sole form of spatial structure.
AI.conclusion: Current spatiotemporal epidemic forecasting methods, including those that incorporate common epidemic priors and adjacency information, are often not robust or accurate enough for operational use and can be outperformed by very simple baselines. SpatialEpiBench provides a challenging, standardized, and realistic testbed that highlights these limitations and will help guide the development of more reliable forecasting models. By openly releasing data, code, and instructions, the authors aim to catalyze progress toward methods that better handle outbreak anticipation, data sparsity/noise, and richer forms of spatial epidemiological structure.

title: Ex Ante Evaluation of AI-Induced Idea Diversity Collapse
summary: Creative AI systems are typically evaluated at the level of individual utility, yet creative outputs are consumed in populations: an idea loses value when many others produce similar ones. This creates an evaluation blind spot, as AI can improve individual outputs while increasing population-level crowding. We introduce a human-relative framework for benchmarking AI-induced human diversity collapse without requiring human-AI interaction data, providing an ex ante protocol to estimate crowding risk from model-only generations and matched unaided human baselines. By modeling ideas as congestible resources, we show that source-level crowding is identifiable from within-distribution comparisons, yielding an excess-crowding coefficient $Δ$ and a human-relative diversity ratio $ρ$. We show that $ρ\ge1$ is the no-excess-crowding parity condition and connect $Δ$ to an adoption game with exposure-dependent redundancy costs. Across short stories, marketing slogans, and alternative-uses tasks, three frontier LLMs fall below parity across crowding kernels. Estimates stabilize with feasible model-only sample sizes. Importantly, generation-protocol variants show that crowding can be reduced through targeted design, making diversity collapse an actionable, development-time evaluation target for population-aware creative AI.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a new framework to measure how much AI-generated creative ideas cause "crowding"—too many similar ideas in a population—relative to humans, and finds that current LLMs tend to reduce diversity but that this can be mitigated by better generation protocols.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluations of creative AI focus on the quality or usefulness of individual outputs, ignoring that creative ideas are consumed at a population level where redundancy reduces value. When many people (or models) produce similar ideas, the overall diversity and utility of ideas collapses, a risk that is not captured by standard benchmarks. There is a need for a principled way to quantify this "diversity collapse" or crowding risk induced by AI, ideally without needing expensive human-AI interaction studies.
AI.method: The authors treat ideas as congestible resources: their value decreases as more similar ideas appear. They develop a human-relative benchmarking framework that estimates how much AI models contribute to crowding relative to unaided humans, using only model generations and matched human baselines. By analyzing within-distribution comparisons, they define two key statistics: an excess-crowding coefficient Δ that captures how much more crowding AI induces compared to humans, and a human-relative diversity ratio ρ that measures diversity parity, with ρ ≥ 1 indicating no excess crowding. They connect Δ to an adoption game where agents incur redundancy costs depending on how exposed an idea already is. They empirically evaluate three frontier LLMs across tasks (short-story writing, marketing slogans, and alternative uses) and under different similarity ("crowding kernel") definitions, and test protocol variants for generation to see how crowding responds.
AI.result: For three frontier LLMs, the estimated human-relative diversity ratio ρ is consistently below 1 across various tasks and crowding kernels, indicating that these models induce more idea crowding than comparable human creators ("diversity collapse"). The excess-crowding coefficient Δ is positive, aligning with a model where adoption of AI-generated ideas imposes exposure-dependent redundancy costs. The estimates converge using feasible sample sizes that require only model outputs and human baselines. Variants in the generation protocol demonstrate that modifying how models are prompted or sampled can measurably reduce crowding.
AI.conclusion: Population-level diversity effects of creative AI can and should be measured ex ante, before deployment, with model-only benchmarks. The proposed human-relative framework, via metrics Δ and ρ, reveals that state-of-the-art LLMs currently fall short of human-level diversity and risk inducing crowding in creative domains. However, the sensitivity of these metrics to generation protocols shows that diversity collapse is not an inevitable property of large models but an actionable design and evaluation target. Developers can use such benchmarks to design population-aware creative AI systems that maintain or improve human-level diversity instead of degrading it.

title: Improved techniques for fine-tuning flow models via adjoint matching: a deterministic control pipeline
summary: We propose a deterministic adjoint matching framework that formulates human preference alignment for flow-based generative models as an optimal control problem over velocity fields. One can directly regress the control toward a value-gradient-induced target under the current policy, leading to a simple and stable training objective. Building on this perspective, we introduce a truncated adjoint scheme that focuses computation on the terminal portion of the trajectory, where reward-relevant signals concentrate, which yields substantial computational savings while preserving alignment quality. We further generalize the framework beyond standard KL-based regularization, allowing more flexible trade-offs between alignment strength and distributional preservation. Experiments on SiT-XL/2 and FLUX.2-Klein-4B demonstrate consistent gains across multiple alignment metrics, along with substantially improved diversity and mode preservation.

AI.tldr: They present a new way to align flow-based generative models with human preferences by treating alignment as an optimal control problem over velocity fields, giving a simple training rule, a truncated adjoint method for efficiency, and more flexible regularization, with strong empirical results.
AI.motivation: Existing preference alignment methods are largely developed for autoregressive models and often rely on stochastic gradient estimators, KL-regularization, and full-trajectory computation, which can be unstable or computationally heavy, especially for flow-based generative models. There is a need for a principled, efficient, and stable way to align continuous-time/flow-based generators to human preferences while preserving sample diversity and modes.
AI.method: They recast preference alignment for flow-based generative models as a deterministic optimal control problem on the model’s velocity field. The control (velocity adjustment) is regressed directly toward a target defined by the value function’s gradient under the current policy, yielding a simple regression-style objective instead of high-variance estimators. They then introduce a truncated adjoint scheme that only backpropagates through the terminal segment of the generative trajectory where rewards are concentrated, reducing computation. Finally, they extend the framework beyond the usual KL regularization, enabling alternative divergences or penalties to modulate the trade-off between matching human preferences and staying close to the pre-trained distribution.
AI.result: On SiT-XL/2 and FLUX.2-Klein-4B flow-based models, their method improves multiple alignment metrics compared to baselines while also enhancing diversity and mode coverage in the generated samples.
