The truncated adjoint variant achieves similar alignment quality with much lower computational cost.
AI.conclusion: Treating preference alignment for flow-based generative models as a deterministic optimal control problem over velocity fields leads to a stable, efficient, and flexible training framework. The truncated adjoint scheme cuts computation while keeping alignment quality, and generalized regularization enables better control over the trade-off between alignment strength and distributional preservation, yielding empirically better-aligned and more diverse models.

title: NeuroAgent: LLM Agents for Multimodal Neuroimaging Analysis and Research
summary: Multimodal neuroimaging analysis often involves complex, modality-specific preprocessing workflows that require careful configuration, quality control, and coordination across heterogeneous toolchains. Beyond preprocessing, downstream statistical analysis and disease classification commonly require task-specific code, evaluation protocols, and data-format conventions, creating additional barriers between raw acquisitions and reproducible scientific analysis. We present NeuroAgent, an LLM-driven agentic framework that automates key preprocessing and analysis steps for heterogeneous neuroimaging data, including sMRI, fMRI, dMRI, and PET, and supports interactive downstream analysis through natural-language queries. NeuroAgent employs a hierarchical multi-agent architecture with a feedback-driven Generate-Execute-Validate engine: agents autonomously generate executable preprocessing code, detect and recover from runtime errors, and validate output integrity. We evaluate the system on 1,470 subjects pooled across all ADNI phases (CN=1,000, AD=470), where all subjects have sMRI and tabular data, with subsets also having Tau-PET (n=469), fMRI (n=278), and DTI ($n=620$). Pipeline ablation studies across multiple LLM backends show that capable models reach up to 100% intent-parsing accuracy, with the strongest backend (Qwen3.5-27B) reaching 84.8% end-to-end preprocessing step correctness. Automated recovery limits manual intervention to edge cases where human review is required via the Human-In-The-Loop interface. For Alzheimer's Disease classification using automatically preprocessed multimodal data, our agent ensemble achieves an AUC of 0.9518 with four modalities, outperforming all single-modality baselines. These results show that NeuroAgent can reduce the manual effort required for neuroimaging preprocessing and enable end-to-end automated analysis pipelines for neuroimaging research.

AI.tldr: NeuroAgent is an LLM-driven multi-agent framework that automatically handles multimodal neuroimaging preprocessing and downstream analysis, significantly reducing manual effort and achieving strong performance on Alzheimer’s disease classification.
AI.motivation: Multimodal neuroimaging studies require complex, modality-specific preprocessing and bespoke downstream analysis code. This complexity, plus heterogeneous tools and data formats, creates barriers to reproducibility, scalability, and accessibility, especially for large datasets and non-expert users. The authors aim to automate and standardize these steps using LLM-based agents, to bridge the gap between raw acquisitions and robust, reproducible scientific analysis.
AI.method: The authors design NeuroAgent, a hierarchical multi-agent system powered by large language models. It uses a feedback-driven Generate–Execute–Validate loop where agents: (1) interpret user intent and data properties, (2) autonomously generate executable preprocessing code for multiple modalities (sMRI, fMRI, dMRI, PET), (3) detect and recover from runtime errors, and (4) validate the integrity of outputs. The framework supports interactive, natural-language-driven downstream analysis. They evaluate the framework on a large ADNI cohort with varying modality availability, conduct pipeline ablation across different LLM backends to measure parsing and step correctness, and then use the preprocessed data in an Alzheimer’s disease classification task.
AI.result: Across LLM backends, intent parsing accuracy reaches up to 100%, and the strongest backend (Qwen3.5-27B) achieves 84.8% correctness on end-to-end preprocessing steps. Error recovery mechanisms minimize the need for human intervention, restricting manual review to difficult edge cases using a human-in-the-loop interface. Using automatically preprocessed multimodal data from ADNI, the NeuroAgent-based pipeline attains an AUC of 0.9518 for Alzheimer’s disease classification with four modalities, outperforming all single-modality baselines.
AI.conclusion: NeuroAgent demonstrates that LLM-driven, multi-agent systems can reliably automate much of the complex preprocessing and analysis of heterogeneous neuroimaging data. The approach reduces manual workload, improves reproducibility, and enables end-to-end, natural-language-driven analysis pipelines, achieving strong performance on a clinically relevant Alzheimer’s disease classification task. This suggests agentic LLM frameworks can serve as practical infrastructure for scalable, multimodal neuroimaging research.

title: Can RL Teach Long-Horizon Reasoning to LLMs? Expressiveness Is Key
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) has been applied to improve large language model (LLM) reasoning, yet the systematic study of how training scales with task difficulty has been hampered by the lack of controlled, scalable environments. We introduce ScaleLogic, a synthetic logical reasoning framework that offers independent control over two axes of difficulty: the depth of the required proof planning (i.e., the horizon) and the expressiveness of the underlying logic. Our proposed framework supports a wide range of logics: from simple implication-only logic ("if-then") towards more expressive first-order reasoning with conjunction ("and"), disjunction ("or"), negation ("not"), and universal quantification ("for all"). Using this framework, we show that the RL training compute $T$ follows a power law with respect to reasoning depth $D$ ($T \propto D^γ$, $R^{2} > 0.99$), and that the scaling exponent $γ$ increases monotonically with logical expressiveness, from $1.04$ to $2.60$. On downstream mathematics and general reasoning benchmarks, more expressive training settings yield both larger performance gains (up to $+10.66$ points) and more compute-efficient transfer compared to less expressive settings, demonstrating that what a model is trained on, not just how much it is trained, shapes downstream transfer. We further show that the power-law relationship holds across multiple RL methods, and curriculum-based training substantially improves scaling efficiency.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ScaleLogic, a synthetic framework to systematically study how RL training for LLM reasoning scales with task difficulty, showing power-law relationships between compute and reasoning depth that depend on logical expressiveness.
AI.motivation: Existing work uses RL to improve LLM reasoning, but there is no controlled, scalable environment to rigorously analyze how training difficulty (e.g., reasoning depth or logical complexity) affects learning dynamics and transfer. This gap makes it hard to understand or predict how much compute is needed as tasks become more complex, and how the nature of the reasoning tasks influences downstream performance.
AI.method: The authors design ScaleLogic, a synthetic logical reasoning benchmark where they can independently vary (1) the depth of proof planning (horizon length) and (2) the expressiveness of the logic used. They instantiate families of tasks ranging from simple implication-only logic to more expressive first-order logics with conjunction, disjunction, negation, and universal quantification. They then train LLM-based agents with various RL methods across these controlled settings, measure the training compute required to reach performance targets, and fit scaling laws relating compute to reasoning depth for different levels of logical expressiveness. They also evaluate transfer to downstream math and general reasoning benchmarks and test curriculum-training strategies.
AI.result: Across the constructed environments, they find that the RL training compute T needed to solve tasks of reasoning depth D obeys a power law, T ∝ D^γ, with very high fit quality (R² > 0.99). The scaling exponent γ grows monotonically as the underlying logic becomes more expressive, ranging from 1.04 for simple implication-only logic up to 2.60 for fully expressive first-order logic. Models trained in more expressive settings yield larger downstream performance gains (up to +10.66 points) and better compute efficiency when transferred to math and general reasoning benchmarks versus models trained only on simpler logics. The observed power-law behavior is consistent across multiple RL algorithms, and curricula that gradually increase difficulty significantly improve scaling efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Logical reasoning difficulty for RL-trained LLMs exhibits robust power-law scaling with reasoning depth, and the steepness of this scaling is strongly influenced by the expressiveness of the training logic. Training on richer, more expressive logical environments not only leads to better downstream reasoning and math performance but also yields more compute-efficient transfer than training on simpler tasks. Curriculum design further improves scaling efficiency. Overall, the work argues that the structure and expressiveness of training tasks are as crucial as raw training quantity for shaping reasoning capabilities and compute scaling of RL-trained LLMs.

title: GlazyBench: A Benchmark for Ceramic Glaze Property Prediction and Image Generation
summary: Developing ceramic glazes is a costly, time-consuming process of trial and error due to complex chemistry, placing a significant burden on independent artists. While recent advances in multimodal AI offer a modern solution, the field lacks the large-scale datasets required to train these models. We propose GlazyBench, the first dataset for AI-assisted glaze design. Comprising 23,148 real glaze formulations, GlazyBench supports two primary tasks: predicting post-firing surface properties, such as color and transparency, from raw materials, and generating accurate visual representations of the glaze based on these properties. We establish comprehensive baselines for property prediction using traditional machine learning and large language models, alongside image generation benchmarks using deep generative and large multimodal models. Our experiments demonstrate promising yet challenging results. GlazyBench pioneers a new research direction in AI-assisted material design, providing a standardized benchmark for systematic evaluation.

AI.tldr: Introduction of GlazyBench, the first large-scale dataset and benchmark for AI-assisted ceramic glaze design, enabling prediction of glaze properties and image generation from formulations.
AI.motivation: Ceramic glaze development is expensive and slow because it relies on trial-and-error experimentation and complex chemistry, which is especially burdensome for independent artists. Despite advances in multimodal AI that could help, there is no large-scale, standardized dataset for training and benchmarking such models in the context of glaze design.
AI.method: The authors construct GlazyBench, a dataset of 23,148 real glaze formulations annotated with post-firing surface properties (e.g., color, transparency). They define two main tasks: (1) predicting surface properties from raw glaze formulations and (2) generating realistic glaze images from those properties. They implement baseline models, including traditional machine learning and large language models for property prediction, and deep generative plus large multimodal models for image generation, and evaluate performance across these tasks.
AI.result: The baseline experiments on GlazyBench yield results that show AI models can capture some aspects of glaze property prediction and image generation, but the tasks remain non-trivial and performance is far from perfect. This indicates both the feasibility of AI-assisted glaze design and the remaining difficulty of the problems.
AI.conclusion: GlazyBench establishes the first standardized benchmark and dataset for AI-assisted ceramic glaze design, opening a new research direction in AI-driven materials and art. It enables systematic comparison of models for property prediction and glaze visualization, and the initial results highlight substantial room for improvement, encouraging further work in this domain.

title: AI Co-Mathematician: Accelerating Mathematicians with Agentic AI
summary: We introduce the AI co-mathematician, a workbench for mathematicians to interactively leverage AI agents to pursue open-ended research. The AI co-mathematician is optimized to provide holistic support for the exploratory and iterative reality of mathematical workflows, including ideation, literature search, computational exploration, theorem proving and theory building. By providing an asynchronous, stateful workspace that manages uncertainty, refines user intent, tracks failed hypotheses, and outputs native mathematical artifacts, the system mirrors human collaborative workflows. In early tests, the AI co-mathematician helped researchers solve open problems, identify new research directions, and uncover overlooked literature references. Besides demonstrating a highly interactive paradigm for AI-assisted mathematical discovery, the AI co-mathematician also achieves state of the art results on hard problem-solving benchmarks, including scoring 48% on FrontierMath Tier 4, a new high score among all AI systems evaluated.

AI.tldr: The paper presents the 'AI co-mathematician,' an interactive workbench that uses AI agents to support end-to-end mathematical research workflows, and shows it both aids real research and achieves state-of-the-art results on challenging benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Mathematical research is exploratory, iterative, and involves many interconnected tasks such as ideation, literature review, computation, theorem proving, and theory building. Existing AI tools tend to focus on narrow subtasks (e.g., solving a problem or proving a theorem in isolation) and lack support for the holistic, collaborative workflow that human mathematicians use, especially in open-ended research. The authors aim to fill this gap by creating an AI system that can serve as an integrated research partner rather than a point solution.
AI.method: The authors design an 'AI co-mathematician' as an asynchronous, stateful workspace composed of AI agents that interact with users over extended sessions. The system is optimized to: refine user intent; manage and represent uncertainty; record and learn from failed hypotheses; perform literature search; carry out computational experiments; assist with theorem proving; and generate mathematical artifacts (e.g., definitions, conjectures, proofs). The interaction paradigm mirrors human collaboration by maintaining context over time and by producing outputs in native mathematical formats. They evaluate the system both through early deployments with researchers and via quantitative benchmarks such as FrontierMath Tier 4.
AI.result: In early-user studies, the AI co-mathematician successfully assisted researchers in solving open problems, discovering new research directions, and finding previously missed literature. Quantitatively, it achieves state-of-the-art performance on hard mathematical problem benchmarks, notably achieving 48% accuracy on FrontierMath Tier 4, which is reported as the highest score among evaluated AI systems at the time of writing.
AI.conclusion: The AI co-mathematician demonstrates that an interactive, stateful, multi-capability AI workbench can meaningfully contribute to real-world mathematical research, not just isolated problem solving. Its strong benchmark performance and positive early research collaborations suggest that such systems are a promising paradigm for AI-assisted scientific discovery more broadly. Future work likely involves scaling the approach, integrating more tools, and conducting larger, systematic evaluations of its impact on mathematical practice.
